articles.title: Hierarchically Robust Zero-shot Vision-language Models
articles.abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can perform zero-shot classification but are susceptible to adversarial attacks. While robust fine-tuning improves their robustness, existing approaches align fixed text embeddings with an image embedding, sacrificing natural performance and robustness. A robustness degradation also occurs when a model faces adversarial attacks targeting superclasses (parent classes, e.g., mammal) in addition to their base (leaf) classes (e.g., cat). Thus, to enhance adversarial rob

articles.title: Stochasticity in Tokenisation Improves Robustness
articles.abstract: The widespread adoption of large language models (LLMs) has increased concerns about their robustness. Vulnerabilities in perturbations of tokenisation of the input indicate that models trained with a deterministic canonical tokenisation can be brittle to adversarial attacks. Recent studies suggest that stochastic tokenisation can deliver internal representations that are less sensitive to perturbations. In this paper, we analyse how stochastic tokenisations affect robustness to adversarial atta

articles.title: How Adversarial Environments Mislead Agentic AI?
articles.abstract: Tool-integrated agents are deployed on the premise that external tools ground their outputs in reality. Yet this very reliance creates a critical attack surface. Current evaluations benchmark capability in benign settings, asking "can the agent use tools correctly" but never "what if the tools lie". We identify this Trust Gap: agents are evaluated for performance, not for skepticism. We formalize this vulnerability as Adversarial Environmental Injection (AEI), a threat model where adversaries co

articles.title: NERO-Net: A Neuroevolutionary Approach for the Design of Adversarially Robust CNNs
articles.abstract: Neuroevolution automates the complex task of neural network design but often ignores the inherent adversarial fragility of evolved models which is a barrier to adoption in safety-critical scenarios. While robust training methods have received significant attention, the design of architectures exhibiting intrinsic robustness remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose NERO-Net, a neuroevolutionary approach to design convolutional neural networks better equipped to resist adversarial att

articles.title: Adversarial Humanities Benchmark: Results on Stylistic Robustness in Frontier Model Safety
articles.abstract: The Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB) evaluates whether model safety refusals survive a shift away from familiar harmful prompt forms. Starting from harmful tasks drawn from MLCommons AILuminate, the benchmark rewrites the same objectives through humanities-style transformations while preserving intent. This extends literature on Adversarial Poetry and Adversarial Tales from single jailbreak operators to a broader benchmark family of stylistic obfuscation and goal concealment. In the benchm

facts.gap: What is missing or limited in existing work, what this paper improves

Recent studies suggest that stochastic tokenisation can deliver internal representations that are less sensitive to perturbations. In this paper, we analyse how stochastic tokenisations affect robustness to adversarial atta

facts.problem: The robustness of large language models (LLMs) to adversarial attacks and random perturbations, specifically the vulnerability of models trained with deterministic canonical tokenisation.
facts.gap: Existing models trained with deterministic canonical tokenisation are brittle to adversarial attacks and random perturbations, while stochastic tokenisation is suggested to deliver internal representations that are less sensitive to such perturbations.

facts.gap: Existing evaluations benchmark agent capability in benign settings without testing the agents' ability to detect or resist adversarial deception in tool outputs.

facts.problem: Neural network design often ignores the inherent adversarial fragility of evolved models, creating a barrier to adoption in safety-critical scenarios.
facts.gap: Existing robust training methods have not addressed the design of architectures that exhibit intrinsic robustness.

Starting from harmful tasks drawn from MLCommons AILuminate, the benchmark rewrites the same objectives through humanities-style transformations while preserving intent. This extends literature on Adversarial Poetry and Adversarial Tales from single jailbreak operators to a broader benchmark family of stylistic obfuscation and goal concealment. In the benchm

facts.problem: Evaluating whether model safety refusals survive when prompts are shifted from familiar harmful forms to humanities-style obfuscation, and identifying the lack of stylistic robustness in current safety techniques.
facts.gap: Existing literature focuses on single jailbreak operators, while current safety techniques suffer from weak generalization due to a lack of stylistic robustness against goal concealment.
methods: Rewriting harmful tasks from MLCommons AILuminate

topics.title: Adversarial Robustness of Hierarchically Robust Zero-shot VLMs in Multi-Tree Alignment
topics.rationale: While recent work like [1] introduces hierarchical embeddings to address adversarial attacks on VLMs, it often sacrifices natural performance by aligning fixed text embeddings. There is a gap in how these hierarchical structures perform under stochastic tokenization, which [2] suggests is crucial for robustness. Furthermore, existing evaluations often lack rigorous testing on adversarial environments that specifically target superclass classes alongside base classes. This research proposes to evaluate the stability of hierarchical alignment when combined with stochastic tokenization.
topics.approach: We will conduct a systematic comparative study using CIFAR-10 and LLaMA-1b datasets. The proposed approach involves training zero-shot VLMs using novel adversarial fine-tuning over multiple hierarchical trees. We will then apply stochastic tokenization to the inputs and measure the resulting accuracy degradation compared to canonical tokenization. Finally, we will analyze the robustness margin size against the theoretical connection between embedding depth and vulnerability.

topics.title: Stochastic Tokenization as a Defense Mechanism Against Adversarial Attacks in LLMs
topics.rationale: The widespread adoption of large language models (LLMs) has increased concerns about their robustness, specifically regarding vulnerabilities in deterministic canonical tokenization. Papers like [2] and [3] highlight that stochastic tokenization leads to internal representations less sensitive to adversarial perturbations, yet this is rarely integrated into standard training pipelines. There is a lack of empirical evidence on how stochastic tokenization affects the robustness of agent AI systems in specific adversarial environments.
topics.approach: We will analyze the impact of stochastic tokenization on LLM robustness by pre-training models with uniformly sampled stochastic tokenizations. The research will evaluate the performance of these models on TruthfulQA, AdvBench, and CIFAR-10 datasets to measure robustness to adversarial attacks and random perturbations. We will compare these results against models trained with deterministic canonical tokenization to quantify the improvement.

topics.title: Epistemic Drift and Policy Collapse in Tool-Integrated Agentic AI under Adversarial Injection
topics.rationale: Current evaluations of agentic AI often benchmark capability in benign settings without testing skepticism when external tools may lie. Papers like [3] identify the reliance on external tools as a critical attack surface, yet there is a gap in understanding how adversarial environments induce epistemic drift and policy collapse. Existing methods lack a framework to specifically measure the degradation of agent behavior under adversarial environmental injection.
topics.approach: We will deploy frontier agents using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and evaluate their robustness against adversarial environmental injection. The study will employ specific attack vectors such as Maze attacks and Epistemic drift to simulate deceptive tool outputs. We will analyze the resulting performance metrics, including policy collapse and infinite loops, to quantify the risk of agent failure in adversarial settings.

topics.title: Neuroevolutionary Design of Intrinsic Robustness in Adversarially Robust CNNs
topics.rationale: Neuroevolution automates neural network design but often ignores the inherent adversarial fragility of evolved models, creating a barrier to safety-critical adoption. While robust training methods have received attention, existing approaches do not address the design of architectures that exhibit intrinsic robustness against attacks like AutoAttack. There is a lack of systematic exploration of how fitness functions can be adapted to enforce robustness during the evolutionary search process.
topics.approach: We will implement a neuroevolutionary framework to automatically design CNNs with explicit adversarial robustness constraints. The fitness function will be optimized to maximize L_infinity-robustness against FGSM and AutoAttack attacks while minimizing training time. We will evaluate the resulting architectures on CIFAR-10 to determine if neuroevolution can produce models with higher robustness margins compared to standard training methods.

topics.title: Stylistic Robustness of Model Safety Refusals in Frontier Models
topics.rationale: Evaluating whether model safety refusals survive a shift from familiar harmful prompt forms to humanities-style obfuscation is critical for frontier model safety. Current literature focuses on single jailbreak operators, yet existing safety techniques suffer from weak generalization due to a lack of stylistic robustness. There is a gap in understanding how stylistic obfuscation affects goal concealment and the overall success rate of safety refusals in diverse model architectures.
topics.approach: We will evaluate the stylistic robustness of safety refusals using the Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB). The study will rewrite harmful tasks from MLCommons AILuminate through humanities-style transformations while preserving the original objectives. We will analyze the attack success rate (ASR) across different model architectures to identify patterns in stylistic robustness and generalization failures.

articles.title: Balanced Co-Clustering of Users and Items for Embedding Table Compression in Recommender Systems
articles.abstract: Recommender systems have advanced markedly over the past decade by transforming each user/item into a dense embedding vector with deep learning models. At industrial scale, embedding tables constituted by such vectors of all users/items demand a vast amount of parameters and impose heavy compute and memory overhead during training and inference, hindering model deployment under resource constraints. Existing solutions towards embedding compression either suffer from severely compromised recommen

articles.title: Mira-Embeddings-V1: Domain-Adapted Semantic Reranking for Recruitment via LLM-Synthesized Data
articles.abstract: Candidate sourcing for recruiters is best viewed as a two-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline with recall as the primary objective under a limited review budget. An upstream production retriever first returns a candidate shortlist for each job description (JD), and our goal is to rerank that shortlist so that qualified candidates appear as high as possible. We present mira-embeddings-v1, a semantic reranking system for the recruitment domain that reshapes the embedding space with LLM-synthesi

articles.title: Predictive Associative Memory: Retrieval Beyond Similarity Through Temporal Co-occurrence
articles.abstract: Current approaches to memory in neural systems rely on similarity-based retrieval: given a query, find the most representationally similar stored state. This assumption -- that useful memories are similar memories -- fails to capture a fundamental property of biological memory: association through temporal co-occurrence. We propose Predictive Associative Memory (PAM), an architecture in which a JEPA-style predictor, trained on temporal co-occurrence within a continuous experience stream, learns

articles.title: Characterizing AlphaEarth Embedding Geometry for Agentic Environmental Reasoning
articles.abstract: Earth observation foundation models encode land surface information into dense embedding vectors, yet the geometric structure of these representations and its implications for downstream reasoning remain underexplored. We characterize the manifold geometry of Google AlphaEarth's 64-dimensional embeddings across 12.1 million Continental United States samples (2017--2023) and develop an agentic system that leverages this geometric understanding for environmental reasoning. The manifold is non-Eucl

articles.title: Semantic Entanglement in Vector-Based Retrieval: A Formal Framework and Context-Conditioned Disentanglement Pipeline for Agentic RAG Systems
articles.abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems depend on the geometric properties of vector representations to retrieve contextually appropriate evidence. When source documents interleave multiple topics within contiguous text, standard vectorization produces embedding spaces in which semantically distinct content occupies overlapping neighborhoods. We term this condition semantic entanglement. We formalize entanglement as a model-relative measure of cross-topic overlap in embedding space and defi

facts.problem: High parameter count and heavy compute/memory overhead in embedding tables for industrial-scale recommender systems, which hinders model deployment under resource constraints.
facts.gap: Existing embedding compression solutions suffer from either severely compromised recommendation accuracy or considerable computational costs.
methods: Principled weighting scheme for users and items

facts.problem: Candidate sourcing for recruiters is a two-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline with limited review budgets; the challenge is to improve recall by reshaping the embedding space to rank qualified candidates higher.
facts.gap: Existing methods often rely on large-scale manually labeled industrial training pairs, which are expensive and time-consuming; this paper addresses the need for robust gains using LLM-synthesized supervision without heavy cross-encoder models.

facts.problem: Existing memory retrieval methods rely on similarity-based approaches that fail to capture temporal co-occurrence, which is a fundamental property of biological memory.
facts.gap: Current approaches assume useful memories are similar, ignoring the temporal structure of associations.

We term this condition semantic entanglement. We formalize entanglement as a model-relative measure of cross-topic overlap in embedding space and defi

facts.problem: RAG systems rely on vector representations, but standard vectorization fails to handle source documents containing multiple topics interleaved within contiguous text, leading to overlapping neighborhoods in embedding spaces.
facts.gap: Existing methods do not effectively address the semantic entanglement caused by multi-topic interleaving in documents, which constrains Top-K retrieval precision.
methods: Continuous feedback mechanism for adapting document structure

topics.title: Temporal Co-occurrence Modeling for Long-Term Memory Retrieval in Neural Systems
topics.rationale: Current neural memory models predominantly rely on static similarity metrics, which fail to capture the temporal dynamics of biological associations. This topic addresses the gap identified in [3] by proposing a predictive associative memory framework that explicitly incorporates temporal co-occurrence patterns. By leveraging the JEPA-style predictor and Outward JEPA mechanisms, the research aims to improve retrieval precision for tasks requiring long-term retention and context-dependent recall, directly addressing the limitations of existing similarity-based approaches.
topics.approach: The study will implement a Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) architecture within a standard neural network architecture. We will train the model using a synthetic benchmark dataset generated to simulate temporal co-occurrence events, ensuring the synthetic data reflects the specific temporal structure of biological memory. Evaluation will involve comparing the model's association precision and discrimination AUC against baselines that ignore temporal features.

topics.title: Domain-Adapted Semantic Reranking for Recruitment via LLM-Synthesized Data
topics.rationale: Existing candidate sourcing methods often depend on expensive, manually labeled industrial training pairs, creating a significant bottleneck in recruitment pipelines. This topic proposes a novel pipeline that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to synthesize high-quality supervision data, thereby reducing the cost and time required for data annotation. By utilizing LLM-synthesized data for Five-stage prompt pipeline training and JD--JD contrastive alignment, the research aims to significantly improve recall@50 while maintaining robustness across diverse job descriptions.
topics.approach: The research will construct a heterogeneous text dataset comprising real job descriptions and a local pool of 300 real job descriptions. We will employ a Five-stage prompt pipeline to generate LLM-synthesized training pairs and supervise the reranking process. The method involves boundaryHead MLP reranking and semantic reranking systems trained on the synthesized global pool of candidates.

topics.title: Characterizing AlphaEarth Embedding Geometry for Agentic Environmental Reasoning
topics.rationale: Earth observation foundation models encode land surface information into dense embeddings, yet the geometric structure of these representations remains largely unexplored, hindering the development of agentic environmental reasoning systems. This research aims to characterize the manifold geometry of Google AlphaEarth's 64-dimensional embeddings to understand how these vectors support downstream reasoning tasks. By developing an agentic system and utilizing FAISS-indexed embedding databases, we will investigate the implications of this geometry for tasks requiring complex spatial reasoning.
topics.approach: The study will characterize the manifold geometry of the embeddings using a combination of intrinsic dimensionality analysis and cross-model benchmarking. We will develop an agentic system capable of reasoning over the embedded land surface data and perform five-condition ablation studies to isolate specific geometric properties. Metrics will include participation ratio, local intrinsic dimensionality, and mean|cosθ| to quantify the embedding space's suitability for reasoning.

topics.title: Semantic Entanglement in Vector-Based Retrieval: A Formal Framework for Agentic RAG Systems
topics.rationale: Standard vectorization fails to handle documents containing multiple interleaved topics, leading to overlapping neighborhoods in embedding spaces that degrade Top-K retrieval precision. This topic addresses the gap identified in [5] by proposing a formal framework to disentangle semantically entangled representations in RAG contexts. By implementing a Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline and a context-conditioned preprocessing framework, the research aims to improve retrieval accuracy in complex, multi-topic enterprise knowledge bases.
topics.approach: The research will utilize an Enterprise healthcare knowledge base as the primary dataset to evaluate the proposed disentanglement methods. We will implement a four-stage preprocessing framework that includes context-conditioned preprocessing and a continuous feedback mechanism for adapting document structure. The study will measure Top-K retrieval precision and Mean EI (Entanglement Index) to validate the effectiveness of the disentanglement approach.

articles.title: Where to Focus: Query-Modulated Multimodal Keyframe Selection for Long Video Understanding
articles.abstract: Long video understanding remains a formidable challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) due to the prohibitive computational cost of processing dense frame sequences. Prevailing solutions, which select a keyframe subset, typically rely on either a single visual-centric metric (e.g., CLIP similarity) or a static fusion of heuristic scores. This ``one-size-fits-all'' paradigm frequently fails: visual-only metrics are ineffective for plot-driven narrative queries, while indiscriminatel

articles.title: EasyVideoR1: Easier RL for Video Understanding
articles.abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated remarkable effectiveness in improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. As models evolve into natively multimodal architectures, extending RLVR to video understanding becomes increasingly important yet remains largely unexplored, due to the diversity of video task types, the computational overhead of repeatedly decoding and preprocessing high-dimensional visual inputs, and the difficulty of reproducible eva

articles.title: Find, Fix, Reason: Context Repair for Video Reasoning
articles.abstract: Reinforcement learning has advanced video reasoning in large multi-modal models, yet dominant pipelines either rely on on-policy self-exploration, which plateaus at the model's knowledge boundary, or hybrid replay that mixes policies and demands careful regularization. Dynamic context methods zoom into focused evidence but often require curated pretraining and two-stage tuning, and their context remains bounded by a small model's capability. In contrast, larger models excel at instruction follow

articles.title: How Far Are Video Models from True Multimodal Reasoning?
articles.abstract: Despite remarkable progress toward general-purpose video models, a critical question remains unanswered: how far are these models from achieving true multimodal reasoning? Existing benchmarks fail to address this question rigorously, as they remain constrained by straightforward task designs and fragmented evaluation metrics that neglect complex multimodal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we introduce CLVG-Bench, an evaluation framework designed to probe video models' zero-shot reasoning capabilit

articles.abstract: Video Scene Graph Generation aims to obtain structured semantic representations of objects and their relationships in videos for high-level understanding. However, existing methods still have limitations in handling long-tail distributions. This paper proposes the Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) model, which enhances the modeling ability of long-tail relationships from a mechanism perspective. facts.problem: Processing dense frame sequences in long video understanding for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) is computationally prohibitive, and existing solutions often fail due to modal noise or lack of adaptability.
facts.gap: Prevailing solutions use single visual-centric metrics or static fusion of heuristic scores, which are ineffective for plot-driven queries or introduce noise for visual tasks.

As models evolve into natively multimodal architectures, extending RLVR to video understanding becomes increasingly important yet remains largely unexplored, due to the diversity of video task types, the computational overhead of repeatedly decoding and preprocessing high-dimensional visual inputs, and the difficulty of reproducible eva

facts.problem: Extending reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) to video understanding tasks, which is challenging due to the diversity of video task types, computational overhead of high-dimensional visual inputs, and difficulty in reproducible evaluation.
facts.gap: Existing open-source RL training frameworks provide solid infrastructure for text and image scenarios but lack systematic optimizations tailored for video modality.

articles_with_facts.title: Find, Fix, Reason: Context Repair for Video Reasoning
articles_with_facts.abstract: Reinforcement learning has advanced video reasoning in large multi-modal models, yet dominant pipelines either rely on on-policy self-exploration, which plateaus at the model's knowledge boundary, or hybrid replay that mixes policies and demands careful regularization. Dynamic context methods zoom into focused evidence but often require curated pretraining and two-stage tuning, and their context remains bounded by a small model's capability. In contrast, larger models excel at instruction follow

facts.problem: Existing reinforcement learning pipelines for video reasoning in large multi-modal models either suffer from on-policy self-exploration plateauing at knowledge boundaries or hybrid replay requiring careful regularization. Additionally, dynamic context methods often require curated pretraining and two-stage tuning, limiting their scalability and context breadth.
facts.gap: There is a need for a scalable approach that leverages larger models' instruction-following and multi-modal capabilities to provide richer context to smaller models, enabling rapid zooming into target regions via simple tools without the heavy pretraining or multi-stage tuning of existing methods.

Existing benchmarks fail to address this question rigorously, as they remain constrained by straightforward task designs and fragmented evaluation metrics that neglect complex multimodal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we introduce CLVG-Bench, an evaluation framework designed to probe video models' zero-shot reasoning capabilit

facts.problem: The main problem is determining how far general-purpose video models are from achieving true multimodal reasoning, as existing benchmarks are constrained by straightforward task designs and fragmented evaluation metrics.
facts.gap: Existing benchmarks fail to address multimodal reasoning rigorously due to straightforward task designs and fragmented evaluation metrics that neglect complex reasoning.

articles_with_facts.title: Frequency-guided Multi-level Reasoning for Scene Graph Generation in Video
articles_with_facts.abstract: Video Scene Graph Generation aims to obtain structured semantic representations of objects and their relationships in videos for high-level understanding. However, existing methods still have limitations in handling long-tail distributions. This paper proposes the Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) model, which enhances the modeling ability of long-tail relationships from a mechanism perspective. We introduce relation-specific branches to deal gradient conflicts, yieldin

facts.problem: Limitations in handling long-tail distributions in video scene graph generation.
facts.gap: Existing methods struggle with long-tail distributions, leading to imbalanced modeling of relationships.

topics.title: Optimizing Query-Modulated Keyframe Selection for Long Video Understanding with Dynamic Modality Routing
topics.rationale: Recent advancements in LongVideoBench and Video-MME highlight the computational prohibitive nature of processing dense frame sequences for MLLMs. While existing approaches like Q-Gate utilize static fusion or single visual-centric metrics, they fail to adapt to plot-driven queries or introduce noise. This research addresses the gap by leveraging the Dynamic modality routing mechanism to enhance query relevance, ensuring efficient processing of long video sequences without modal noise.
topics.approach: The study proposes a modified Q-Gate framework integrating Dynamic modality routing to select keyframes based on temporal and semantic query alignment. We will evaluate this approach on the LongVideoBench dataset, focusing on reducing computational overhead while maintaining high accuracy in visual grounding tasks.

topics.title: Enhancing Video Reasoning Pipelines via Frozen Teacher-Student Dynamics and Minimal Evidence Patches
topics.rationale: Existing reinforcement learning pipelines for video reasoning suffer from on-policy self-exploration plateaus or hybrid replay requiring heavy regularization. The gap lies in the lack of scalable methods that leverage larger instruction-following models to provide richer context to smaller models. This research aims to bridge this by utilizing a Frozen teacher-student dynamic with minimal evidence patches (e.g., timestamps, regions) to enable rapid context zooming.
topics.approach: We will implement a Frozen teacher-student framework integrated with a Minimal evidence patch scheme to enable scalable context repair. The method will be tested on benchmarks similar to those used in VideoMAE and VideoBench to evaluate improvements in reasoning accuracy and scalability.

topics.title: Evaluating True Multimodal Reasoning Capabilities in General-Purpose Video Models using Adaptive Video Evaluator
topics.rationale: Despite significant progress, existing benchmarks like AdvBench and CIFAR-10 fail to address multimodal reasoning rigorously due to straightforward task designs and fragmented evaluation metrics. There is a critical need for a rigorous framework to determine how far general-purpose video models are from achieving true multimodal reasoning. This research proposes using the Adaptive Video Evaluator (AVE) to assess complex reasoning capabilities beyond standard success rates.
topics.approach: The proposed approach utilizes the Adaptive Video Evaluator (AVE) to construct a rigorous evaluation framework that neglects straightforward task designs. We will analyze model outputs to identify fragmentation in reasoning capabilities and propose a minimal annotations strategy for future benchmarks.

topics.title: Frequency-Guided Multi-level Reasoning for Long-Tail Scene Graph Generation in Video
topics.rationale: Existing methods struggle with long-tail distributions in video scene graph generation, leading to imbalanced modeling of object relationships. The Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) model addresses this by incorporating frequency-aware dual-branch predicate embedding networks. However, there is a need to validate these methods on diverse long-tail distributions and improve robustness.
topics.approach: This study will evaluate the Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning model on action recognition tasks to measure recall rates of long-tail relationships. We will analyze the performance of the Bayesian Head and Gaussian Mixture Model Head components in handling diverse video distributions.

topics.title: Cross-Benchmark RLVR Framework for Unified Video Understanding with Modular Extension and Asynchronous Training
topics.rationale: While RLVR has demonstrated effectiveness in text and image scenarios, existing open-source frameworks lack systematic optimizations tailored for video modality. The diversity of video task types, computational overhead, and difficulty in reproducible evaluation present significant challenges. This research proposes a modular extension and asynchronous multi-benchmark evaluation framework to address these gaps.
topics.approach: We will design a cross-benchmark RLVR framework that integrates modular extension and asynchronous multi-benchmark evaluation. The study will compare this unified routing approach against traditional offline preprocessing methods using TruthfulQA and MME datasets.

articles.abstract: The Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB) evaluates whether model safety refusals survive a shift away from familiar harmful prompt forms. Starting from harmful tasks drawn from MLCommons AILuminate, the benchmark rewrites the same objectives through humanities-style transformations while preserving intent. A robustness degradation also occurs when a model faces adversarial attacks targeting superclasses (parent classes, e.g., mammal) in addition to their base (leaf) classes (e.g., cat). Thus, to enhance adversarial rob

facts.problem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are susceptible to adversarial attacks, and existing robust fine-tuning approaches sacrifice natural performance and robustness by aligning fixed text embeddings with image embeddings. Additionally, robustness degrades when attacks target superclasses (parent classes) beyond their base classes.

facts.problem: RLHF introduces a vulnerability where an imperfect Reward Model (RM) can become a single point of failure, and existing red-teaming approaches only target policy-level weaknesses, overlooking systemic weaknesses where both the core LLM and the RM fail in tandem.
facts.gap: Existing red-teaming approaches primarily target policy-level weaknesses and overlook systemic weaknesses cases where both the core LLM and the RM fail in tandem.

topics.title: Adversarial Robustness of Stochastic Tokenisation in Vision-Language Models
topics.rationale: Recent studies indicate that deterministic canonical tokenisation leads to brittle performance under adversarial attacks, whereas stochastic tokenisation improves robustness by introducing internal representations less sensitive to perturbations. However, the specific mechanisms by which stochasticity in VLMs (like LLaMA-1b) mitigates attack vectors against superclasses and parent classes remain underexplored in zero-shot classification settings. This gap suggests that integrating stochastic tokenisation into existing robust fine-tuning frameworks could significantly enhance safety without sacrificing natural performance.
topics.approach: We propose a systematic analysis of stochastic tokenisation strategies within a hierarchical robust fine-tuning framework. The method involves pre-training VLMs with uniformly sampled stochastic tokenisations and comparing them against deterministic baselines. We will evaluate the impact of these tokenisations on robustness to adversarial attacks targeting superclasses and random perturbations, using a theoretical connection between embedding depth and maximum viable margin size.

topics.title: Adversarial Environmental Injection in Tool-Agentic AI Systems
topics.rationale: Tool-integrated AI agents face critical vulnerabilities when evaluated in benign settings, as their reliance on external tools creates an attack surface where agents are assessed for capability rather than skepticism. Existing benchmarks fail to test agents' ability to detect or resist adversarial deception when tools are compromised. This research aims to bridge the gap between benign capability evaluation and robustness testing under adversarial environmental injection.
topics.approach: We will employ Adversarial Environmental Injection (AEI) and Breadth/Depth attacks to simulate compromised tool environments. The approach involves running five frontier agents through a protocol that injects adversarial inputs into their tool usage, measuring metrics such as epistemic drift, policy collapse, and infinite loops. We will compare these results against standard benchmark metrics to quantify the systemic weaknesses in current agent evaluations.

topics.title: Adaptive Robustness of Large Language Models to Hallucination Representations
topics.rationale: Transformer-based LLMs are vulnerable to optimization-based jailbreak attacks that exploit internal gradient structures, yet existing methods lack fine-grained, mechanistic identification of hallucination signals at the hidden-state dimension level. This research addresses the gap in providing adaptive defenses that minimize visibility to defenders while maintaining robustness against such attacks.
topics.approach: We develop a mechanistic framework using logistic regression probes and forward hooks to identify specific hidden-state dimensions responsible for hallucination. The approach involves dynamic iterative extension and confidence-weighted cancellation to construct adaptive defenses. We will evaluate the effectiveness of these methods on benchmarks like MMLU and OPT-125M to measure probe AUC and MMLU degradation.

topics.title: Feature-Level Robustness in Rotationally Equivariant Quantum Machine Learning
topics.rationale: Group-equivariant quantum models are designed to exploit symmetry for improved trainability, but it remains unclear how these symmetry constraints shape their adversarial robustness. Current literature lacks a comprehensive understanding of how these constraints affect the model's vulnerability to targeted input transformations and symmetry sector suppression.
topics.approach: We will conduct feature-level analysis and targeted input transformations to study the impact of symmetry constraints on adversarial robustness. By utilizing group-twirled input analysis and rotation-invariant image statistics, we will identify suppression mechanisms for symmetry sectors. The study will compare classification accuracy across standard and rotationally equivariant architectures under adversarial attacks.

topics.title: Adversarial Transferability of Sparse Autoencoders for LLM Interpretability
topics.rationale: While Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for interpretability, their robustness implications for LLMs remain underexplored. Existing LLMs are vulnerable to optimization-based jailbreak attacks that exploit internal gradient structure, and the potential for SAEs to be compromised by similar attacks is not well understood.
topics.approach: We will integrate pretrained SAEs into transformer residual streams at inference time without modifying weights or blocking gradients. This approach will be tested against GCG and BEAST attacks to evaluate cross-model attack transferability. We will compare jailbreak success rates and robustness metrics across different LLM architectures like Gemma and Qwen.

articles.title: Balanced Co-Clustering of Users and Items for Embedding Table Compression in Recommender Systems
articles.abstract: Recommender systems have advanced markedly over the past decade by transforming each user/item into a dense embedding vector with deep learning models. At industrial scale, embedding tables constituted by such vectors of all users/items demand a vast amount of parameters and impose heavy compute and memory overhead during training and inference, hindering model deployment under resource constraints. Existing solutions towards embedding compression either suffer from severely compromised recommen

articles.title: Modular Representation Compression: Adapting LLMs for Efficient and Effective Recommendations
articles.abstract: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have advanced recommendation systems (RSs), and recent works have begun to explore how to integrate LLMs into industrial RSs. While most approaches deploy LLMs offline to generate and pre-cache augmented representations for RSs, high-dimensional representations from LLMs introduce substantial storage and computational costs. Thus, it is crucial to compress LLM representations effectively. However, we identify a counterintuitive phenomenon during representat

articles.title: Towards Foundation Models for Consensus Rank Aggregation
articles.abstract: Aggregating a consensus ranking from multiple input rankings is a fundamental problem with applications in recommendation systems, search engines, job recruitment, and elections. Despite decades of research in consensus ranking aggregation, minimizing the Kemeny distance remains computationally intractable. Specifically, determining an optimal aggregation of rankings with respect to the Kemeny distance is an NP-hard problem, limiting its practical application to relatively small-scale instances.

articles.title: Mira-Embeddings-V1: Domain-Adapted Semantic Reranking for Recruitment via LLM-Synthesized Data
articles.abstract: Candidate sourcing for recruiters is best viewed as a two-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline with recall as the primary objective under a limited review budget. An upstream production retriever first returns a candidate shortlist for each job description (JD), and our goal is to rerank that shortlist so that qualified candidates appear as high as possible. articles.title: Predictive Associative Memory: Retrieval Beyond Similarity Through Temporal Co-occurrence
articles.abstract: Current approaches to memory in neural systems rely on similarity-based retrieval: given a query, find the most representationally similar stored state. This assumption -- that useful memories are similar memories -- fails to capture a fundamental property of biological memory: association through temporal co-occurrence. articles.abstract: Earth observation foundation models encode land surface information into dense embedding vectors, yet the geometric structure of these representations and its implications for downstream reasoning remain underexplored. articles.abstract: Table annotation is crucial for making web and enterprise tables usable in downstream NLP applications. topics.title: Optimizing Recall@50 in JD-Sourcing via LLM-Synthesized Supervision for Limited Review Budgets
topics.rationale: Recent literature indicates that existing methods for candidate sourcing rely on costly manual industrial training pairs, which are time-consuming and expensive. The paper 'Mira-Embeddings-V1' highlights the need for robust gains using LLM-synthesized supervision to address this bottleneck. Furthermore, 'Predictive Associative Memory' suggests that temporal co-occurrence is a fundamental mechanism for associative memory that similarity-based methods fail to capture, offering a potential alternative for improving recall in resource-constrained environments. Combining these insights allows for a novel approach that leverages LLM-generated data to optimize recall while maintaining efficiency.
topics.approach: This research will employ LLM-synthesized training data to create a lightweight reranking head, utilizing a five-stage prompt pipeline to generate high-quality supervision. We will implement a JD--JD contrastive training strategy and JD--CV triplet alignment to align candidate-job embeddings effectively. To address the computational constraints, we will apply LoRA adaptation and use a BoundaryHead MLP reranking head to ensure efficient inference without the need for full model retraining.

topics.title: Efficient Embedding Compression for Large Language Models by Exploiting Mid-Layer Representation Advantage
topics.rationale: Current compression methods that rely on final layers are suboptimal because Mid-layer Representation Advantage (MRA) causes middle layers to outperform final layers in recommendation tasks. The paper 'Modular Representation Compression' identifies this counterintuitive phenomenon and proposes Modular Adjustment and Modular Task Decoupling as methods to leverage these superior mid-layer representations. This gap represents a critical opportunity to reduce storage and computational costs in industrial recommendation systems without sacrificing accuracy.
topics.approach: We propose a Modular Adjustment technique that selectively prunes or reweights specific mid-layer representations to maximize their contribution to the final output. By decoupling the modular task, we can optimize the compression process independently for different recommendation sub-tasks. This approach will be evaluated using an online A/B test within a large-scale commercial search advertising scenario to measure improvements in eCPM.
datasets: online A/B test within a large-scale commercial search advertising scenario

topics.title: Consensus Rank Aggregation in Recommendation Systems: Scaling Beyond the Kemeny Distance Limitation
topics.rationale: Aggregating consensus ranking is fundamental but minimizing the Kemeny distance is computationally intractable (NP-hard), limiting practical application to small-scale instances. Existing approaches like classical majority-heuristic and Markov-chain methods are limited in scalability and inference speed compared to exact solvers. The paper 'Towards Foundation Models for Consensus Rank Aggregation' outlines the need for scalable algorithms that can handle large-scale instances efficiently.
topics.approach: This study will develop a Transformer-based algorithm specifically designed to minimize Kemeny distance while maintaining scalability. We will integrate Reinforcement learning and Integer linear programming solvers to address the computational intractability. The proposed method will be tested on a benchmark dataset to evaluate its inference speed and accuracy compared to existing solvers.

topics.title: Characterizing AlphaEarth Embedding Geometry for Agentic Environmental Reasoning
topics.rationale: Earth observation foundation models encode land surface information into dense embedding vectors, yet the geometric structure of these representations and its implications for downstream reasoning remain underexplored. The paper 'Characterizing AlphaEarth Embedding Geometry for Agentic Environmental Reasoning' identifies this gap and suggests that understanding the manifold geometry is crucial for developing agentic systems. This research will bridge the gap between raw embedding data and effective reasoning capabilities in environmental applications.
topics.approach: We will characterize the manifold geometry of the embeddings using PCA-derived directions and probe-derived directions to understand the local intrinsic dimensionality. The study will develop an agentic system capable of reasoning on these embeddings using specialized tools and reasoning chains. We will evaluate the model's performance using metrics such as participation ratio and mean|cosθ| to quantify the geometric properties.

topics.title: Training-Free Multimodal Embedder for Vector Graphics and Image Retrieval via Instruction-Guided Semantics
topics.rationale: Existing methods rasterize SVGs, discarding their structured code, while recent sentence embedding methods do not naturally extend to visual or structured modalities. The paper 'mEOL: Training-Free Instruction-Guided Multimodal Embedder for Vector Graphics and Image Retrieval' highlights the lack of training-free, instruction-guided multimodal embedding frameworks that can align text, raster images, and structured SVG code. This gap presents an opportunity to create a unified embedding space for diverse visual and textual modalities.
topics.approach: We will develop a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) combined with a Multimodal Explicit One-word Limitation (mEOL) module to handle structured SVG code. The Semantic SVG rewriting module will be trained on a visual reasoning dataset to align raster images and structured text into an aligned embedding space. We will utilize the Repurposed VGBench dataset to evaluate the system's ability to retrieve vector graphics based on semantic instructions.

articles.abstract: Long video understanding remains a formidable challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) due to the prohibitive computational cost of processing dense frame sequences. Prevailing solutions, which select a keyframe subset, typically rely on either a single visual-centric metric (e.g., CLIP similarity) or a static fusion of heuristic scores. This ``one-size-fits-all'' paradigm frequently fails: visual-only metrics are ineffective for plot-driven narrative queries, while indiscriminatel

articles.abstract: Open-Vocabulary Temporal Action Detection (OV-TAD) aims to localize and classify action segments of unseen categories in untrimmed videos, where effective alignment between action semantics and video representations is critical for accurate detection. articles.abstract: With the rapid growth of video data, Composed Video Retrieval (CVR) has emerged as a novel paradigm in video retrieval and is receiving increasing attention from researchers. Unlike unimodal video retrieval methods, the CVR task takes a multi-modal query consisting of a reference video and a piece of modification text as input. articles.title: How Far Are Video Models from True Multimodal Reasoning?
articles.abstract: Despite remarkable progress toward general-purpose video models, a critical question remains unanswered: how far are these models from achieving true multimodal reasoning? Existing benchmarks fail to address this question rigorously, as they remain constrained by straightforward task designs and fragmented evaluation metrics that neglect complex multimodal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we introduce CLVG-Bench, an evaluation framework designed to probe video models' zero-shot reasoning capabilit

articles.abstract: Video Scene Graph Generation aims to obtain structured semantic representations of objects and their relationships in videos for high-level understanding. However, existing methods still have limitations in handling long-tail distributions. This paper proposes the Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) model, which enhances the modeling ability of long-tail relationships from a mechanism perspective. We introduce relation-specific branches to deal gradient conflicts, yieldin

articles.title: AVRT: Audio-Visual Reasoning Transfer through Single-Modality Teachers
articles.abstract: Recent advances in reasoning models have shown remarkable progress in text-based domains, but transferring those capabilities to multimodal settings, e.g., to allow reasoning over audio-visual data, still remains a challenge, in part because of the limited availability of high-quality reasoning data in targeted multimodal combinations. To address this problem, we introduce AVRT, a novel framework that generates high-quality audio-visual reasoning traces from single-modality teacher models. We ge

articles.title: Spatiotemporal Sycophancy: Negation-Based Gaslighting in Video Large Language Models
articles.abstract: Video Large Language Models (Vid-LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in video understanding tasks, yet their robustness under conversational interaction remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we identify spatiotemporal sycophancy, a failure mode in which Vid-LLMs retract initially correct, visually grounded judgments and conform to misleading user feedback under negation-based gaslighting. Infrared (IR) imaging, a well-established complement to RGB, offers inherent robustness in these conditions, but its integration into MLLMs remains underexplored. facts.problem: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are fragile under common visual degradations like fog, blur, and low-light conditions, while infrared (IR) imaging offers robustness but its integration into MLLMs is underexplored.
facts.gap: Existing MLLMs perform well on RGB imagery but fail under degradations; IR imaging is robust but its fusion into MLLMs via localized cross-attention is not well explored.

topics.title: Cross-Modal Adversarial Robustness for Video Scene Graph Generation under Fog and Low-Light Conditions
topics.rationale: Current research on Video Scene Graph Generation (VSG) focuses heavily on long-tail distributions and frequency-guided reasoning, yet few studies address the fragility of these models under environmental degradations like fog or low-light. The provided papers highlight that while RGB-based MLLMs perform well in standard conditions, their reliance on localized cross-attention makes them vulnerable to visual noise, and infrared integration remains underexplored. This gap necessitates a study on how to maintain semantic coherence in VSG when input modalities are compromised, leveraging the robustness of infrared data as a baseline for fusion strategies.
topics.approach: We propose a dual-stream adversarial training framework where we generate synthetic fog and low-light patches using diffusion models to create a robust evaluation dataset. The approach involves training the VSG model to predict scene graph structures while simultaneously minimizing the discrepancy between predicted and ground truth relations under these degraded conditions. We will evaluate the model's ability to maintain grounding constraints and temporal reasoning under adversarial perturbations, comparing it against baseline RGB-only models.

topics.title: Adaptive Context Repair for Video Reasoning Pipelines with Frozen Teacher Models
topics.rationale: Existing reinforcement learning methods for video reasoning plateau at knowledge boundaries or require complex two-stage tuning. The provided papers indicate that hybrid replay and dynamic context methods often fail to fully leverage larger models' instruction-following capabilities for smaller models. This creates a critical need for a method that can dynamically inject context without disrupting the frozen teacher model's stability. By analyzing the limitations of current self-exploration and curriculum learning strategies, we can design a minimal evidence patch mechanism that enhances context relevance.
topics.approach: We develop a context repair module that identifies critical temporal regions or object states requiring context injection without freezing the teacher. Using a minimal evidence patch strategy, the system will selectively inject timestamps or region embeddings to guide the student model toward higher accuracy. We will evaluate this on benchmarks like AdvBench and TruthfulQA, measuring improvements in reasoning robustness and accuracy compared to standard frozen and on-policy approaches.

topics.title: Frequency-Guided Multi-Stream Reasoning for Robust Long-Tail Scene Graph Generation
topics.rationale: While frequency-guided models show promise in scene graph generation, handling long-tail distributions and complex relational reasoning remains a significant challenge. The provided research emphasizes the need for relation-specific branches and Bayesian heads to improve recall rates, yet existing methods often lack the robustness required for diverse video content. Integrating these advanced reasoning techniques into a unified pipeline could address the limitations of current state-of-the-art approaches in handling unseen categories.
topics.approach: We will implement a frequency-aware dual-branch network where one branch processes object relations based on their frequency in the video and another handles complex predicate embeddings. This architecture will incorporate a Bayesian head and Gaussian Mixture Model to predict uncertain relations, improving recall rates for long-tail distributions. We will apply this to Action Genome and other video datasets to measure improvements in reasoning robustness and recall.

topics.title: Negation-Based Adversarial Evaluation for Grounded Spatiotemporal Beliefs in Video LLMs
topics.rationale: Video Large Language Models (Vid-LLMs) often exhibit robustness issues under adversarial conversational feedback, specifically through spatiotemporal sycophancy. The provided papers highlight the lack of robust mechanisms for maintaining grounded beliefs and the need for curated benchmarks like GasVideo-1000. There is a clear gap in understanding how to enforce prompt-level grounding constraints to prevent hallucinated justifications and belief reversals.
topics.approach: We propose a negation-based gaslighting evaluation framework designed to test Vid-LLMs under adversarial conversational inputs. This approach will involve generating adversarial prompts that attempt to reverse the model's temporal reasoning and grounding constraints. We will use the curated dataset GasVideo-1000 and evaluate performance against TruthfulQA and AdvBench to quantify the model's ability to maintain grounded spatiotemporal beliefs.

articles.title: Hierarchically Robust Zero-shot Vision-language Models
articles.abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can perform zero-shot classification but are susceptible to adversarial attacks. While robust fine-tuning improves their robustness, existing approaches align fixed text embeddings with an image embedding, sacrificing natural performance and robustness. A robustness degradation also occurs when a model faces adversarial attacks targeting superclasses (parent classes, e.g., mammal) in addition to their base (leaf) classes (e.g., cat). Thus, to enhance adversarial rob

articles.title: Stochasticity in Tokenisation Improves Robustness
articles.abstract: The widespread adoption of large language models (LLMs) has increased concerns about their robustness. Vulnerabilities in perturbations of tokenisation of the input indicate that models trained with a deterministic canonical tokenisation can be brittle to adversarial attacks. Recent studies suggest that stochastic tokenisation can deliver internal representations that are less sensitive to perturbations. In this paper, we analyse how stochastic tokenisations affect robustness to adversarial atta

articles.title: Evaluating Answer Leakage Robustness of LLM Tutors against Adversarial Student Attacks
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used in education, yet their default helpfulness often conflicts with pedagogical principles. Prior work evaluates pedagogical quality via answer leakage-the disclosure of complete solutions instead of scaffolding-but typically assumes well-intentioned learners, leaving tutor robustness under student misuse largely unexplored. In this paper, we study scenarios where students behave adversarially and aim to obtain the correct answer from the tutor. We

articles.title: How Adversarial Environments Mislead Agentic AI?
articles.abstract: Tool-integrated agents are deployed on the premise that external tools ground their outputs in reality. Yet this very reliance creates a critical attack surface. Current evaluations benchmark capability in benign settings, asking "can the agent use tools correctly" but never "what if the tools lie". We identify this Trust Gap: agents are evaluated for performance, not for skepticism. We formalize this vulnerability as Adversarial Environmental Injection (AEI), a threat model where adversaries co

articles.title: NERO-Net: A Neuroevolutionary Approach for the Design of Adversarially Robust CNNs
articles.abstract: Neuroevolution automates the complex task of neural network design but often ignores the inherent adversarial fragility of evolved models which is a barrier to adoption in safety-critical scenarios. While robust training methods have received significant attention, the design of architectures exhibiting intrinsic robustness remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose NERO-Net, a neuroevolutionary approach to design convolutional neural networks better equipped to resist adversarial att

articles.title: H-Node Attack and Defense in Large Language Models
articles.abstract: We present H-Node Adversarial Noise Cancellation (H-Node ANC), a mechanistic framework that identifies, exploits, and defends hallucination representations in transformer-based large language models (LLMs) at the level of individual hidden-state dimensions. A logistic regression probe trained on last-token hidden states localizes hallucination signal to a small set of high-variance dimensions -- termed Hallucination Nodes (H-Nodes) -- with probe AUC reaching 0.90 across four architectures. A whi

articles.title: Feature-level analysis and adversarial transfer in rotationally equivariant quantum machine learning
articles.abstract: Group-equivariant quantum models are designed to exploit symmetry and can improve trainability, but it remains unclear how symmetry constraints shape their adversarial robustness. We study this question through a feature-level analysis of equivariant quantum models in a transfer-attack setting. Under equivariance with an invariant readout, predictions depend only on the group-twirled input, which identifies the symmetry-invariant information accessible to the model together with a complementary

articles.title: Adversarial Humanities Benchmark: Results on Stylistic Robustness in Frontier Model Safety
articles.abstract: The Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB) evaluates whether model safety refusals survive a shift away from familiar harmful prompt forms. Starting from harmful tasks drawn from MLCommons AILuminate, the benchmark rewrites the same objectives through humanities-style transformations while preserving intent. This extends literature on Adversarial Poetry and Adversarial Tales from single jailbreak operators to a broader benchmark family of stylistic obfuscation and goal concealment. In the benchm

articles.title: Beyond Explicit Refusals: Soft-Failure Attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation
articles.abstract: Existing jamming attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems typically induce explicit refusals or denial-of-service behaviors, which are conspicuous and easy to detect. In this work, we formalize a subtler availability threat, termed soft failure, which degrades system utility by inducing fluent and coherent yet non-informative responses rather than overt failures. We propose Deceptive Evolutionary Jamming Attack (DEJA), an automated black-box attack framework that generates adversa

articles.title: Towards Understanding the Robustness of Sparse Autoencoders
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to optimization-based jailbreak attacks that exploit internal gradient structure. While Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for interpretability, their robustness implications remain underexplored. We present a study of integrating pretrained SAEs into transformer residual streams at inference time, without modifying model weights or blocking gradients. Across four model families (Gemma, LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen) and two strong white-box attacks

articles.title: Benign Overfitting in Adversarial Training for Vision Transformers
articles.abstract: Despite the remarkable success of Vision Transformers (ViTs) across a wide range of vision tasks, recent studies have revealed that they remain vulnerable to adversarial examples, much like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). A common empirical defense strategy is adversarial training, yet the theoretical underpinnings of its robustness in ViTs remain largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first theoretical analysis of adversarial training under simplified ViT architectures. We show

articles.title: ARES: Adaptive Red-Teaming and End-to-End Repair of Policy-Reward System
articles.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is central to aligning Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it introduces a critical vulnerability: an imperfect Reward Model (RM) can become a single point of failure when it fails to penalize unsafe behaviors. While existing red-teaming approaches primarily target policy-level weaknesses, they overlook what we term systemic weaknesses cases where both the core LLM and the RM fail in tandem. articles.title: The Cognitive Penalty: Ablating System 1 and System 2 Reasoning in Edge-Native SLMs for Decentralized Consensus
articles.abstract: Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) are inclined explore Small Language Models (SLMs) as edge-native constitutional firewalls to vet proposals and mitigate semantic social engineering. While scaling inference-time compute (System 2) enhances formal logic, its efficacy in highly adversarial, cryptoeconomic governance environments remains underexplored. To address this, we introduce Sentinel-Bench, an 840-inference empirical framework executing a strict intra-model ablation on Qwen-3.5-9

articles.title: CCAR: Intrinsic Robustness as an Emergent Geometric Property
articles.abstract: Standard supervised learning optimizes for predictive accuracy but remains agnostic to the internal geometry of learned features, often yielding representations that are entangled and brittle. We propose Class-Conditional Activation Regularization (CCAR) to explicitly engineer the feature space, imposing a block-diagonal structure via a soft inductive bias. By shaping the latent representation to confine class energy to orthogonal subspaces, we create an intrinsic geometric scaffold that natural

articles.title: The Consensus Trap: Rescuing Multi-Agent LLMs from Adversarial Majorities via Token-Level Collaboration
articles.abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) architectures increasingly rely on response-level aggregation, such as Majority Voting (MAJ), to raise reasoning ceilings. However, in open environments, agents are highly susceptible to stealthy contextual corruption, such as targeted prompt injections. We reveal a critical structural vulnerability in current multi-agent systems: response-level aggregation collapses when corrupted agents form a local majority. facts.gap: Existing robust training methods have received attention, but the design of architectures with intrinsic robustness remains largely unexplored.

articles_with_facts.title: H-Node Attack and Defense in Large Language Models
articles_with_facts.abstract: We present H-Node Adversarial Noise Cancellation (H-Node ANC), a mechanistic framework that identifies, exploits, and defends hallucination representations in transformer-based large language models (LLMs) at the level of individual hidden-state dimensions. A logistic regression probe trained on last-token hidden states localizes hallucination signal to a small set of high-variance dimensions -- termed Hallucination Nodes (H-Nodes) -- with probe AUC reaching 0.90 across four architectures. A whi

facts.problem: Identifying, exploiting, and defending hallucination representations in transformer-based large language models (LLMs) at the level of individual hidden-state dimensions.
facts.gap: Existing methods likely lack fine-grained, mechanistic identification of hallucination signals at the hidden-state dimension level, or do not offer adaptive, real-time defense mechanisms with minimal visibility to defenders.

articles_with_facts.title: Feature-level analysis and adversarial transfer in rotationally equivariant quantum machine learning
articles_with_facts.abstract: Group-equivariant quantum models are designed to exploit symmetry and can improve trainability, but it remains unclear how symmetry constraints shape their adversarial robustness. We study this question through a feature-level analysis of equivariant quantum models in a transfer-attack setting. Under equivariance with an invariant readout, predictions depend only on the group-twirled input, which identifies the symmetry-invariant information accessible to the model together with a complementary

facts.problem: How symmetry constraints in group-equivariant quantum models shape their adversarial robustness.
facts.gap: It remains unclear how symmetry constraints affect adversarial robustness in these models.

articles_with_facts.title: Adversarial Humanities Benchmark: Results on Stylistic Robustness in Frontier Model Safety
articles_with_facts.abstract: The Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB) evaluates whether model safety refusals survive a shift away from familiar harmful prompt forms. Starting from harmful tasks drawn from MLCommons AILuminate, the benchmark rewrites the same objectives through humanities-style transformations while preserving intent. This extends literature on Adversarial Poetry and Adversarial Tales from single jailbreak operators to a broader benchmark family of stylistic obfuscation and goal concealment. In the benchm

facts.problem: Whether model safety refusals survive when prompts are shifted away from familiar harmful forms, specifically addressing the lack of stylistic robustness in current safety techniques.
facts.gap: Existing safety techniques suffer from weak generalization, as they fail to maintain safety refusals when prompts are transformed into humanities-style obfuscation, and deep understanding of 'non-maleficence' remains an unresolved problem.
methods: Rewriting harmful objectives through humanities-style transformations

articles_with_facts.title: Beyond Explicit Refusals: Soft-Failure Attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation
articles_with_facts.abstract: Existing jamming attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems typically induce explicit refusals or denial-of-service behaviors, which are conspicuous and easy to detect. In this work, we formalize a subtler availability threat, termed soft failure, which degrades system utility by inducing fluent and coherent yet non-informative responses rather than overt failures. We propose Deceptive Evolutionary Jamming Attack (DEJA), an automated black-box attack framework that generates adversa

facts.problem: Existing jamming attacks on RAG systems induce overt failures or denial-of-service behaviors, which are conspicuous and easy to detect. The paper addresses the subtler 'soft failure' threat, where attacks degrade system utility by inducing fluent, coherent, yet non-informative responses.
facts.gap: Current attacks are conspicuous and easy to detect because they induce overt failures or denial-of-service behaviors. Existing work lacks effective methods to trigger soft failures that degrade utility without being easily detected.

articles_with_facts.title: Towards Understanding the Robustness of Sparse Autoencoders
articles_with_facts.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to optimization-based jailbreak attacks that exploit internal gradient structure. While Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for interpretability, their robustness implications remain underexplored. We present a study of integrating pretrained SAEs into transformer residual streams at inference time, without modifying model weights or blocking gradients. Across four model families (Gemma, LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen) and two strong white-box attacks

facts.problem: LLMs are vulnerable to optimization-based jailbreak attacks that exploit internal gradient structure.
facts.gap: The robustness implications of using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) for interpretability remain underexplored.
methods: integrating pretrained SAEs into transformer residual streams at inference time

articles_with_facts.title: Benign Overfitting in Adversarial Training for Vision Transformers
articles_with_facts.abstract: Despite the remarkable success of Vision Transformers (ViTs) across a wide range of vision tasks, recent studies have revealed that they remain vulnerable to adversarial examples, much like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). A common empirical defense strategy is adversarial training, yet the theoretical underpinnings of its robustness in ViTs remain largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first theoretical analysis of adversarial training under simplified ViT architectures. We show

facts.problem: The vulnerability of Vision Transformers (ViTs) to adversarial examples and the lack of theoretical understanding regarding adversarial training under ViT architectures.
facts.gap: The theoretical underpinnings of adversarial training's robustness in ViTs are largely unexplored, unlike in CNNs where this phenomenon has been observed.

articles_with_facts.title: ARES: Adaptive Red-Teaming and End-to-End Repair of Policy-Reward System
articles_with_facts.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is central to aligning Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it introduces a critical vulnerability: an imperfect Reward Model (RM) can become a single point of failure when it fails to penalize unsafe behaviors. While existing red-teaming approaches primarily target policy-level weaknesses, they overlook what we term systemic weaknesses cases where both the core LLM and the RM fail in tandem.   We present ARES, a framework that systematically discov

facts.problem: RLHF introduces a vulnerability where an imperfect Reward Model (RM) can become a single point of failure, and existing red-teaming approaches only target policy-level weaknesses, overlooking systemic weaknesses where both the core LLM and RM fail in tandem.
facts.gap: Existing red-teaming approaches primarily target policy-level weaknesses and overlook systemic weaknesses cases where both the core LLM and the RM fail in tandem.

articles_with_facts.title: The Cognitive Penalty: Ablating System 1 and System 2 Reasoning in Edge-Native SLMs for Decentralized Consensus
articles_with_facts.abstract: Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) are inclined explore Small Language Models (SLMs) as edge-native constitutional firewalls to vet proposals and mitigate semantic social engineering. While scaling inference-time compute (System 2) enhances formal logic, its efficacy in highly adversarial, cryptoeconomic governance environments remains underexplored. To address this, we introduce Sentinel-Bench, an 840-inference empirical framework executing a strict intra-model ablation on Qwen-3.5-9

facts.problem: The efficacy of scaling inference-time compute (System 2) in highly adversarial, cryptoeconomic governance environments for edge-native Small Language Models (SLMs).
facts.gap: Existing work has not explored the impact of scaling inference-time compute on formal logic in adversarial, cryptoeconomic governance environments.
methods: Toggling latent reasoning across frozen weights

articles_with_facts.title: CCAR: Intrinsic Robustness as an Emergent Geometric Property
articles_with_facts.abstract: Standard supervised learning optimizes for predictive accuracy but remains agnostic to the internal geometry of learned features, often yielding representations that are entangled and brittle. We propose Class-Conditional Activation Regularization (CCAR) to explicitly engineer the feature space, imposing a block-diagonal structure via a soft inductive bias. By shaping the latent representation to confine class energy to orthogonal subspaces, we create an intrinsic geometric scaffold that natural

facts.problem: Standard supervised learning optimizes for predictive accuracy but remains agnostic to the internal geometry of learned features, often yielding representations that are entangled and brittle.
facts.gap: Existing approaches do not explicitly engineer the feature space or impose structural constraints to achieve geometric disentanglement and algorithmic stability.

articles_with_facts.title: The Consensus Trap: Rescuing Multi-Agent LLMs from Adversarial Majorities via Token-Level Collaboration
articles_with_facts.abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) architectures increasingly rely on response-level aggregation, such as Majority Voting (MAJ), to raise reasoning ceilings. However, in open environments, agents are highly susceptible to stealthy contextual corruption, such as targeted prompt injections. We reveal a critical structural vulnerability in current multi-agent systems: response-level aggregation collapses when corrupted agents form a local majority. Because voting aggregates fully-formed conclus

facts.problem: Response-level aggregation in multi-agent large language models collapses when corrupted agents form a local majority due to the vulnerability of blind counting final votes over flawed intermediate logic.
facts.gap: Existing multi-agent systems rely on response-level aggregation (e.g., Majority Voting), which is blind to flawed intermediate logic and fails when adversarial corruptions create a local majority.
methods: Sequential interleaving of generation within a shared auto-regressive context

topics.title: Adversarial Robustness of Vision-Language Models under Hierarchical Superclass Attacks
topics.rationale: Recent research indicates that existing robust fine-tuning approaches often fail to degrade performance when adversarial attacks target superclass (parent) classes, a gap identified in the context of Hierarchically Robust Zero-shot Vision-language Models. This is critical because current methods typically align fixed text embeddings with image embeddings, sacrificing natural performance and robustness. Addressing this specific vulnerability requires a novel framework that handles adversarial attacks at multiple levels of the classification hierarchy.
topics.approach: We propose a novel adversarial fine-tuning framework utilizing hierarchical embeddings. The approach involves training the model to maintain robust alignment across multiple trees, specifically targeting the margin size required to prevent collapse on superclass classes. We will employ visual embeddings at the desired depth of hierarchy and conduct systematic experiments on CIFAR-10 to evaluate how different embedding depths affect the maximum viable margin size under superclass adversarial perturbations.

topics.title: Stochastic Tokenization as a Defense Against Adversarial Perturbations in LLMs
topics.rationale: While recent literature suggests stochastic tokenization improves robustness by reducing sensitivity to adversarial perturbations, the specific mechanisms and benchmarks required to validate this claim remain underexplored. Existing models trained with stochastic tokenization may not exhibit the claimed robustness against various types of adversarial attacks, necessitating a rigorous evaluation framework.
topics.approach: We will evaluate the effectiveness of stochastic tokenization as a defense against adversarial perturbations in LLMs. Specifically, we will conduct experiments on the TIGER benchmark to measure the impact of tokenization strategies on model robustness. We will also explore the interaction between stochastic tokenization and other existing defense mechanisms to identify synergies or conflicts.

articles.title: Balanced Co-Clustering of Users and Items for Embedding Table Compression in Recommender Systems
articles.abstract: Recommender systems have advanced markedly over the past decade by transforming each user/item into a dense embedding vector with deep learning models. At industrial scale, embedding tables constituted by such vectors of all users/items demand a vast amount of parameters and impose heavy compute and memory overhead during training and inference, hindering model deployment under resource constraints.
