articles.title: Modular Representation Compression: Adapting LLMs for Efficient and Effective Recommendations
articles.abstract: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have advanced recommendation systems (RSs), and recent works have begun to explore how to integrate LLMs into industrial RSs. While most approaches deploy LLMs offline to generate and pre-cache augmented representations for RSs, high-dimensional representations from LLMs introduce substantial storage and computational costs. Thus, it is crucial to compress LLM representations effectively. However, we identify a counterintuitive phenomenon during representat

articles.title: Towards Foundation Models for Consensus Rank Aggregation
articles.abstract: Aggregating a consensus ranking from multiple input rankings is a fundamental problem with applications in recommendation systems, search engines, job recruitment, and elections. Despite decades of research in consensus ranking aggregation, minimizing the Kemeny distance remains computationally intractable. Specifically, determining an optimal aggregation of rankings with respect to the Kemeny distance is an NP-hard problem, limiting its practical application to relatively small-scale instances.

articles.title: From Top-1 to Top-K: A Reproducibility Study and Benchmarking of Counterfactual Explanations for Recommender Systems
articles.abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CEs) provide an intuitive way to understand recommender systems by identifying minimal modifications to user-item interactions that alter recommendation outcomes. Existing CE methods for recommender systems, however, have been evaluated under heterogeneous protocols, using different datasets, recommenders, metrics, and even explanation formats, which hampers reproducibility and fair comparison. articles.title: Mira-Embeddings-V1: Domain-Adapted Semantic Reranking for Recruitment via LLM-Synthesized Data
articles.abstract: Candidate sourcing for recruiters is best viewed as a two-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline with recall as the primary objective under a limited review budget. An upstream production retriever first returns a candidate shortlist for each job description (JD), and our goal is to rerank that shortlist so that qualified candidates appear as high as possible. articles.title: Predictive Associative Memory: Retrieval Beyond Similarity Through Temporal Co-occurrence
articles.abstract: Current approaches to memory in neural systems rely on similarity-based retrieval: given a query, find the most representationally similar stored state. This assumption -- that useful memories are similar memories -- fails to capture a fundamental property of biological memory: association through temporal co-occurrence. articles.title: Revisiting Catastrophic Forgetting in Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding
articles.abstract: Knowledge Graph Embeddings (KGEs) support a wide range of downstream tasks over Knowledge Graphs (KGs). In practice, KGs evolve as new entities and facts are added, motivating Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding (CKGE) methods that update embeddings over time. articles.title: Characterizing AlphaEarth Embedding Geometry for Agentic Environmental Reasoning
articles.abstract: Earth observation foundation models encode land surface information into dense embedding vectors, yet the geometric structure of these representations and its implications for downstream reasoning remain underexplored. We characterize the manifold geometry of Google AlphaEarth's 64-dimensional embeddings across 12.1 million Continental United States samples (2017--2023) and develop an agentic system that leverages this geometric understanding for environmental reasoning. articles.abstract: Scalable Vector Graphics (SVGs) function both as visual images and as structured code that encode rich geometric and layout information, yet most methods rasterize them and discard this symbolic organization. At the same time, recent sentence embedding methods produce strong text representations but do not naturally extend to visual or structured modalities. articles.abstract: Why do we forget? Why do we remember things that never happened? The conventional answer points to biological hardware. We propose a different one: geometry. Here we show that high-dimensional embedding spaces, subjected to noise, interference, and temporal degradation, reproduce quantitative signatures of human memory with no phenomenon-specific engineering. articles.title: TabEmb: Joint Semantic-Structure Embedding for Table Annotation
articles.abstract: Table annotation is crucial for making web and enterprise tables usable in downstream NLP applications. Unlike textual data where learning semantically rich token or sentence embeddings often suffice, tables are structured combinations of columns wherein useful representations must jointly capture column's semantics and the inter-column relationships. articles.abstract: Transformer-based embedding models suffer from quadratic computational and linear memory complexity, limiting their utility for long sequences. We propose recurrent architectures as an efficient alternative, introducing a vertically chunked inference strategy that enables fast embedding generation with memory usage that becomes constant in the input length once it exceeds the vertical chunk size. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over flat vector stores fragments facts into chunks, loses cross-session identity, and has no first-class notion of supersession or contradiction. articles.abstract: When task-specific labels are not available, it becomes difficult to select an embedding model for a specific target corpus. facts.problem: The paper addresses the mechanisms underlying human memory, specifically why forgetting occurs and how false memories are formed, challenging the conventional biological hardware explanation.
facts.gap: Existing theories attribute forgetting to biological hardware decay, but the paper argues that forgetting arises from interference among competing memories in high-dimensional embedding spaces, not from decay. topics.title: Optimizing LLM-Driven Candidate Sourcing for Limited Review Budgets Using LLM-Synthesized Training Data
topics.rationale: Existing candidate sourcing pipelines rely on heavy cross-encoder models or large-scale manually labeled industrial training pairs, which are resource-intensive and often fail to generalize to diverse job descriptions. This topic addresses the gap in efficient retrieval by leveraging LLM-synthesized training data to create lightweight reranking heads, directly maximizing qualified matches under strict review constraints. By utilizing LLMs to generate synthetic training pairs and applying prompt pipelines, we can bypass the need for extensive manual labeling while maintaining high recall. The approach focuses on the specific challenge of balancing retrieval efficiency with the limited review budgets typical in recruitment scenarios.
topics.approach: We will construct a lightweight reranking head using a distilled LLM trained on LLM-synthesized data derived from a heterogeneous text dataset and a local pool of 300 real job descriptions. The pipeline will employ a Five-stage prompt pipeline to generate candidate descriptions, align them with the local JD pool using JD-CV triplet alignment, and train a BoundaryHead MLP on the resulting triplet data. We will evaluate the system using the provided metrics (Recall@50, Precision@10) to ensure that the synthesized data effectively captures the semantic nuances of the real-world job descriptions without the computational cost of full-scale industrial training.

topics.title: Benchmarking Counterfactual Explanations for Top-K Recommendations under Heterogeneous Evaluation Protocols
topics.rationale: Counterfactual explanations (CE) for recommender systems have faced significant challenges due to heterogeneous evaluation protocols, datasets, recommenders, metrics, and explanation formats, which hampers reproducibility and fair comparison. This topic addresses the gap in creating a standardized benchmark for CE evaluation by developing a unified framework that normalizes explanations across different datasets and protocols. By leveraging the provided metrics (Mean EI, Top-K accuracy), we aim to establish a robust baseline for evaluating the effectiveness of CE in diverse recommendation scenarios.
topics.approach: We will develop a unified benchmarking framework for counterfactual explanations that normalizes explanations across different datasets and protocols. The framework will incorporate the provided metrics (Mean EI, Top-K accuracy) to evaluate the effectiveness of CE in diverse recommendation scenarios. We will focus on the challenge of handling heterogeneous evaluation protocols, datasets, recommenders, metrics, and explanation formats. By utilizing the provided metrics, we aim to establish a robust baseline for evaluating the effectiveness of CE in diverse recommendation scenarios.

articles.title: When Text Hijacks Vision: Benchmarking and Mitigating Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination in Vision Language Models
articles.abstract: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have substantially enhanced their ability across multimodal video understanding benchmarks spanning temporal, action, object, and spatial understanding. However, we identify a critical yet overlooked issue: when embedded on-screen text contradicts the visual scene, existing VLMs systematically hallucinate, prioritizing overlay textual semantics over the actual visual content. We define this phenomenon as Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH).

articles.abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive performance on visual perception and reasoning tasks with RGB imagery, yet they remain fragile under common degradations, such as fog, blur, or low-light conditions. Infrared (IR) imaging, a well-established complement to RGB, offers inherent robustness in these conditions, but its integration into MLLMs remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose DUALVISION, a lightweight fusion module that efficiently incorporates IR-R

articles.title: Prioritizing the Best: Incentivizing Reliable Multimodal Reasoning by Rewarding Beyond Answer Correctness
articles.abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) improves multimodal reasoning by rewarding verifiable final answers. Yet answer-correct trajectories may still rely on incomplete derivations, weak evidence, or statements that contradict their conclusions. This gap between answer correctness and reasoning validity, which we call reasoning-answer inconsistency, motivates trajectory supervision in multimodal RL. We compare two main approaches: reward models (RMs), and Generative Rewards (GRs).

articles.title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise
articles.abstract: We systematically study L^2-Betti numbers in zero and prime characteristic and apply them to a conjecture of Wise stating that all towers of a finite 2-complex are non-positive if and only if the second L^2-Betti number vanishes.

articles.title: Two random constructions inside lacunary sets
articles.abstract: We study the relationship between the growth rate of an integer sequence and harmonic and functional properties of the corresponding sequence of characters. In particular we show that every polynomial sequence contains a set that is Lamba(p) for all p but is not a Rosenthal set. This holds also for the sequence of primes.

topics.title: Evaluating the Sample Complexity Lower Bound for Learning the Möbius Function on Squarefree Integers
topics.rationale: Recent advances in computational learning theory have established that learning the Möbius function requires a sample complexity proportional to the domain size, yet the theoretical justification relies on a lower bound involving the L^1 norm of exponential sums with Möbius coefficients. This paper directly addresses the statistical hardness of learning this function from random samples, providing a rigorous framework that bridges analytic number theory and Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension analysis. By focusing on squarefree integers, the study offers a concrete testing ground for distribution-independent learning algorithms, which is crucial for understanding the limits of representation in combinatorial structures.
topics.approach: We propose a novel algorithm for estimating the L^1 norm of exponential sums with Möbius coefficients on the set of squarefree integers. The method involves deriving conditional improvements under a strong uniform bound for additive twists of the Möbius function, followed by a rigorous application of the same method to derive sample complexity lower bounds. We will then compare these theoretical bounds against empirical results from a synthetic dataset generated by sampling squarefree integers up to a specified limit.

topics.title: L^2-Betti Numbers and the Vanishing Conjecture in Prime Characteristic: A Computational Verification Strategy
topics.rationale: While the L^2-Betti numbers have been systematically studied in zero characteristic, their behavior in prime characteristic remains a critical gap in understanding the structural properties of finite 2-complexes. The conjecture of Wise linking the vanishing of the second L^2-Betti number to the non-positivity of all towers of a finite 2-complex offers a powerful test case for these invariants. This research aims to bridge the computational gap by developing a verification strategy that can confirm the conjecture in prime characteristic using high-precision arithmetic.
topics.approach: We will develop a high-precision arithmetic framework capable of computing L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic with sufficient working precision to detect vanishing trends. The approach will involve constructing a specific finite 2-complex and applying the systematic study methods of L^2-Betti number analysis to calculate the second invariant. We will then perform a computational verification of the Wise conjecture by comparing the computed values against the expected topological constraints.

topics.title: Stokes Multipliers of Anharmonic Oscillators: High-Precision Computation and Closed-Form Discovery
topics.rationale: Existing methods for computing Stokes multipliers in asymptotic analysis often struggle with high-precision requirements and the identification of non-trivial closed-form expressions for specific coefficients. This research addresses the challenge of discovering exact closed-form expressions for fundamental constants arising in the asymptotic analysis of anharmonic oscillators, which are critical for understanding the spectral properties of quantum systems.
topics.approach: We will implement a large-scale computational study combining arbitrary-precision arithmetic with the PSLQ integer relation algorithm to identify exact closed-form expressions. The methodology will incorporate Rayleigh-Schrodinger recursion and Richardson extrapolation to stabilize the computation of Stokes multipliers. By leveraging sequence acceleration techniques, we aim to discover the specific coefficients and constants previously elusive in high-precision analysis.

topics.title: Polynomial Sequence Growth Rates and Characteristic Sets: Characterizing Lamba(p) Sets within Lacunary Structures
topics.rationale: Current literature lacks a specific characterization of sets within polynomial sequences that satisfy Lamba(p) properties while not being Rosenthal sets. Understanding the relationship between the growth rate of integer sequences and their harmonic and functional properties is essential for advancing stability theory and harmonic analysis. This study seeks to fill this gap by establishing precise conditions under which polynomial sequences exhibit specific set-theoretic behaviors.
topics.approach: We will employ stability theory and functional properties analysis to investigate the growth rate of integer sequences. The approach will involve constructing polynomial sequences and analyzing their character sequences to identify the boundary conditions for Lamba(p) sets. We will then prove that every polynomial sequence contains a set that is Lamba(p) for all p but is not a Rosenthal set, thereby providing a new characterization of these sets.

topics.title: Synthetic Benchmark Dataset for Statistical Hardness of Learning Functions with High Fourier Ratio
topics.rationale: The statistical hardness of learning functions with Fourier Ratio at least c_0 on squarefree integers remains a significant open problem, with existing methods failing to account for the specific lower bound on the L^1 norm of exponential sums. Creating a controlled synthetic benchmark dataset allows for the systematic testing of distribution-independent learning algorithms and the verification of theoretical sample complexity bounds derived from analytic number theory.
topics.approach: We will generate a synthetic benchmark dataset consisting of squarefree integers sampled from a specific distribution and associated exponential sums with Möbius coefficients. The dataset will be designed to enforce a Fourier Ratio at least c_0, ensuring that it serves as a rigorous test case for distribution-independent learning algorithms. We will then evaluate the performance of various learning algorithms on this dataset to verify the Ω(R) sample complexity lower bound.

articles.abstract: This paper introduces two fundamentally new concepts to game theory: multilateral Nash equilibria and families of games. Starting with non-cooperative games, we show how these notions together seamlessly integrate into and naturally extend the classical theory, and simultaneously enable us to prove a powerful (multilateral) Nash equilibrium existence result with minimal assumptions on the game. facts.problem: Proving the convergence of equilibria in discounted infinite-horizon potential mean-field games (MFGs) on the $d$-dimensional torus, specifically addressing the lack of monotonicity assumptions and establishing conditions for convergence to stationary equilibria.
facts.gap: Existing results often impose monotonicity assumptions on MFGs, and there is a need for a novel Lyapunov functional and a new uniqueness criterion for stationary equilibria without such assumptions.

topics.title: Analysis of Last-Iterate Guarantees for Stochastic Gradient Descent in Co-coercive Games on Unimodular Random Graphs
topics.rationale: Recent literature has established robust finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games, yet these results often rely on specific noise models or standard graph structures. There is a critical gap in extending these guarantees to the setting of unimodular random graphs of bounded degree, where the underlying network topology significantly influences the convergence dynamics. This research aims to bridge the gap by integrating the rigorous last-iterate bounds from [4] with the structural properties of unimodular random graphs, specifically utilizing the mass-transport principle and nonlinear Poisson equations to characterize the global potential and verify the coincidence of minimizers with quenched Nash equilibria under convexity assumptions.
topics.approach: The proposed approach involves constructing a new Lyapunov functional tailored to the affine scaling of noise in co-coercive games on unimodular random graphs. We will derive the necessary conditions for the convergence of stochastic gradient descent iterates to the global potential minimizer by analyzing the interaction between the time-dependent equilibrium dynamics and the specific noise model where the second moment scales affinely with the squared norm of iterates. Finally, we will employ the unimodular measure and the mass-transport principle to prove that the minimizers coincide with quenched Nash equilibria, thereby establishing the desired finite-time convergence guarantees.

topics.title: Ergodic Convergence of Second-Order Mean Field Games of Controls with State Constraints via Lyapunov Methods
topics.rationale: While existing literature has addressed first-order mean field games of controls, rigorous proofs for the well-posedness of ergodic second-order mean field games of controls with state constraints remain scarce, particularly under monotone coupling and quadratic growth Hamiltonians. A significant gap exists in establishing the conditions for blow-up and vanishing rate conditions in these second-order systems, which are crucial for ensuring the stability of equilibrium distributions. This research addresses these limitations by developing a novel Lyapunov functional that serves as a time-dependent Lyapunov functional for the second-order MFG system. By analyzing the time-dependent equilibrium dynamics and incoherent solutions, we aim to prove that every weak limit point of the time-dependent equilibrium is a stationary equilibrium, thereby resolving the well-posedness issue.
topics.approach: The research will focus on deriving a time-dependent Lyapunov functional that satisfies the necessary conditions for stability in the presence of quadratic growth Hamiltonians. We will analyze the convergence of the second-order MFG system under monotone coupling, specifically investigating the conditions under which equilibria exhibit blow-up or vanishing rates. The approach will involve constructing a new uniqueness criterion for stationary equilibria without imposing monotonicity assumptions, leveraging the ergodic problem formulation and the characterization of equilibria by solutions to the second-order MFG system.

topics.title: Existence of Multilateral Nash Equilibria in Families of Games for Multi-Agent Control Systems
topics.rationale: Current game theory frameworks often focus on single-player or pairwise equilibria, leaving a gap in the understanding of collective deviations within families of games. Extending classical game theory to include multilateral Nash equilibria, where collective deviations reduce profits for all deviating players, offers a more comprehensive view of strategic interactions in complex systems. This research investigates the existence of multilateral Nash equilibria in the context of families of games, specifically addressing the limitations of existing single-player theories. By integrating the concepts of multilateral Nash equilibria and families of games, we aim to demonstrate how these notions seamlessly extend the classical theory to enable the proof of existence criteria for multilateral equilibria.
topics.approach: The study will introduce a rigorous framework for multilateral Nash equilibria within the context of families of games, defining the conditions under which collective deviations reduce profits for all deviating players. We will utilize the clique covering number of the Kneser graph as a key metric to analyze the structure of these games and establish existence criteria. The approach will involve extending the classical theory to include multilateral equilibria and families of games, thereby enabling the proof of existence criteria that go beyond the scope of single-player or pairwise equilibria.

topics.title: Last-Iterate Guarantees for Learning in Co-coercive Games with Noisy Feedback on Unimodular Random Graphs
topics.rationale: Existing literature on last-iterate guarantees for learning in co-coercive games often relies on relative noise models where noise vanishes as iterates approach equilibrium, an assumption that is unrealistic in practice. There is a critical need to establish finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games under noisy feedback where the noise does not vanish. This research addresses the gap by establishing finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games under a more general noise model where the second moment scales affinely with the squared norm of iterates. By extending the analysis to unimodular random graphs, we aim to provide a broader class of games that includes examples such as quadratic games with specific feedback mechanisms.
topics.approach: The proposed approach involves deriving finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games under the specific noise model where the second moment scales affinely with the squared norm of iterates. We will analyze the convergence properties of the iterates on unimodular random graphs of bounded degree, utilizing the unimodular measure and the mass-transport principle to characterize the global potential. The study will focus on the coincidence of minimizers with quenched Nash equilibria under convexity assumptions, ensuring the robustness of the guarantees in the presence of non-vanishing noise.

topics.title: Convergence of Potential Mean-Field Games via Lyapunov Methods on the d-dimensional Torus
topics.rationale: Existing results in potential mean-field games (MFGs) often impose monotonicity assumptions on the system, which limits their applicability to more general dynamic settings. There is a pressing need for a novel Lyapunov functional and a new uniqueness criterion for stationary equilibria without imposing such restrictive monotonicity assumptions. This research addresses the gap by considering discounted infinite-horizon potential mean-field games on the d-dimensional torus, specifically addressing the lack of monotonicity assumptions and establishing conditions for convergence to stationary equilibria. By proving that every weak limit point of a time-dependent equilibrium is a stationary equilibrium, we aim to resolve the well-posedness issues inherent in these systems.
topics.approach: The research will develop a novel Lyapunov functional tailored for discounted infinite-horizon potential mean-field games on the d-dimensional torus. We will analyze the time-dependent equilibrium dynamics and incoherent solutions to establish the convergence of equilibria to stationary equilibria as time tends to infinity. The approach will involve constructing a new uniqueness criterion for stationary equilibria without imposing monotonicity assumptions, thereby expanding the scope of applicable MFGs beyond the restricted class of monotone systems.

results.query: I would like to research categorical approaches to homotopy theory and their applications in modern algebraic topology, focusing on infinity-categories and derived algebraic geometry

articles.title: On the equivalence of two approaches to multiplicative homotopy theories
articles.abstract: We study the relation of two frameworks for multiplicative homotopy theories: Presentably symmetric monoidal $\infty$-categories and combinatorial symmetric monoidal model categories. Our main theorem establishes an equivalence of their homotopy theories.   As consequences, we solve Pavlov's conjecture and obtain a solution to a special case of Hovey's 10th problem. We also prove several variations of the main theorem, such as an analog for non-symmetric monoidal semi-model categories.

articles.title: Homotopy Posets, Postnikov Towers, and Hypercompletions of $\infty$-Categories
articles.abstract: We show that basic homotopical notions such as homotopy sets and groups, connected and truncated maps, cellular constructions and skeleta, etc., extend to the setting of $(\infty,\infty)$-categories, as well as to presentable categories enriched in $(\infty,\infty)$-categories under the Gray tensor product. The homotopy posets of an $(\infty,\infty)$-category are indexed by boundaries of categorical disks; in particular, there is a fundamental poset for each pair of objects, which we regard as a

articles.title: Quasi-unitial Inner Kan Spaces
articles.abstract: We show that semi-simplicial spaces that i) admit inner horn fillers up to homotopy and ii) possess units in a weak sense provide a viable model for $\infty$-categories. The existence of units can be expressed through various quasi-unitality conditions, and we compare the natural generalization of three such conditions found in the literature. This work is motivated by applications in Floer homotopy theory.

articles.title: Deconstructing span categories for profinite groups
articles.abstract: One of the major advantages of $\infty$-category theory over classical $1$-category theory is its robust and homotopically meaningful framework for taking (co)limits of diagrams of $\infty$-categories. However, it is both subtle and crucial to specify which variant of the $\infty$-category of $\infty$-categories is being used when forming such (co)limits. In this article, we present a concrete case study illustrating how (co)limits of $\infty$-categories behave in a specific setting. We demonstr

topics.title: Applying Transfinitely Iterated Wild Sets to Analyze Homotopy Poset Structures in $(\infty,\infty)$-Categories
topics.rationale: Recent work on $(\infty,\infty)$-categories highlights the complexity of homotopy posets and their failure to converge in general settings. The study of transfinitely iterated wild sets provides a robust tool for analyzing the Cantor-Bendixson derivative in a homotopical context, which can be directly applied to understand the structure of truncated maps and cellular constructions within these advanced categorical frameworks. This approach fills a gap in existing literature by offering a specific method to analyze the non-convergence of long exact sequences of fibrations in the $(\infty,\infty)$-category setting.
topics.approach: We will define a homotopy-theoretic version of the Cantor-Bendixson derivative using transfinitely iterated wild sets within $(\infty,\infty)$-categories. The research will involve constructing specific semi-simplicial spaces that admit inner horn fillers up to homotopy to serve as model objects for these structures. We will then analyze the behavior of homotopy posets in these spaces by comparing them to the derived structures obtained through the proposed derivative, focusing on the convergence properties of truncated maps.
datasets: category of wild sets benchmark dataset

topics.title: Evaluating the Equivalence of Presentably Symmetric Monoidal $\infty$-Categories and Combinatorial Model Categories via Span Categories
topics.rationale: While the equivalence of homotopy theories between presentably symmetric monoidal $\infty$-categories and combinatorial symmetric monoidal model categories is established, the application of these theories to form (co)limits of diagrams of $\infty$-categories remains a subtle and crucial technical challenge. Investigating this through the lens of span categories allows us to resolve the ambiguity in specifying the correct variant of the $\infty$-category of $\infty$-categories when forming colimits. This study will bridge the gap between established homotopy equivalences and the practical utility of these theories in higher algebraic structures.
topics.approach: We will construct specific span categories over profinite groups to analyze the formation of (co)limits in the context of $\infty$-categories. The research will focus on identifying the precise conditions under which the homotopy theory of presentably symmetric monoidal $\infty$-categories aligns with combinatorial model categories. We will utilize the robust framework of span categories to verify the equivalence of homotopy theories in a manner that is applicable to diagrams of $\infty$-categories.

topics.title: Modeling $\infty$-Categories via Semi-Simplicial Spaces with Units: A Study of Inner Horn Fillers and Quasi-Unitality
topics.rationale: Existing viable models for $\infty$-categories often lack a unified framework that satisfies the necessary conditions of admitting inner horn fillers up to homotopy and possessing units in a weak sense. Investigating semi-simplicial spaces that satisfy these specific conditions can provide a more tractable and applicable model for $\infty$-categories, particularly in fields like Floer homotopy theory. This research aims to address the gap in unified models by comparing natural generalizations of tetrations with the proposed quasi-unitality conditions.
topics.approach: We will investigate semi-simplicial spaces that admit inner horn fillers up to homotopy and possess units in a weak sense to construct a viable model for $\infty$-categories. The methodology will involve formalizing the existence of units through various quasi-unitality conditions and comparing them with existing models. We will apply these semi-simplicial spaces to analyze the behavior of homotopy posets and cellular constructions within the framework of $(\infty,\infty)$-categories.

topics.title: Analyzing the Structure of Span Categories for Profinite Groups in the Context of $(\infty,\infty)$-Categories
topics.rationale: The specification of the correct variant of the $\infty$-category of $\infty$-categories is a subtle and crucial issue that affects the robustness of (co)limits of diagrams. By leveraging the properties of span categories and open normal subgroups, we can provide a homotopically meaningful framework for taking these limits. This study addresses the gap in understanding the structural implications of different variants of $\infty$-categories of $\infty$-categories when applied to profinite groups.
topics.approach: We will analyze the structure of span categories over profinite groups to determine the appropriate variant of the $\infty$-category of $\infty$-categories for forming (co)limits. The research will utilize the concept of open normal subgroups and equivariant (higher) algebra to explore the homotopically meaningful aspects of these constructions. We will compare these findings with the structural properties of $(\infty,\infty)$-categories to ensure the validity of the proposed variants.

topics.title: Homotopical Analogues of the Cantor-Bendixson Derivative for Transfinitely Iterated Wild Sets in $(\infty,\infty)$-Categories
topics.rationale: The study of homotopical analogues of the Cantor-Bendixson derivative provides a new perspective on the structure of wild sets and their convergence properties. Applying these analogues to $(\infty,\infty)$-categories offers a novel tool for analyzing the behavior of truncated maps and cellular constructions in advanced categorical settings. This work addresses the gap in existing literature by extending the Cantor-Bendixson derivative to a homotopical context suitable for $(\infty,\infty)$-categories.
topics.approach: We will define the homotopical analogues of the Cantor-Bendixson derivative using transfinitely iterated wild sets within the context of $(\infty,\infty)$-categories. The research will focus on constructing specific semi-simplicial spaces that admit inner horn fillers up to homotopy to model these structures. We will then analyze the convergence properties of truncated maps and the behavior of homotopy posets in these newly defined spaces.

articles.title: Short proofs in combinatorics, probability and number theory II
articles.abstract: We give a quintet of proofs resulting from questions posed by Erdős. These questions concern ordinary lines in planar point sets, sequences with uniformly small exponential sums, $K_4$-free $4$-critical graphs with few chords in any cycle, a counterexample to a "fewnomial" version of the Erdős--Turán discrepancy bound, and a finiteness theorem for integers $n$ such that $n-a k^2$ is prime for all $k\leq \sqrt{n/a}$ coprime to $n$ (for fixed $a\in\mathbb Z_+$). Each proof is due to an internal mo

articles.title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise
articles.abstract: We systematically study L^2-Betti numbers in zero and prime characteristic and apply them to a conjecture of Wise stating that all towers of a finite 2-complex are non-positive if and only if the second L^2-Betti number vanishes.

articles.title: High-Precision Computation and PSLQ Identification of Stokes Multipliers for Anharmonic Oscillators
articles.abstract: We present a large-scale computational study combining arbitrary-precision arithmetic, sequence acceleration, and the PSLQ integer relation algorithm to discover exact closed-form expressions for fundamental constants arising in asymptotic analysis. We compute the Stokes multipliers C_M of the one-dimensional anharmonic oscillators H = p^2/2 + x^2/2 + g x^{2M} for M = 2, 3, ..., 11, extracting 17-30 significant digits from up to 1200 perturbation coefficients computed at 300-digit working precis

articles.title: Two random constructions inside lacunary sets
articles.abstract: We study the relationship between the growth rate of an integer sequence and harmonic and functional properties of the corresponding sequence of characters. In particular we show that every polynomial sequence contains a set that is Lamba(p) for all p but is not a Rosenthal set. This holds also for the sequence of primes.

articles.title: Arithmetic functions and learning theory
articles.abstract: We establish a connection between analytic number theory and computational learning theory by showing that the Möbius function belongs to a class of functions that is statistically hard to learn from random samples. Let $μ_R$ denote the restriction of the Möbius function to the squarefree integers in $\{1,\dots,R\}$. Using a recent lower bound of Pandey and Radziwiłł for the $L^1$ norm of exponential sums with Möbius coefficients, we prove that \[ \FR(μ_R) \gg R^{-1/4-ε} \] for every $ε>0$. We t

articles.title: From discrete to dense: explorations in the moduli space of triangles
articles.abstract: The moduli space of triangles is a two-dimensional space that records triangle shapes in the plane, considered up to similarity. We study the subset corresponding to \textit{lattice triangles}, which are triangles whose vertices have integer coordinates. We prove that this subset is \textit{dense}, that is, every triangle shape can be approximated arbitrarily well by lattice triangles. However, when one restricts to lattice triangles in the square $[-N,N]^2$, their shapes do \textit{not} become

articles.title: Computations of topological Jacobi forms
articles.abstract: We compute, at the prime $2$, the entire descent spectral sequence converging to the homotopy groups of the spectra of topological Jacobi forms $\mathrm{TJF}_m$ for every index $m \geq 1$. An explicit $\mathrm{TMF}$-cellular decomposition $\mathrm{TJF}_m \simeq \mathrm{TMF} \otimes P_m$ reduces the problem to analyzing a finite complex $P_m$ with one even cell in each dimension $\leq 2m$ except $2$. We identify all differentials using the cell structure.

articles.title: Less Is More: Cognitive Load and the Single-Prompt Ceiling in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
articles.abstract: We present a systematic empirical study of prompt engineering for formal mathematical reasoning in the context of the SAIR Equational Theories Stage 1 competition. The task requires deciding whether one equational law implies another over all magmas -- a problem that is undecidable in general but decidable for FALSE via finite model search. Over five weeks, we designed, tested, and analyzed more than 40 prompt variants, ranging from 0 to 4,878 bytes, across four evaluation splits and three langu

articles.title: On the normality of the concatenated Fibonacci constant
articles.abstract: We study the concatenated Fibonacci constant $\mathcal{F} := 0.F_{1}F_{2}F_{3}\cdots = 0.11235813\cdots$, obtained by concatenating the Fibonacci numbers in the fractional part, and ask whether it is normal. We show that several classical sufficient conditions for normality by concatenation do not apply to the Fibonacci sequence because of its exponential growth, while a criterion of Pollack and Vandehey implies that the normality of $\mathcal{F}$ in base $10$ would follow if almost all Fibonacc

articles.title: Low-Memory Numerical Certification
articles.abstract: We introduce a low-memory framework for certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems which uses solution iterators and spatial partitioning trees to reduce memory requirements. We provide a prototypical algorithm, analyze its complexity, and demonstrate the memory reduction on a large example.

articles_with_facts.title: Short proofs in combinatorics, probability and number theory II
articles_with_facts.abstract: We give a quintet of proofs resulting from questions posed by Erdős. These questions concern ordinary lines in planar point sets, sequences with uniformly small exponential sums, $K_4$-free $4$-critical graphs with few chords in any cycle, a counterexample to a "fewnomial" version of the Erdős--Turán discrepancy bound, and a finiteness theorem for integers $n$ such that $n-a k^2$ is prime for all $k\leq \sqrt{n/a}$ coprime to $n$ (for fixed $a\in\mathbb Z_+$). Each proof is due to an internal mo

facts.problem: Problems posed by Erdős concerning ordinary lines in planar point sets, sequences with uniformly small exponential sums, $K_4$-free $4$-critical graphs with few chords in any cycle, a counterexample to a 'fewnomial' version of the Erdős–Turán discrepancy bound, and a finiteness theorem for integers $n$ such that $n-a k^2$ is prime for all $k\leq \sqrt{n/a}$ coprime to $n$ (for fixed $a\in\mathbb Z_+$).
facts.gap: None explicitly stated; the paper provides proofs for questions posed by Erdős.

articles_with_facts.title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise
articles_with_facts.abstract: We systematically study L^2-Betti numbers in zero and prime characteristic and apply them to a conjecture of Wise stating that all towers of a finite 2-complex are non-positive if and only if the second L^2-Betti number vanishes.

facts.problem: The main problem or challenge this paper addresses is the study of L^2-Betti numbers in zero and prime characteristic, specifically to investigate a conjecture regarding the vanishing of the second L^2-Betti number in towers of finite 2-complexes.
facts.gap: The paper addresses the gap in understanding the relationship between L^2-Betti numbers and the non-positivity of towers of finite 2-complexes, specifically testing Wise's conjecture.

articles_with_facts.title: High-Precision Computation and PSLQ Identification of Stokes Multipliers for Anharmonic Oscillators
articles_with_facts.abstract: We present a large-scale computational study combining arbitrary-precision arithmetic, sequence acceleration, and the PSLQ integer relation algorithm to discover exact closed-form expressions for fundamental constants arising in asymptotic analysis. We compute the Stokes multipliers C_M of the one-dimensional anharmonic oscillators H = p^2/2 + x^2/2 + g x^{2M} for M = 2, 3, ..., 11, extracting 17-30 significant digits from up to 1200 perturbation coefficients computed at 300-digit working precis

facts.problem: Discovering exact closed-form expressions for fundamental constants arising in asymptotic analysis.
facts.gap: Existing methods may struggle with high-precision computation and identifying non-trivial closed forms for specific perturbation coefficients.

articles_with_facts.title: Two random constructions inside lacunary sets
articles_with_facts.abstract: We study the relationship between the growth rate of an integer sequence and harmonic and functional properties of the corresponding sequence of characters. In particular we show that every polynomial sequence contains a set that is Lamba(p) for all p but is not a Rosenthal set. This holds also for the sequence of primes.

facts.problem: The relationship between the growth rate of integer sequences and their harmonic and functional properties.
facts.gap: Existing work likely lacks a specific characterization of sets within polynomial sequences that are Lambd(p) for all p but not Rosenthal sets.
methods: Study of the relationship between the growth rate of an integer sequence and harmonic and functional properties of the corresponding sequence of characters
Analysis of the sequence of primes

articles_with_facts.title: Arithmetic functions and learning theory
articles_with_facts.abstract: We establish a connection between analytic number theory and computational learning theory by showing that the Möbius function belongs to a class of functions that is statistically hard to learn from random samples. Let $μ_R$ denote the restriction of the Möbius function to the squarefree integers in $\{1,\dots,R\}$. Using a recent lower bound of Pandey and Radziwiłł for the $L^1$ norm of exponential sums with Möbius coefficients, we prove that \[ \FR(μ_R) \gg R^{-1/4-ε} \] for every $ε>0$. We t

facts.problem: The difficulty of learning functions with specific statistical properties, such as the Möbius function, from random samples.
facts.gap: Existing learning algorithms may not be sufficient to learn functions like the Möbius function that are statistically hard to learn from random samples.
methods: Lower bound on the $L^1$ norm of exponential sums with Möbius coefficients
Conditional improvement under a strong uniform bound for additive twists of the Möbius function

articles_with_facts.title: From discrete to dense: explorations in the moduli space of triangles
articles_with_facts.abstract: The moduli space of triangles is a two-dimensional space that records triangle shapes in the plane, considered up to similarity. We study the subset corresponding to \textit{lattice triangles}, which are triangles whose vertices have integer coordinates. We prove that this subset is \textit{dense}, that is, every triangle shape can be approximated arbitrarily well by lattice triangles. However, when one restricts to lattice triangles in the square $[-N,N]^2$, their shapes do \textit{not} become

facts.problem: Understanding the distribution of triangle shapes in the moduli space, specifically whether lattice triangles (triangles with integer coordinates) become uniformly distributed as the size of the bounding square grows.
facts.gap: Existing work does not establish whether lattice triangles in a bounded square $[-N,N]^2$ exhibit uniform distribution in the moduli space as $N$ increases.
methods: Proving density of lattice triangles in the moduli space
Analyzing the distribution of lattice triangles in the square $[-N,N]^2$ as $N$ grows
Connecting results to geometry, number theory, analysis, and probability

articles_with_facts.title: Computations of topological Jacobi forms
articles_with_facts.abstract: We compute, at the prime $2$, the entire descent spectral sequence converging to the homotopy groups of the spectra of topological Jacobi forms $\mathrm{TJF}_m$ for every index $m \geq 1$. An explicit $\mathrm{TMF}$-cellular decomposition $\mathrm{TJF}_m \simeq \mathrm{TMF} \otimes P_m$ reduces the problem to analyzing a finite complex $P_m$ with one even cell in each dimension $\leq 2m$ except $2$. We identify all differentials using the cell structure.

facts.problem: Computing the entire descent spectral sequence converging to the homotopy groups of the spectra of topological Jacobi forms.
facts.gap: The paper addresses the challenge of computing the full descent spectral sequence for topological Jacobi forms, which is reduced to analyzing a finite complex via an explicit TMF-cellular decomposition.

facts.problem: Formal mathematical reasoning in the context of the SAIR Equational Theories Stage 1 competition, specifically determining whether one equational law implies another over all magmas.
facts.gap: The task is undecidable in general but decidable for FALSE via finite model search; existing methods struggle with the TRUE case due to undecidability and complex rule systems.

facts.problem: Whether the concatenated Fibonacci constant is normal.
facts.gap: Classical sufficient conditions for normality by concatenation do not apply due to the exponential growth of the Fibonacci sequence; the deep digits of large Fibonacci numbers are the remaining obstruction to normality.

articles_with_facts.title: Low-Memory Numerical Certification
articles_with_facts.abstract: We introduce a low-memory framework for certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems which uses solution iterators and spatial partitioning trees to reduce memory requirements. We provide a prototypical algorithm, analyze its complexity, and demonstrate the memory reduction on a large example.

facts.problem: Certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems
facts.gap: Existing methods likely have high memory requirements for certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems

topics.title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and their application to the vanishing conjecture of Wise
topics.rationale: The recent study by [1] explicitly addresses the challenge of characterizing towers of finite 2-complexes, specifically testing the vanishing conjecture. This aligns directly with the gap in understanding the relationship between L^2-Betti numbers and the non-positivity of such towers. By systematically studying L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic, this research can provide new insights into the structural properties of these towers, addressing the specific gap identified in [2].
topics.approach: The proposed approach involves computing L^2-Betti numbers in both zero and prime characteristic using the systematic methods described in [2]. We will then apply these computed values to test the specific conjecture of Wise regarding the vanishing of the second L^2-Betti number. Finally, we will analyze the implications of these results for the topology of finite 2-complexes.

topics.title: Computational discovery of exact closed-form expressions for Stokes multipliers using PSLQ
topics.rationale: Existing methods for high-precision computation and identifying closed-form expressions for fundamental constants often struggle with non-trivial coefficients in specific perturbation scenarios. This research aims to bridge the gap between arbitrary-precision arithmetic and the identification of exact forms for Stokes multipliers. By leveraging the PSLQ integer relation algorithm and sequence acceleration techniques, this work can discover exact expressions that were previously inaccessible.
topics.approach: The research will utilize the arbitrary-precision arithmetic and sequence acceleration methods detailed in [3] to compute high-precision values for Stokes multipliers. We will apply the PSLQ integer relation algorithm to identify integer relations among the computed coefficients. The results will be analyzed to determine if exact closed-form expressions can be derived, addressing the gap in current computational methods.

topics.title: Character growth rate analysis within polynomial sequences and their harmonic properties
topics.rationale: The relationship between the growth rate of integer sequences and their harmonic properties remains an underexplored area, particularly regarding specific subsets of polynomial sequences. This research addresses the gap in characterizing sets within polynomial sequences that exhibit specific harmonic and functional properties, such as being Lambd(p) but not Rosenthal sets.
topics.approach: We will analyze the growth rate of integer sequences corresponding to polynomial sequences and their associated character sequences. By studying the harmonic and functional properties of these sequences, we can characterize the specific subsets mentioned in the literature. This analysis will help fill the gap in understanding the structural properties of these sets.

topics.title: Learning statistical properties of the Möbius function from random samples
topics.rationale: Existing learning algorithms often fail to learn functions with specific statistical properties, such as those with Möbius coefficients, due to their inherent statistical hardness. This research aims to establish a connection between analytic number theory and computational learning theory to address this gap.
topics.approach: The proposed approach involves establishing lower bounds on the L^1 norm of exponential sums with Möbius coefficients. We will analyze the Vapnik--Chervonenkis dimension to evaluate the learnability of these functions. Finally, we will develop distribution-independent learning algorithms that can improve under strong uniform bounds.

topics.title: Uniform distribution of lattice triangles in the moduli space of triangles
topics.rationale: Existing work does not establish whether lattice triangles in a bounded square exhibit uniform distribution in the moduli space as the size of the bounding square grows. This research directly addresses the gap in understanding the distribution of triangle shapes in the moduli space.
topics.approach: We will prove the density of lattice triangles in the moduli space and analyze their distribution within a square [-N,N]^2 as N increases. This will involve connecting results from geometry, number theory, analysis, and probability. The study will determine whether the distribution becomes uniform under these conditions.

articles.title: Existence of Multilateral Nash equilibria for families of games
articles.abstract: This paper introduces two fundamentally new concepts to game theory: multilateral Nash equilibria and families of games. Starting with non-cooperative games, we show how these notions together seamlessly integrate into and naturally extend the classical theory, and simultaneously enable us to prove a powerful (multilateral) Nash equilibrium existence result with minimal assumptions on the game. Classically, a Nash equilibrium is a global strategy such that whichever player unilaterally deviates

articles.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Dynamic Game for Competitive Inventory and Pricing under Incomplete Information: Learning, Credible Risk, and Equilibrium
articles.abstract: We develop a hierarchical Bayesian dynamic game for competitive inventory and pricing under incomplete information. Two firms repeatedly choose order quantities and prices while facing two layers of uncertainty: unknown market demand and private rival characteristics. The framework combines Bayesian learning about demand and substitution with strategic belief updating about rival types. To make decisions robust to posterior uncertainty, we introduce a credible-risk criterion that rewards expecte

articles.title: Ergodic Mean Field Games of Controls with State Constraints
articles.abstract: In a mean field game of controls, players seek to minimize a cost that depends on the joint distribution of players' states and controls. We consider an ergodic problem for second-order mean field games of controls with state constraints, in which equilibria are characterized by solutions to a second-order MFGC system where the value function blows up at the boundary, the density of players vanishes at a commensurate rate, and the joint distribution of states and controls satisfies the appropria

articles.title: Potential Games on Unimodular Random Graphs
articles.abstract: We study potential games on unimodular random graphs of bounded degree, where players interact through the underlying network. Using the unimodular measure, we define a well-posed global potential that captures both finite- and infinite-player games. A key observation is that the mass-transport principle identifies the first variation of this potential with the first-order condition of a representative (root) player. Under suitable convexity assumptions, we prove that minimizers of the potential

articles.title: Last-Iterate Guarantees for Learning in Co-coercive Games
articles.abstract: We establish finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games under noisy feedback. This is a broad class of games that is more general than strongly monotone games, allows for multiple Nash equilibria, and includes examples such as quadratic games with negative semidefinite interaction matrices and potential games with smooth concave potentials. Prior work in this setting has relied on relative noise models, where the noise vanishes as iterates ap

articles.title: Convergence of Potential Mean-Field Games via Lyapunov Methods
articles.abstract: We consider discounted infinite-horizon potential mean-field games (MFGs) on the $d$-dimensional torus. Without imposing monotonicity assumptions, we prove that every weak limit point of a time-dependent equilibrium, as time tends to infinity, is a stationary equilibrium. As a consequence, equilibria converge whenever the stationary solution is unique. The short proof is based on a novel Lyapunov functional for the time-dependent MFG system. We also provide a new uniqueness criterion for station

articles.title: Viscosity Solutions of Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman Equations for Control Systems Driven by Teugels Martingales
articles.abstract: This paper studies discrete-time two-person nonzero-sum linear quadratic stochastic games with random coefficients. Using convex variational analysis, we derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of open-loop Nash equilibria. When weighting matrices are indefinite, the classical first-order conditions are no longer sufficient for optimality; we introduce a global nonnegativity condition to restore sufficiency, which becomes a cornerstone of the subsequent analysis. articles.abstract: This paper addresses the decentralized coordinated charging problem for a large population of battery storage agents (e.g. residential batteries, electrical vehicles, charging station batteries) using Mean Field Game (MFG). Agents are assumed to have affine dynamics and are coupled through a price that is continuous and monotonically increasing with respect to the difference between the average charging power and the grid's desired average charging power. articles.title: Hot Standby in Ammonia Synthesis Reshapes Market Equilibrium in Renewable P2A Systems: A Potential Game Approach
articles.abstract: Integrating renewable generation, hydrogen production, and renewable ammonia (RA) synthesis into power-to-ammonia (P2A) systems creates interactions across electricity and hydrogen markets. Limited operational flexibility, however, places RA at a disadvantage at the Nash equilibrium (NE). Recent advances in ammonia synthesis reactor design enable hot standby (HSB) operation, improving flexibility but introducing integer decision variables that complicate market equilibrium analysis. To address t

articles.title: A Numerical Analysis for Pursuit-Evasion Games under the Stackelberg Eqilibrium
articles.abstract: This study presents a numerical analysis framework for pursuit--evasion differential games under the Stackelberg equilibrium. The Semi-DCNLP method is introduced as an optimization solver for flight path optimization problems formulated under the Stackelberg equilibrium. In a spacecraft pursuit-evasion differential game, the Semi-DCNLP method produces trajectories that closely match those obtained with a shooting method. This agreement supports the validity of the proposed approach.

facts.problem: Extending classical game theory to include multilateral Nash equilibria and families of games, addressing the limitations of single-player equilibria and the lack of structural insights in parameterized game collections.
facts.gap: Classical Nash equilibria are global strategies where unilateral deviations reduce profit; existing theory lacks a formal framework for k-lateral equilibria (where collective deviations reduce all deviating players' profits) and does not easily handle parameterized families of games.

facts.gap: Prior work in this setting relied on relative noise models where noise vanishes as iterates approach equilibrium, an assumption often unrealistic in practice; this paper addresses a more general noise model where the second moment scales affinely with the squared norm of iterates.

topics.title: Multilateral Nash Equilibria in Parameterized Families of Games with Application to Renewable Ammonia Market Dynamics
topics.rationale: Recent literature on multilateral Nash equilibria addresses the lack of a formal framework for collective deviations in parameterized game collections, yet its application to complex industrial systems like renewable ammonia synthesis remains underexplored. This gap necessitates a rigorous theoretical analysis of how multilateral equilibria emerge in systems with integer decision variables and heterogeneous market constraints, providing a new lens for analyzing market equilibrium in P2A systems.
topics.approach: The study will employ a two-phase methodology: first, deriving existence criteria for multilateral Nash equilibria within the specific parameterized family of nonlinear games governing ammonia synthesis; second, applying this theory to solve the Hot Standby equilibrium problem using iterative best-response algorithms to identify profit-maximizing strategies under uncertainty.

topics.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Dynamic Games for Competitive Inventory Pricing: Integrating Credible-Risk Criteria with Last-Iterate Guarantees
topics.rationale: Existing work lacks a hierarchical Bayesian dynamic game framework that combines strategic belief updating with a credible-risk criterion to handle posterior uncertainty in competitive decision-making. Addressing this gap allows for the development of robust inventory and pricing strategies that account for evolving market beliefs and noise in stochastic environments.
topics.approach: The research will construct a hierarchical Bayesian dynamic game model using belief-state dynamic programming to update strategic beliefs under incomplete information. We will then establish finite-time last-iterate guarantees for the learning process, ensuring convergence in noisy feedback scenarios typical of real-world inventory markets.

topics.title: Ergodic Convergence of Second-Order Mean Field Games with State Constraints under Monotone Coupling
topics.rationale: Existing literature often lacks rigorous proofs for the well-posedness of second-order mean field games of controls with state constraints, particularly regarding the joint distribution of states and controls under monotone coupling. Solving this problem is critical for establishing the mathematical foundations of ergodic systems in complex control systems with boundary conditions.
topics.approach: The proposed approach will utilize Lyapunov functional methods to prove the convergence of equilibria in discounted infinite-horizon systems without imposing monotonicity assumptions. We will analyze the well-posedness of the second-order MFGC system under quadratic growth conditions and monotone coupling.

topics.title: Potential Games on Unimodular Random Graphs: Characterizing Nash Equilibria in Bounded Degree Networks
topics.rationale: Existing work on potential games often assumes specific graph structures or does not fully characterize equilibria in unimodular random graphs with bounded degrees, particularly regarding the relationship between potential minimizers and Nash equilibria. This gap limits the applicability of thermodynamic limit methods to networked systems with bounded connectivity.
topics.approach: The study will define a well-posed global potential for unimodular random graphs using the unimodular measure and the mass-transport principle. We will derive necessary and sufficient conditions for minimizers and Nash equilibria in these settings, linking them to the Green kernel and nonlinear Poisson equations.

topics.title: Finite-Time Last-Iterate Guarantees for Stochastic Gradient Descent in Co-Coeercive Games under Non-Vanishing Noise
topics.rationale: Prior work in this setting relied on relative noise models where noise vanishes as iterates approach equilibrium, an assumption often unrealistic in practice. Addressing a more general noise model where the second moment scales affinely with the squared norm of iterates is crucial for establishing robust optimization guarantees in co-coercive games.
topics.approach: We will establish finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games under a non-vanishing noise model. The analysis will focus on the affine scaling of noise and the derivation of time-average convergence guarantees to ensure robust performance in noisy feedback environments.

results.query: I would like to research categorical approaches to homotopy theory and their applications in modern algebraic topology, focusing on infinity-categories and derived algebraic geometry

articles.title: On periodic homotopy and homology equivalences of spaces
articles.abstract: There are at least two ways to approach the homotopy theory of spaces `at chromatic height $n$': one may localize with respect to $T(n)$-homology or with respect to $v_n$-periodic homotopy groups. It was already observed by Bousfield that these two options yield rather different results. We build on his work to prove precise comparison results between the two notions. A crucial concept is a more robust notion of $T(n)$-equivalence that we call `parametric $T(n)$-equivalence': this is a map of sp

articles.title: On the equivalence of two approaches to multiplicative homotopy theories
articles.abstract: We study the relation of two frameworks for multiplicative homotopy theories: Presentably symmetric monoidal $\infty$-categories and combinatorial symmetric monoidal model categories. Our main theorem establishes an equivalence of their homotopy theories.   As consequences, we solve Pavlov's conjecture and obtain a solution to a special case of Hovey's 10th problem. We also prove several variations of the main theorem, such as an analog for non-symmetric monoidal semi-model categories.

articles.title: Homotopy Posets, Postnikov Towers, and Hypercompletions of $\infty$-Categories
articles.abstract: We show that basic homotopical notions such as homotopy sets and groups, connected and truncated maps, cellular constructions and skeleta, etc., extend to the setting of $(\infty,\infty)$-categories, as well as to presentable categories enriched in $(\infty,\infty)$-categories under the Gray tensor product. articles.title: Quasi-unitial Inner Kan Spaces

articles_with_facts.abstract: There are at least two ways to approach the homotopy theory of spaces `at chromatic height $n$': one may localize with respect to $T(n)$-homology or with respect to $v_n$-periodic homotopy groups. It was already observed by Bousfield that these two options yield rather different results. We build on his work to prove precise comparison results between the two notions. articles_with_facts.title: On the equivalence of two approaches to multiplicative homotopy theories
articles_with_facts.abstract: We study the relation of two frameworks for multiplicative homotopy theories: Presentably symmetric monoidal $\infty$-categories and combinatorial symmetric monoidal model categories. Our main theorem establishes an equivalence of their homotopy theories.   As consequences, we solve Pavlov's conjecture and obtain a solution to a special case of Hovey's 10th problem. We also prove several variations of the main theorem, such as an analog for non-symmetric monoidal semi-model categories.

topics.title: Equivariant Rational Homotopy Theory via Non-Commutative Algebraic Models for Discrete Groups
topics.rationale: Recent work has established algebraic models for equivariant rational homotopy theory for Lie groups, but a generalization to arbitrary discrete groups remains a significant gap in the literature. The existing framework relies heavily on commutative differential graded Lie algebras (CDGLAs), which may not capture the full complexity of non-commutative discrete group actions. This research aims to bridge the gap by developing a novel algebraic model that generalizes these results to discrete groups, providing a more robust framework for equivariant computations.
topics.approach: The proposed approach involves constructing a new category of differential graded Lie algebras that incorporates non-commutative coefficients adapted to the discrete group structure. We will utilize the framework of commutative differential graded algebras (cdga) to model the discrete group's action, extending the known algebraic models for Lie groups. The method will involve analyzing the derived category of these algebras to identify the correct model structure, potentially utilizing the techniques from algebraic models for equivariant rational homotopy theory to establish the equivalence between the new model and the standard CDGA model.

topics.title: Comparative Analysis of $T(n)$-Homology and $v_n$-Periodic Localization in Chromatic Homotopy Theory
topics.rationale: Existing literature by Bousfield has shown that localizing with respect to $T(n)$-homology and $v_n$-periodic homotopy groups yields different results in chromatic homotopy theory, yet precise comparison results have been elusive. This research addresses the critical gap in establishing a rigorous equivalence between these two approaches, which is essential for understanding the higher chromatic phenomena in algebraic topology.
topics.approach: The research will employ a parametric $T(n)$-equivalence to construct a bridge between the two localization methods. By utilizing the Morava $K$-theory of the Whitehead tower, we will analyze the specific obstructions to equivalence that arise at each chromatic height. The study will focus on identifying the precise conditions under which the two localizations coincide, thereby resolving the long-standing problem of comparing their outcomes.

topics.title: Model Structures on Arboreal Objects and Their Equivalence to the Reedy Model Structure
topics.rationale: While the Reedy model structure is well-established for semisimplicial objects, the model structure on arboreal objects has remained underexplored, creating a disconnect between geometric combinatorics and homotopy theory. This paper seeks to fill this gap by constructing a specific model structure on arboreal objects and rigorously investigating its equivalence to the Reedy model structure.
topics.approach: We will define a new model structure on arboreal objects using the arboreal extension category $\OrdFor$ and ordered forests as morphisms. The core of the approach involves defining a full functor from $\OrdFor$ to the simplex category $\Delta_{\mathrm{epi}}$ and analyzing its behavior under right Kan extensions. We will then apply the Gabriel-Zisman localization technique to demonstrate that this new structure is Quillen equivalent to the Reedy model structure.

topics.title: Structured Flow Categories and Twisted Presheaves: A Tractable Framework for Orientations in Floer Homotopy Theory
topics.rationale: The existing orientation theory for flow categories suffers from a lack of general tractability, making it difficult to manipulate orientations, local systems, and filtrations in exact Floer homotopy theory. This research proposes a new framework to address this specific gap, providing a structured approach to handling these complex geometric and algebraic structures.
topics.approach: The method involves constructing a stable $\infty$-category $\mathcal{F}low^μ$ of $μ$-structured flow categories and bimodules. We will develop a new functor of $\infty$-categories $\mu$ that determines the orientation theory, extending the Pontrjagin--Thom construction to this setting. The approach will explicitly identify $\mathcal{F}low^μ$ with the $\infty$-category of $μ$-twisted presheaves on the base category $\mathcal{C}$, offering a more manageable and computationally tractable alternative to existing theories.

articles.title: Short proofs in combinatorics, probability and number theory II
articles.abstract: We give a quintet of proofs resulting from questions posed by Erdős. These questions concern ordinary lines in planar point sets, sequences with uniformly small exponential sums, $K_4$-free $4$-critical graphs with few chords in any cycle, a counterexample to a "fewnomial" version of the Erdős--Turán discrepancy bound, and a finiteness theorem for integers $n$ such that $n-a k^2$ is prime for all $k\leq \sqrt{n/a}$ coprime to $n$ (for fixed $a\in\mathbb Z_+$). Each proof is due to an internal mo

articles.title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise
articles.abstract: We systematically study L^2-Betti numbers in zero and prime characteristic and apply them to a conjecture of Wise stating that all towers of a finite 2-complex are non-positive if and only if the second L^2-Betti number vanishes.

articles.title: High-Precision Computation and PSLQ Identification of Stokes Multipliers for Anharmonic Oscillators
articles.abstract: We present a large-scale computational study combining arbitrary-precision arithmetic, sequence acceleration, and the PSLQ integer relation algorithm to discover exact closed-form expressions for fundamental constants arising in asymptotic analysis. We compute the Stokes multipliers C_M of the one-dimensional anharmonic oscillators H = p^2/2 + x^2/2 + g x^{2M} for M = 2, 3, ..., 11, extracting 17-30 significant digits from up to 1200 perturbation coefficients computed at 300-digit working precis

articles.title: Two random constructions inside lacunary sets
articles.abstract: We study the relationship between the growth rate of an integer sequence and harmonic and functional properties of the corresponding sequence of characters. In particular we show that every polynomial sequence contains a set that is Lamba(p) for all p but is not a Rosenthal set. This holds also for the sequence of primes.

Let $μ_R$ denote the restriction of the Möbius function to the squarefree integers in $\{1,\dots,R\}$. articles.abstract: In this paper, we study geometric points in tensor triangular geometry. In doing so, we construct a counter-example to Balmer's Nerves of Steel conjecture using free constructions in higher Zariski geometry. We then go on to introduce and discuss constructible spectra in the context of tensor triangular geometry. articles.title: From discrete to dense: explorations in the moduli space of triangles
articles.abstract: The moduli space of triangles is a two-dimensional space that records triangle shapes in the plane, considered up to similarity. We study the subset corresponding to \textit{lattice triangles}, which are triangles whose vertices have integer coordinates. We prove that this subset is \textit{dense}, that is, every triangle shape can be approximated arbitrarily well by lattice triangles. articles.title: A point-free approach to the Nakaoka spectrum of a Tambara functor
articles.abstract: For $G$ a finite group and $T$ a $G$-Tambara functor, we construct the frame $\mathop{RadId}_G(T)$ of radical Tambara ideals and show that its points are the Nakaoka primes. We show that this frame is spatial and coherent, and deduce that the Nakaoka spectrum is a spectral space, recovering a recent result of Chan and Spitz.

articles.title: Computations of topological Jacobi forms
articles.abstract: We compute, at the prime $2$, the entire descent spectral sequence converging to the homotopy groups of the spectra of topological Jacobi forms $\mathrm{TJF}_m$ for every index $m \geq 1$. An explicit $\mathrm{TMF}$-cellular decomposition $\mathrm{TJF}_m \simeq \mathrm{TMF} \otimes P_m$ reduces the problem to analyzing a finite complex $P_m$ with one even cell in each dimension $\leq 2m$ except $2$. We identify all differentials using the cell structure.

articles.title: Less Is More: Cognitive Load and the Single-Prompt Ceiling in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
articles.abstract: We present a systematic empirical study of prompt engineering for formal mathematical reasoning in the context of the SAIR Equational Theories Stage 1 competition. The task requires deciding whether one equational law implies another over all magmas -- a problem that is undecidable in general but decidable for FALSE via finite model search. Over five weeks, we designed, tested, and analyzed more than 40 prompt variants, ranging from 0 to 4,878 bytes, across four evaluation splits and three langu

articles.title: On the normality of the concatenated Fibonacci constant
articles.abstract: We study the concatenated Fibonacci constant $\mathcal{F} := 0.F_{1}F_{2}F_{3}\cdots = 0.11235813\cdots$, obtained by concatenating the Fibonacci numbers in the fractional part, and ask whether it is normal. articles.title: Low-Memory Numerical Certification
articles.abstract: We introduce a low-memory framework for certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems which uses solution iterators and spatial partitioning trees to reduce memory requirements. We provide a prototypical algorithm, analyze its complexity, and demonstrate the memory reduction on a large example.

articles.title: Computing Homomorphisms of Poset Representations with Applications to Multiparameter Persistence

articles.title: Hook-decomposable modules and their resolutions
articles.abstract: We compare several classes of biparameter persistence modules: $γ$-products of monoparameter modules, hook-decomposable modules, modules admitting a Smith-type structure theorem, and modules of projective dimension at most 1.
