The task requires deciding whether one equational law implies another over all magmas -- a problem that is undecidable in general but decidable for FALSE via finite model search. Over five weeks, we designed, tested, and analyzed more than 40 prompt variants, ranging from 0 to 4,878 bytes, across four evaluation splits and three langu

facts.problem: Formal mathematical reasoning in the context of the SAIR Equational Theories Stage 1 competition, specifically determining whether one equational law implies another over all magmas.
facts.gap: The task is undecidable in general but decidable for FALSE via finite model search; existing methods struggle with the TRUE case due to undecidability and complex rule systems, leading to performance plateaus.

articles_with_facts.title: On the normality of the concatenated Fibonacci constant
articles_with_facts.abstract: We study the concatenated Fibonacci constant $\mathcal{F} := 0.F_{1}F_{2}F_{3}\cdots = 0.11235813\cdots$, obtained by concatenating the Fibonacci numbers in the fractional part, and ask whether it is normal. We show that several classical sufficient conditions for normality by concatenation do not apply to the Fibonacci sequence because of its exponential growth, while a criterion of Pollack and Vandehey implies that the normality of $\mathcal{F}$ in base $10$ would follow if almost all Fibonacc

facts.problem: Whether the concatenated Fibonacci constant is normal.
facts.gap: Classical sufficient conditions for normality by concatenation do not apply due to the exponential growth of the Fibonacci sequence; the paper isolates the obstruction to normality in the deep digits of large Fibonacci numbers.

articles_with_facts.title: Low-Memory Numerical Certification
articles_with_facts.abstract: We introduce a low-memory framework for certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems which uses solution iterators and spatial partitioning trees to reduce memory requirements. We provide a prototypical algorithm, analyze its complexity, and demonstrate the memory reduction on a large example.

facts.problem: Certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems
facts.gap: Existing methods likely have high memory requirements for certifying numerical solutions to polynomial systems

articles_with_facts.title: Computing Homomorphisms of Poset Representations with Applications to Multiparameter Persistence
articles_with_facts.abstract: We present algorithms to compute the vector space of homomorphisms Hom(X,Y) between finitely generated representations of the partially ordered set Z^d. Our results generalise to any partially ordered set. Our main theoretical contribution is a uniqueness result for lifts of homomorphisms along free resolutions, which we use to obtain an algorithm running in O(n^4 (thick(Y) + thick(Omega^1 Y))^2 + T_ker(d,n)) time, where thick(Y) denotes the maximal pointwise dimension of Y and T_ker is the time

facts.problem: Computing the vector space of homomorphisms between finitely generated representations of the partially ordered set Z^d and improving the runtime of decomposition algorithms.
facts.gap: Existing methods have a naive O(n^6) bound; the paper improves this for small thick(Y) and specifically for uniquely graded modules.
methods: algorithms to compute the vector space of homomorphisms Hom(X,Y)
lifting of homomorphisms along free resolutions
classical approach due to Green, Heath, and Struble

articles_with_facts.title: Hook-decomposable modules and their resolutions
articles_with_facts.abstract: We compare several classes of biparameter persistence modules: $γ$-products of monoparameter modules, hook-decomposable modules, modules admitting a Smith-type structure theorem, and modules of projective dimension at most 1. We determine all logical implications among these classes, providing explicit counterexamples showing that the converses fail when appropriate. In particular, $γ$-products (i.e., hook-decomposable modules) form a very small subclass of biparameter modules, precisely the one

facts.problem: The paper addresses the structural complexity of biparameter persistence modules, specifically investigating the relationships between various classes of modules and determining logical implications among them.
facts.gap: Existing work likely lacks explicit counterexamples showing that the converses of logical implications fail in the biparameter setting, and it does not fully characterize the structural complexity of biparameter modules compared to monoparameter ones.
methods: Comparison of several classes of biparameter persistence modules
Analysis of logical implications among these classes
Investigation of γ-products of monoparameter modules
Investigation of modules admitting a Smith-type structure theorem
Investigation of modules of projective dimension at most 1

articles_with_facts.title: Limit Computation Over Posets via Minimal Initial Functors
articles_with_facts.abstract: It is well known that limits can be computed by restricting along an initial functor, and that this often simplifies limit computation. We systematically study the algorithmic implications of this idea for diagrams indexed by a finite poset. We say an initial functor $F\colon C\to D$ with $C$ small is \emph{minimal} if the sets of objects and morphisms of $C$ each have minimum cardinality, among the sources of all initial functors with target $D$. For $Q$ a finite poset or $Q\subseteq \mathbb N^

facts.problem: Computing limits of functors, particularly for diagrams indexed by finite posets, and determining the cost of computing generalized rank.
facts.gap: Existing methods for computing limits often rely on restricting along an initial functor, which simplifies computation but limits the generality of the approach. The paper addresses the algorithmic implications of this restriction for diagrams indexed by finite posets.
methods: Systematic study of algorithmic implications of restricting along an initial functor for diagrams indexed by a finite poset
Description of all minimal initial functors $F\colon P	o Q$ where $P$ is a finite poset or $Q\subseteq \mathbb N^d$ is an interval
Efficient algorithms to compute a choice of minimal initial functor
Computation of limits of functors $G\colon Q	o \mathbf{Vec}$
Computation of the generalized rank of a functor $G$ via the induced map $\lim G	o \mathop{\mathrm{colim}} G$

topics.title: Analyzing the Normality of Concatenated Fibonacci Constants using Large-Scale Numerical Experiments and Pollack-Vandehey Criterion
topics.rationale: Recent work on concatenated Fibonacci constants has established that classical sufficient conditions for normality fail due to the exponential growth of the sequence. This study addresses the gap by isolating the specific obstruction in the deep digits using large-scale numerical experiments on the first 500,000 Fibonacci numbers. By applying the Pollack and Vandehey criterion to pooled interior blocks, we can rigorously determine the asymptotic behavior of the deep digits and verify the normality of the constant.
topics.approach: The research will employ large-scale numerical computation to analyze the global single-digit counts and k-block statistics for k=2, 3, and 4. We will utilize the Pollack and Vandehey criterion combined with positional decomposition techniques to isolate the obstruction to normality. Statistical tests on the deep digits will be conducted to confirm whether the concatenation process satisfies the necessary conditions for normality.

topics.title: Computing Homomorphisms of Poset Representations with Applications to Multiparameter Persistence
topics.rationale: Existing methods for computing the vector space of homomorphisms between finitely generated representations of the partially ordered set $\mathbb{Z}^d$ suffer from a naive $O(n^6)$ bound, which becomes computationally prohibitive for higher dimensions. This topic addresses the gap in runtime efficiency for decomposition algorithms by improving the complexity specifically for uniquely graded modules. The proposed algorithm aims to achieve an $O(n^4)$ or $O(n^3)$ complexity bound depending on the thickness of the poset.
topics.approach: We will present novel algorithms to compute the vector space of homomorphisms $\mathrm{Hom}(X, Y)$ between finitely generated representations of $\mathbb{Z}^d$. The method will leverage the lifting of homomorphisms along free resolutions and the classical approach due to Green, Heath, and Struble, while introducing a new algorithm AIDA to enhance performance. We will also utilize the Persistence Algebra C++ library to implement these computations efficiently.

topics.title: Limit Computation Over Posets via Minimal Initial Functors
topics.rationale: Computing limits of functors, particularly for diagrams indexed by finite posets, is a fundamental challenge in category theory. While restricting along an initial functor simplifies computation, it limits generality. This research addresses the gap by systematically analyzing the algorithmic implications of this restriction for diagrams indexed by finite posets, aiming to determine the cost of computing generalized rank and limits efficiently.
topics.approach: We will conduct a systematic study of the algorithmic implications of restricting along an initial functor for diagrams indexed by a finite poset. We will describe all minimal initial functors $F: P 	o Q$ where $P$ is a finite poset or $Q \subseteq \mathbb{N}^d$ is an interval. Efficient algorithms to compute a choice of minimal initial functor and the computation of limits of functors $G: Q 	o \mathbf{Vec}$ will be developed. Finally, we will compute the generalized rank of a functor $G$ via the induced map $\lim G 	o \mathrm{colim} G$.

articles.title: Existence of Multilateral Nash equilibria for families of games
articles.abstract: This paper introduces two fundamentally new concepts to game theory: multilateral Nash equilibria and families of games. Starting with non-cooperative games, we show how these notions together seamlessly integrate into and naturally extend the classical theory, and simultaneously enable us to prove a powerful (multilateral) Nash equilibrium existence result with minimal assumptions on the game. articles.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Dynamic Game for Competitive Inventory and Pricing under Incomplete Information: Learning, Credible Risk, and Equilibrium
articles.abstract: We develop a hierarchical Bayesian dynamic game for competitive inventory and pricing under incomplete information. Two firms repeatedly choose order quantities and prices while facing two layers of uncertainty: unknown market demand and private rival characteristics. The framework combines Bayesian learning about demand and substitution with strategic belief updating about rival types. To make decisions robust to posterior uncertainty, we introduce a credible-risk criterion that rewards expecte

articles.title: Ergodic Mean Field Games of Controls with State Constraints
articles.abstract: In a mean field game of controls, players seek to minimize a cost that depends on the joint distribution of players' states and controls. We consider an ergodic problem for second-order mean field games of controls with state constraints, in which equilibria are characterized by solutions to a second-order MFGC system where the value function blows up at the boundary, the density of players vanishes at a commensurate rate, and the joint distribution of states and controls satisfies the appropria

articles.title: Potential Games on Unimodular Random Graphs
articles.abstract: We study potential games on unimodular random graphs of bounded degree, where players interact through the underlying network. Using the unimodular measure, we define a well-posed global potential that captures both finite- and infinite-player games. A key observation is that the mass-transport principle identifies the first variation of this potential with the first-order condition of a representative (root) player. articles.title: Last-Iterate Guarantees for Learning in Co-coercive Games
articles.abstract: We establish finite-time last-iterate guarantees for vanilla stochastic gradient descent in co-coercive games under noisy feedback. This is a broad class of games that is more general than strongly monotone games, allows for multiple Nash equilibria, and includes examples such as quadratic games with negative semidefinite interaction matrices and potential games with smooth concave potentials. Prior work in this setting has relied on relative noise models, where the noise vanishes as iterates ap

articles.title: Convergence of Potential Mean-Field Games via Lyapunov Methods
articles.abstract: We consider discounted infinite-horizon potential mean-field games (MFGs) on the $d$-dimensional torus. Without imposing monotonicity assumptions, we prove that every weak limit point of a time-dependent equilibrium, as time tends to infinity, is a stationary equilibrium. As a consequence, equilibria converge whenever the stationary solution is unique. The short proof is based on a novel Lyapunov functional for the time-dependent MFG system. We also provide a new uniqueness criterion for station

articles.title: Viscosity Solutions of Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman Equations for Control Systems Driven by Teugels Martingales
articles.abstract: This paper studies discrete-time two-person nonzero-sum linear quadratic stochastic games with random coefficients. Using convex variational analysis, we derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of open-loop Nash equilibria. When weighting matrices are indefinite, the classical first-order conditions are no longer sufficient for optimality; we introduce a global nonnegativity condition to restore sufficiency, which becomes a cornerstone of the subsequent analysis. articles.title: Price-Coordinated Mean Field Games with State Augmentation for Decentralized Battery Charging
articles.abstract: This paper addresses the decentralized coordinated charging problem for a large population of battery storage agents (e.g. residential batteries, electrical vehicles, charging station batteries) using Mean Field Game (MFG). Agents are assumed to have affine dynamics and are coupled through a price that is continuous and monotonically increasing with respect to the difference between the average charging power and the grid's desired average charging power. articles.title: Hot Standby in Ammonia Synthesis Reshapes Market Equilibrium in Renewable P2A Systems: A Potential Game Approach
articles.abstract: Integrating renewable generation, hydrogen production, and renewable ammonia (RA) synthesis into power-to-ammonia (P2A) systems creates interactions across electricity and hydrogen markets. Limited operational flexibility, however, places RA at a disadvantage at the Nash equilibrium (NE). Recent advances in ammonia synthesis reactor design enable hot standby (HSB) operation, improving flexibility but introducing integer decision variables that complicate market equilibrium analysis. To address t

articles.title: A Numerical Analysis for Pursuit-Evasion Games under the Stackelberg Eqilibrium
articles.abstract: This study presents a numerical analysis framework for pursuit--evasion differential games under the Stackelberg equilibrium. The Semi-DCNLP method is introduced as an optimization solver for flight path optimization problems formulated under the Stackelberg equilibrium. In a spacecraft pursuit-evasion differential game, the Semi-DCNLP method produces trajectories that closely match those obtained with a shooting method. This agreement supports the validity of the proposed approach.

articles.title: A Stackelberg Game Framework with Drainability Guardrails for Pricing and Scaling in Multi-Tenant GPU Cloud Platforms
articles.abstract: Modern Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)-backed services must satisfy strict latency service-level objectives (SLOs) while controlling spare-capacity cost. In multi-tenant GPU cloud platforms, this trade-off is inherently dynamic because workload demand is endogenous; specifically, pricing shapes the submissions of heterogeneous tenants, which subsequently impact congestion and delay. We formulate the joint pricing-and-scaling problem as a large-population Stackelberg game problem, and we derive an

articles.title: Body-Reservoir Governance in Repeated Games: Embodied Decision-Making, Dynamic Sentinel Adaptation, and Complexity-Regularized Optimization
articles.abstract: Standard game theory explains cooperation in repeated games through conditional strategies such as Tit-for-Tat (TfT), but these require continuous computation that imposes physical costs on embodied agents. We propose a three-layer Body-Reservoir Governance (BRG) architecture: (1) a body reservoir (echo state network) whose $d$-dimensional state performs implicit inference over interaction history, serving as both decision-maker and anomaly detector, (2) a cognitive filter providing costly strat

articles.title: Linearly Solvable Continuous-Time General-Sum Stochastic Differential Games
articles.abstract: This paper introduces a class of continuous-time, finite-player stochastic general-sum differential games that admit solutions through an exact linear PDE system. We formulate a distribution planning game utilizing the cross-log-likelihood ratio to naturally model multi-agent spatial conflicts, such as congestion avoidance. A contest designer with a fixed budget must then design a mechanism that allocates a prize $p_i$ to the $i$-th rank based on the outcome, to incentivize contestants to exert higher costly efforts and induce high-quality outcomes. facts.gap: Existing work does not address the structural failure mode where delay-insensitive workloads sustain a residual demand floor, making the backlog undrainable under bounded price and service capacity.

facts.gap: Existing game theory relies on continuous computation for cooperation strategies, which imposes physical costs on embodied agents; this paper addresses the need for a computationally efficient, embodied alternative.

facts.gap: Existing contest literature has little or no attention to nonconvex objectives in contestants' qualities, such as arbitrary posynomials, convex combinations of user welfare and average quality, and S-shaped functions.

topics.title: Multilateral Nash Equilibria in Parameterized Game Families for Collaborative Energy Grids
topics.rationale: Current game theory literature focuses on pairwise or single-player Nash equilibria, which fails to capture the collective deviation strategies necessary for large-scale collaborative energy grids. The gap identified in recent work regarding families of games and multilateral equilibria offers a theoretical foundation for analyzing how groups of agents can simultaneously reduce collective profits to achieve stability. This is particularly relevant for modeling renewable energy markets where individual rationality often conflicts with system-wide efficiency.
topics.approach: We propose a framework to define multilateral Nash equilibria within parameterized game families of energy grid dynamics. The approach involves constructing a Kneser graph covering number to characterize the existence of collective deviations. We will utilize a domain-specific evaluation corpus of simulated grid scenarios to test the robustness of these equilibria under varying load conditions.

topics.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Dynamic Games for Uncertain Battery Charging and Pricing
topics.rationale: Existing competitive inventory and pricing models often lack a rigorous framework that combines strategic belief updating with a credible-risk criterion to handle uncertainty in private rival characteristics. The specific gap of combining hierarchical Bayesian dynamic games with a credible-risk criterion remains unaddressed in operational research literature. This topic addresses the critical need for a method that can learn and update beliefs dynamically while ensuring risk-averse decision-making in volatile battery charging markets.
topics.approach: We develop a hierarchical Bayesian dynamic game model specifically for competitive inventory and pricing under incomplete information. The methodology will integrate belief-state dynamic programming with a credible-risk criterion to derive equilibrium strategies. We will evaluate the model using a high-dimensional protein-expression dataset as a proxy for complex state-space dynamics in battery systems.

topics.title: Second-Order Mean Field Games of Controls with State Constraints in Autonomous Systems
topics.rationale: Existing literature lacks a complete characterization of equilibria for second-order mean field games of controls with state constraints, specifically regarding the blow-up of the value function and the vanishing density of players. This limitation hinders the analysis of complex autonomous systems where state constraints play a critical role. Filling this gap requires a rigorous mathematical framework that addresses the fixed-point relation for the joint distribution in infinite-player contexts.
topics.approach: We study second-order mean field games of controls with state constraints and propose a complete characterization of their equilibria. The approach utilizes monotone coupling and Hamiltonians with at most quadratic growth to analyze the system dynamics. We will employ numerical simulations on a synthetic benchmark dataset to validate the convergence properties of the proposed equilibria.

topics.title: Potential Games on Unimodular Random Graphs for Distributed Network Optimization
topics.rationale: Existing work on potential games often assumes specific graph structures or finite-player settings, limiting their applicability to modern, large-scale networked systems. The gap in characterizing global potentials and Nash equilibria for unimodular random graphs of bounded degree represents a significant theoretical advancement. This research aims to establish well-posed global potentials and characterize equilibria in both finite and infinite-player contexts for distributed network optimization.
topics.approach: We extend the theory of potential games to unimodular random graphs of bounded degree. The approach involves characterizing global potentials and Nash equilibria in both finite and infinite-player contexts. We will evaluate the model using a synthetic benchmark dataset to demonstrate its effectiveness in distributed network optimization scenarios.

articles.title: On the equivalence of two approaches to multiplicative homotopy theories
articles.abstract: We study the relation of two frameworks for multiplicative homotopy theories: Presentably symmetric monoidal $\infty$-categories and combinatorial symmetric monoidal model categories. Our main theorem establishes an equivalence of their homotopy theories.   As consequences, we solve Pavlov's conjecture and obtain a solution to a special case of Hovey's 10th problem. We also prove several variations of the main theorem, such as an analog for non-symmetric monoidal semi-model categories.

articles.title: Quasi-unitial Inner Kan Spaces
articles.abstract: We show that semi-simplicial spaces that i) admit inner horn fillers up to homotopy and ii) possess units in a weak sense provide a viable model for $\infty$-categories. The existence of units can be expressed through various quasi-unitality conditions, and we compare the natural generalization of three such conditions found in the literature. This work is motivated by applications in Floer homotopy theory.

articles.title: Kahn-Priddy theorems via the norm
articles.abstract: We revisit the Kahn-Priddy theorem from the perspective of modern equivariant homotopy theory. This allows for a short proof that may be applied in other settings with sufficiently robust analogues of multiplicative norms and the Adams isomorphism. We illustrate this by establishing new Kahn-Priddy theorems in $L_n$ and $L_n^f$-local homotopy theory, motivic homotopy theory, and synthetic homotopy theory.

articles.title: Algebraic models for equivariant rational homotopy theory for discrete groups
articles.abstract: We provide a framework which generalizes algebraic models of a homotopy theory of spaces to the genuine equivariant case for a discrete group. We explain how this applies to commutative differential graded algebra (cdga) models and complete differential graded Lie algebra models for rational spaces. We compare the cdga model to other model categories in the literature.

Using classical computations of Cohen [Coh], we explicitly compute a set of generators for these operations under composition, and show that they agree with the p-fold equivariant cap products previously studied by the author [Che2] in relation to equivariant Gromov-Witten theory with mod p coefficients. For any such functor, we construct a stable $\infty$-category $\mathcal{F}low^μ$ of $μ$-structured flow categories and bimodules. articles.title: On sequential versions of various parametrized invariants
articles.abstract: In this paper, we introduce and study sequential versions of several fibrewise homotopy invariants, including parametrized topological complexity, parametrized (subspace) homotopic distance. We investigate their basic properties, establish relationships among them, and compare them with the corresponding classical homotopical invariants.

articles.abstract: The topology of cosmic reionization, the sizes, shapes, and connectivity of ionized bubbles is a primary observable of next-generation 21\,cm experiments. We show that this topology is sensitive to the microphysics of dark matter. Self-interacting dark matter (SIDM), with cross-sections $σ/m\sim 1$--$10\;\mathrm{cm^2/g}$ motivated by small-scale structure anomalies, reduces halo gas binding energies and increases the duty cycle of ionizing-photon escape. articles.title: Hunting Dark Matter with the Einstein Telescope
articles.abstract: Too light primordial black holes evaporate and are therefore strongly constrained by various bounds, e.g. Cosmic Microwave Background distortion. However, if they are formed strongly clustered, the corresponding haloes may collapse in heavier black holes which may form the entirety of the dark matter of the universe. The indirect signal of such scenario is the production of a flat stochastic background of gravitational waves which is detectable by the Einstein Telescope.

topics.title: Dark Matter Heating and Planetary Spectral Analysis: Identifying Dark Matter Candidates via Thermal Emission
topics.rationale: Recent simulations suggest that a hypothetical ninth planet with a mass of 5-10 Earth masses could induce significant dark matter heating, altering surface temperatures and spectral features. The gap lies in the lack of direct observational data linking these thermal anomalies to specific dark matter candidates like axions or dark matter stars. This research will fill the gap by developing a novel thermal modeling framework to simulate dark matter impact parameters and kinetic energy deposition rates, which will then be applied to analyze the spectral features observed in infrared domains. By integrating the time evolution of the planet and long-term dark matter capture models, we can predict saturation cross-sections and dark matter-ordinary matter interaction rates, providing a robust method to distinguish between black hole, axion, and dark matter star hypotheses.
topics.approach: The study will employ a dark matter heating model coupled with numerical evaluation of the impact parameter to simulate the thermal evolution of the planet. We will calculate the surface temperature and spectral features over long time scales, incorporating the mass and kinetic energy deposition rates derived from the dark matter capture dynamics. Data analysis will focus on the saturation cross-section for dark matter and the ratio of dark matter to ordinary matter, using the predicted spectral features to constrain the dark matter density and time evolution.
datasets: Dark matter impact parameter simulation data

topics.title: Reionization Topology Constraints on Self-Interacting Dark Matter via Cosmic Microwave Background Analysis
topics.rationale: The topology of cosmic reionization, specifically the sizes, shapes, and connectivity of ionized bubbles, serves as a primary observable for next-generation 21cm experiments. However, existing models of dark matter and reionization fail to account for the effects of self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) on halo gas binding energies and ionization morphology. This research addresses the gap by proposing a methodology to analyze the Euler characteristic and duty cycle of reionization bubbles to infer the microphysics of SIDM. By leveraging the sensitivity of these observables to small-scale structure anomalies, we can constrain the cross-sections of self-interacting dark matter particles.
topics.approach: We will utilize halo-by-halo semi-numerical simulations to model the effects of SIDM on halo gas binding energies. The analysis will involve calculating the Euler characteristic and evaluating the duty cycle and emissivity-weighted bias of reionization bubbles. These metrics will be cross-referenced with shot-noise suppression techniques to derive a CMB baryonic uncertainty band, thereby constraining the SIDM cross-sections.
datasets: Duty cycle and emissivity-weighted bias metrics

topics.title: Primordial Black Hole Clustering and Gravitational Wave Detection via the Einstein Telescope
topics.rationale: Existing constraints from primordial black hole (PBH) evaporation, such as those derived from Cosmic Microwave Background distortion, do not account for the scenario where PBHs are strongly clustered and form the entirety of dark matter. This research addresses the gap by investigating the potential for detecting a flat stochastic background of gravitational waves produced by these strongly clustered PBHs. By utilizing the Einstein Telescope, we aim to probe the formation of heavy black holes that constitute the dark matter of the universe, distinguishing them from standard dark matter candidates.
topics.approach: The study will focus on PBH evaporation dynamics and gravitational wave production mechanisms, specifically analyzing the constraints imposed by strong clustering. We will evaluate the Einstein Telescope's sensitivity to the flat stochastic background generated by these clustered PBHs, addressing the limitation of existing evaporation bounds.
datasets: Primordial black hole clustering simulation data

topics.title: Ultralight Dark Matter Interaction Search in the Gotthard Base Tunnel Using Atom Interferometry
topics.rationale: Atom interferometer experiments offer promising prospects for searching for interactions of ultralight bosonic dark matter with Standard Model particles and detecting gravitational waves in frequency bands inaccessible to current experiments. The gap lies in the specific limitations of existing experiments operating or under construction regarding their operational frequency bands. This research addresses the gap by proposing a long-baseline atom interferometer experiment located in the Sedrun Access Shaft to the Gotthard Base Tunnel, a site with unique environmental conditions.
topics.approach: We will conduct an environmental measurement campaign to evaluate ground motion activity and background electromagnetic field quality within the tunnel. The experiment will operate in the specific frequency band inaccessible to existing facilities, utilizing the stable environment of the Gotthard Base Railway Tunnel to enhance signal-to-noise ratios for ultralight bosonic dark matter searches.
datasets: Gotthard base railway tunnel environmental data

topics.title: Ultralight Dark Matter Oscillations in Nuclear Parameters via Precision Nuclear Spectroscopy at JILA
topics.rationale: Ultralight dark matter is expected to induce oscillations in nuclear parameters, characterized by extremely weak couplings or high suppression scales, with the Planck scale serving as a natural benchmark. The gap is the lack of exceptional sensitivity in existing systems to shifts in nuclear energies required to detect these ultralight dark matter effects. This research addresses the gap by proposing a precision nuclear spectroscopy approach at the JILA facility to probe these oscillations at the Mega-Planck scale.
topics.approach: We will utilize precision nuclear spectroscopy methods to search for oscillations in nuclear parameters induced by ultralight dark matter. The experiment will target the Planck scale as a natural benchmark, employing ultrasensitive search techniques and nuclear isomeric transition measurements to detect these subtle energy shifts.

articles.title: Sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of cesium with static magnetic fields
articles.abstract: Laser cooling of alkali atoms typically requires time-varying magnetic fields, introducing unwanted coupling between atom preparation and coherent operations. Here we demonstrate sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of alkali atoms in a fully static magnetic-field configuration. Using a blue-detuned Type-II magneto-optical trap (MOT) operating on the closed $F=3 \rightarrow F'=2$ transition of the D2 line in cesium, we achieve temperatures of 17(1) $μ$K without changing the magnetic-f

articles.title: Hyperfine spectroscopy and laser cooling of the fermionic isotopes $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti
articles.abstract: We report on magneto-optical trapping of the two fermionic isotopes of atomic titanium, $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti. Unlike the even mass-number isotopes, which were recently laser cooled, $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti have nonzero nuclear spins and, consequently, their atomic levels are split by hyperfine structure. Combining and comparing theoretical calculations and atomic beam-spectroscopy measurements, we determine the hyperfine structures and isotope shifts of the $\mathrm{3d^24s^2}$ $\mathrm{a^3F_4

articles.title: Ultracold atoms in a dipole trap in microgravity
articles.abstract: Most cold atoms experiments in microgravity platforms or in Space are achieved using atom chips, leading to limitations in terms of optical access and inhomogeneous magnetic fields. Optical dipole traps do not have these drawbacks but have difficulties producing atomic samples with a large number of atoms at ultra low temperature in the absence of gravity. Here, we report on an efficient evaporative cooling in two-crossed laser beams during parabolic flights. Time-averaged potentials combine the

articles.abstract: We consider a laser cooling and trapping of alkaline-earth and similar atoms in a bichromatic field resonant to a closed optical transition $^1S_0 \to \, ^1P_1$ or $^1S_0 \to \, ^3P_1$. It is shown that new kinetic effects emerge compared to monochromatic fields, enabling the formation of a deep macroscopic trap capable of capturing and cooling neutral atoms to sub-Doppler temperatures. articles.title: Precision spectroscopy of a trapped $^{173}$Yb$^+$ ion using a bath of ultracold atoms
articles.abstract: We demonstrate precision laser spectroscopy of a trapped $^{173}$Yb$^+$ ion that is not directly laser cooled by coupling it to ultracold atoms. The atomic bath continuously cools the internal degrees of freedom of the ion to its hyperfine ground state via spin-exchange collisions. Successful laser excitation is detected via state-selective charge transfer and subsequent ion loss. facts.gap: Existing laser cooling techniques for titanium isotopes with nonzero nuclear spins (like $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti) are not applicable due to hyperfine structure splitting, requiring a new approach to achieve MOTs.

facts.problem: Achieving efficient evaporative cooling of large atomic samples at ultra-low temperatures in microgravity or space environments.
facts.gap: Existing atom chips suffer from limitations in optical access and inhomogeneous magnetic fields, while optical dipole traps lack the ability to produce large numbers of atoms at ultra-low temperatures without gravity.

facts.problem: Laser cooling and trapping of neutral atoms using bichromatic fields to achieve sub-Doppler temperatures and minimize magnetic fields.
facts.gap: Existing macroscopic traps rely on magneto-optical traps which require magnetic fields; this paper introduces a purely optical alternative.

facts.problem: Performing precision laser spectroscopy on trapped ions that are not directly laser cooled, specifically addressing the challenge of achieving high-precision measurements without direct laser cooling.
facts.gap: Existing methods for precision spectroscopy on non-directly laser-cooled ions often lack the precision or require complex setups; this paper improves precision by using spin-exchange collisions to cool the internal degrees of freedom.
methods: magnetic and electric hyperfine interaction constants measurement

topics.title: Development of sub-Doppler cooling protocols for fermionic titanium isotopes with non-zero nuclear spins
topics.rationale: Recent advances in optical transport for cesium demonstrate the feasibility of static magnetic fields, yet titanium isotopes like $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti present a critical gap due to their non-zero nuclear spins causing hyperfine splitting that standard MOTs cannot address. This research is timely as it bridges the gap between established optical cooling techniques for alkali atoms and the specific hyperfine challenges of titanium, offering a pathway to unlock previously inaccessible isotopes for quantum simulation and precision spectroscopy.
topics.approach: We propose a novel two-tone laser cooling scheme utilizing repumping to maximum-spin states and additional optical tones to counteract hyperfine structure splitting. The approach will involve theoretical calculations of the hyperfine interaction constants and optical-pumping transition analysis, followed by experimental validation using magneto-optical trapping and atomic beam-spectroscopy measurements.

topics.title: Optical dipole trap for large atomic samples in microgravity environments
topics.rationale: While optical dipole traps have emerged as a solution to magnetic field limitations in space, they currently struggle to produce large numbers of atoms at ultra-low temperatures due to the lack of gravity-induced evaporative cooling. This topic addresses the critical gap in space-based research where atom chips suffer from optical access issues and inhomogeneous magnetic fields, necessitating a new cooling methodology for future microgravity experiments.
topics.approach: The research will utilize time-averaged potentials and two-crossed laser beams to simulate microgravity conditions and evaluate the phase space density and collision rates of large atomic samples. We will compare the performance of optical dipole traps against existing atom chip technologies to identify optimal parameters for large-sample cooling.

topics.title: Bichromatic field laser cooling for sub-Doppler temperatures in alkaline-earth atoms
topics.rationale: Existing macroscopic traps rely heavily on magneto-optical traps which require magnetic fields, whereas bichromatic fields offer a purely optical alternative that minimizes magnetic field requirements. However, the formation of deep traps and the emergence of new kinetic effects in bichromatic fields remain underexplored, creating an urgent need for optimized protocols to achieve sub-Doppler temperatures.
topics.approach: We will analyze the formation of deep macroscopic traps using bichromatic fields and investigate the emergence of new kinetic effects compared to monochromatic fields. The study will focus on optimizing laser cooling parameters and characterizing the resulting sub-Doppler temperatures through theoretical modeling.

topics.title: Spin-exchange collision cooling of trapped $^{173}$Yb$^+$ ions for high-precision spectroscopy
topics.rationale: Precision laser spectroscopy of trapped ions requires high-precision measurements without direct laser cooling, yet existing methods often lack precision or require complex setups. This gap is critical for applications in fundamental physics and quantum computing, as this paper demonstrates that spin-exchange collisions can effectively cool internal degrees of freedom.
topics.approach: The proposed method involves using ultracold atoms as a bath to continuously cool the internal degrees of freedom of the trapped ion via spin-exchange collisions. We will implement state-selective charge transfer and laser excitation to enhance the efficiency of the cooling process while measuring magnetic and electric hyperfine interaction constants.

results.query: I want to explore experimental and theoretical aspects of quantum sensing and metrology, including atom interferometry, optical clocks, and their applications to tests of fundamental physics and gravitational wave detection

articles.title: Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Matter-Wave Interference
articles.abstract: Operating matter-wave interferometers as quantum detectors for fundamental physics or inertial sensors in real-world applications with unprecedented accuracies relies on noise rejection, often implemented by correlating two sensors. Such sensors can be spatially separated (gradiometry or gravitational-wave detection) or consist of different internal states (magnetometry or quantum clock interferometry), in which case a signal-amplitude modulation may serve as a signature of a differential phase.

articles.title: Fundamental Limits of Quantum Sensors for Gravitational Wave Detection
articles.abstract: Recent advances in quantum sensing -- optical clocks at $5.5\times 10^{-19}$ systematic uncertainty, frequency-dependent squeezing below the standard quantum limit, quantum magnetometers approaching fundamental sensitivity limits -- raise a natural question: can these technologies detect gravitational waves directly, or enhance existing detectors beyond current capabilities? We show that the answer is primarily determined by the \emph{coupling mechanism} between the gravitational wave and the se

articles.title: A fast and accurate method for simulating Bragg atom interferometers
articles.abstract: Atom interferometers are used in a variety of applications, from measuring gravity and gravity gradients in the field to performing tests of fundamental physics in the lab. One method of increasing interferometer sensitivity is to produce a larger momentum difference between interferometer arms through the use of large momentum transfer methods, such as Bragg diffraction. However, Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to char

articles.abstract: We present an analytically tractable model of a driven quantum harmonic emitter, such as an oscillating charged dipole, emitting radiation via resonance fluorescence. With this model we are able to characterize the quantum mechanical correlations that are built up at early times between the drive, the resonant emitter, and its fluorescence. We describe detection strategies that can reveal these quantum signatures in experiments by performing joint measurements on the quantum emitter and its fluo

Quantitative agreement with experimental benchmark results

topics.title: Quantum Noise Mitigation in High-Dimensional Atom Interferometry via Amplitude Collapse
topics.rationale: Recent advances in matter-wave interferometry have highlighted the critical role of noise rejection in achieving unprecedented sensor accuracies. However, existing methods often rely on standard tools that suffer from significant bias reduction, and there is a lack of a generally applicable complementary evaluation method for correlated interferometers without phase stability. This gap necessitates a new approach specifically designed to address the noise rejection challenges in real-world applications.
topics.approach: We propose a novel framework combining targeted fitting methods for different magnetically sensitive substates with a phase estimation from amplitude collapse (PEAC) algorithm. The approach will involve analyzing the correlation of two spatially separated sensors to implement a complementary evaluation method that addresses the 80% bias reduction limitation of current tools.

topics.title: Gravitational Wave Detection via Enhanced Quantum Magnetometer Sensitivity
topics.rationale: Recent breakthroughs in quantum sensing, including optical clocks and frequency-dependent squeezing, have pushed systematic uncertainties down to $5.5	imes 10^{-19}$ levels. However, existing technologies do not yet demonstrate the ability to detect gravitational waves directly or significantly enhance existing detectors beyond current capabilities. This limitation remains a critical barrier for the direct detection of gravitational waves using quantum sensing technologies.
topics.approach: We will investigate the feasibility of integrating quantum magnetometers and optical clocks into a single transducer architecture to achieve combined quantum enhancement. The research will utilize a tidal Hamiltonian and linearized general relativity framework to model the transducer gains and noise budgets within the 0.01--10~Hz band.

topics.title: Simulating Bragg Diffraction Systematic Effects in Atom Interferometers
topics.rationale: Atom interferometers are essential for measuring gravity and fundamental physics, yet Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to characterize. Existing methods often fail to provide a fast and accurate simulation tool capable of handling these complex partial differential equations.
topics.approach: We will develop a fast and accurate simulation method utilizing a 1D-TDSE partial differential equation solver. The approach involves separating the system into ordinary differential equations and applying adaptive step size Runge-Kutta methods to accurately model the Bragg diffraction effects.

topics.title: Quantum Noise Characterization of Driven Harmonic Emitter Radiation
topics.rationale: Driven quantum harmonic emitters, such as oscillating charged dipoles, emit radiation via resonance fluorescence, but existing work lacks an analytically tractable model for these correlations. This limitation prevents the characterization of quantum mechanical correlations built up at early times and the probing of quantum noise in short-time experiments.
topics.approach: We propose an analytically tractable model of a driven quantum harmonic emitter to characterize quantum mechanical correlations between the drive and its fluorescence. The method will utilize joint measurements on the quantum emitter and its fluorescence field to reveal quantum signatures in short-time experiments.

topics.title: Performance Constraints of Large Momentum Transfer in Optical Lattices
topics.rationale: Large-momentum-transfer techniques are instrumental for the next generation of atom interferometers, significantly improving sensitivity through elastic scattering processes from optical lattices. However, existing implementations rely on imperfect pulse efficiencies in these processes, which constrains their overall performance.
topics.approach: We will implement a Floquet-based theoretical framework to analyze the performance constraints of state-of-the-art implementations. The research will focus on addressing the performance constraints of elastic scattering processes, specifically the imperfect pulse efficiencies, using exact numerical solutions of the Schrödinger equation.

articles.abstract: The observed unusual behaviors of the orbits of Trans-Neptunian objects as well as the gravitational anomalies detected by the Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment can be explained by assuming the existence of a ninth planet in the Solar System, having a mass of the order of $5-10M_{\oplus}$, and located at the distance of 300-1000 AU from the Sun. Since no optical counterpart of Planet 9 was observed, it is reasonable to assume that it has a very low luminosity. Various proposals on the nat

articles.title: Reionization Topology as a Probe of Self-Interacting Dark Matter
articles.abstract: The topology of cosmic reionization, the sizes, shapes, and connectivity of ionized bubbles is a primary observable of next-generation 21\,cm experiments. We show that this topology is sensitive to the microphysics of dark matter. Self-interacting dark matter (SIDM), with cross-sections $σ/m\sim 1$--$10\;\mathrm{cm^2/g}$ motivated by small-scale structure anomalies, reduces halo gas binding energies and increases the duty cycle of ionizing-photon escape. articles.title: Hunting Dark Matter with the Einstein Telescope
articles.abstract: Too light primordial black holes evaporate and are therefore strongly constrained by various bounds, e.g. Cosmic Microwave Background distortion. However, if they are formed strongly clustered, the corresponding haloes may collapse in heavier black holes which may form the entirety of the dark matter of the universe. The indirect signal of such scenario is the production of a flat stochastic background of gravitational waves which is detectable by the Einstein Telescope.

articles.title: Constraining axion-like dark matter with a radio-frequency atomic magnetometer
articles.abstract: We report on a broadband search for axion-like-particle (ALP) interactions using a radio-frequency-operated $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$ atomic magnetometer. The instrument provides wide spectral coverage and sensitivity to an oscillating pseudomagnetic field that may be generated by the gradient coupling of the ALP field to the constituent fermions of atoms. We search for an ALP-gradient signature in the mass range $2.40\times10^{-10}\,\mathrm{eV}/c^{2}$--$2.11\times10^{-9}\,\mathrm{eV}/c^{2}$. No statis

articles.title: Cornering MeV-GeV Axions and Dark Photons with LDMX
articles.abstract: Axion-like particles (ALPs), the QCD axion, and dark photons in the MeV-GeV mass range are motivated by various dark matter models and the strong CP problem, and are ubiquitous in extensions of the Standard Model. A long-standing blind spot for experimental searches is the sub-100 MeV mass range, where the particle lifetime is too long to be constrained by prompt-decay collider searches yet too short to be reached by beam-dump experiments. We investigate and estimate the sensitivity of the Light

articles.title: Probing Ultralight Dark Matter at the Mega-Planck Scale with the Thorium Nuclear Clock
articles.abstract: Ultralight dark matter is expected to induce oscillations of nuclear parameters. These oscillations are characterized by extremely weak couplings or high suppression scales, with the Planck scale - the characteristic scale of quantum gravity - serving as a natural benchmark. Probing this phenomenon requires systems with exceptional sensitivity to shifts in nuclear energies. The uniquely low-energy nuclear isomeric transition in ${}^{229}$Th provides such sensitivity: it directly probes the nucle

articles.title: Beyond the Standard Model of Cosmology: Testing new paradigms with a Multiprobe Exploration of the Dark Universe
articles.abstract: Cosmology is living through fascinating times, where new observations from ground and space telescopes are questioning the established paradigm, the so-called Lambda Cold Dark Matter model. The particle nature of Dark Matter is severely constrained by underground experiments, while recent observations by galaxy surveys indicate that the cosmological constant (Lambda) may not be constant after all. Furthermore, observations at high redshift of fully-formed galaxies with massive black holes at the

articles.title: Gravitational wave signatures and periodic orbits of a charged black hole in a Hernquist dark matter halo
articles.abstract: In this work, we study the motion of massive test particles and the gravitational--wave emission associated with periodic trajectories around a magnetically charged black hole immersed in a \textit{Hernquist} dark matter halo. We begin by analyzing the effective potential and the conditions for stable motion, with particular attention to the marginally bound radius and the innermost stable circular orbit. Our results show that the dark matter parameters, namely the halo density and scale radius,

articles.title: Shadow, Quasinormal Modes, Sparsity, and Energy Emission Rate of Euler-Heisenberg Black Hole Surrounded by Perfect Fluid Dark Matter
articles.abstract: In this work, we investigate the optical, dynamical, and radiative properties of an Euler--Heisenberg black hole immersed in a perfect fluid dark matter (PFDM) background. We analyze the photon sphere and shadow, the scalar quasinormal-mode spectrum in the eikonal regime, the grey-body factor through the eikonal QNM correspondence, the sparsity of Hawking radiation, and the corresponding energy emission rate. articles_with_facts.title: Cornering MeV-GeV Axions and Dark Photons with LDMX
articles_with_facts.abstract: Axion-like particles (ALPs), the QCD axion, and dark photons in the MeV-GeV mass range are motivated by various dark matter models and the strong CP problem, and are ubiquitous in extensions of the Standard Model. A long-standing blind spot for experimental searches is the sub-100 MeV mass range, where the particle lifetime is too long to be constrained by prompt-decay collider searches yet too short to be reached by beam-dump experiments. We investigate and estimate the sensitivity of the Light

facts.problem: The sub-100 MeV mass range for axion-like particles (ALPs) and dark photons presents a 'blind spot' for experimental searches because particle lifetimes are too long for prompt-decay collider searches and too short for beam-dump experiments.
facts.gap: Existing searches have a blind spot in the sub-100 MeV mass range due to the conflict between particle lifetimes and experimental detection capabilities.

articles_with_facts.title: Probing Ultralight Dark Matter at the Mega-Planck Scale with the Thorium Nuclear Clock
articles_with_facts.abstract: Ultralight dark matter is expected to induce oscillations of nuclear parameters. These oscillations are characterized by extremely weak couplings or high suppression scales, with the Planck scale - the characteristic scale of quantum gravity - serving as a natural benchmark. Probing this phenomenon requires systems with exceptional sensitivity to shifts in nuclear energies. The uniquely low-energy nuclear isomeric transition in ${}^{229}$Th provides such sensitivity: it directly probes the nucle

facts.problem: Probing the existence of ultralight dark matter, which induces oscillations in nuclear parameters with extremely weak couplings or high suppression scales.
facts.gap: Existing methods lack the exceptional sensitivity to shifts in nuclear energies required to detect these weak couplings or high suppression scales.

articles_with_facts.title: Beyond the Standard Model of Cosmology: Testing new paradigms with a Multiprobe Exploration of the Dark Universe
articles_with_facts.abstract: Cosmology is living through fascinating times, where new observations from ground and space telescopes are questioning the established paradigm, the so-called Lambda Cold Dark Matter model. The particle nature of Dark Matter is severely constrained by underground experiments, while recent observations by galaxy surveys indicate that the cosmological constant (Lambda) may not be constant after all. Furthermore, observations at high redshift of fully-formed galaxies with massive black holes at the

facts.problem: The Lambda Cold Dark Matter (LambdaCDM) paradigm is being questioned by new observations from ground and space telescopes, galaxy surveys, and gravitational wave detectors, which challenge the established understanding of Dark Matter and Dark Energy.
facts.gap: Existing models rely on new particles or new degrees of freedom to explain Dark Matter and Dark Energy, but the paper proposes a unified explanation based on known physics without requiring new particles or degrees of freedom.
methods: Primordial Black Hole scenario for Dark Matter
Non-equilibrium thermodynamics in the expanding universe
Statistical properties of the primordial spectrum of fluctuations
metrics: Observations from ground and space telescopes

articles_with_facts.title: Gravitational wave signatures and periodic orbits of a charged black hole in a Hernquist dark matter halo
articles_with_facts.abstract: In this work, we study the motion of massive test particles and the gravitational--wave emission associated with periodic trajectories around a magnetically charged black hole immersed in a \textit{Hernquist} dark matter halo. We begin by analyzing the effective potential and the conditions for stable motion, with particular attention to the marginally bound radius and the innermost stable circular orbit. Our results show that the dark matter parameters, namely the halo density and scale radius,

facts.problem: The motion of massive test particles and gravitational-wave emission around a magnetically charged black hole in a Hernquist dark matter halo.
facts.gap: Existing work likely lacks a comprehensive analysis of the interplay between dark matter parameters, magnetic charge, and the resulting stable orbits and gravitational-wave signatures in this specific magnetized dark matter environment.
methods: Study of conditions for stable motion
Investigation of innermost stable circular orbit

articles_with_facts.title: Shadow, Quasinormal Modes, Sparsity, and Energy Emission Rate of Euler-Heisenberg Black Hole Surrounded by Perfect Fluid Dark Matter
articles_with_facts.abstract: In this work, we investigate the optical, dynamical, and radiative properties of an Euler--Heisenberg black hole immersed in a perfect fluid dark matter (PFDM) background. We analyze the photon sphere and shadow, the scalar quasinormal-mode spectrum in the eikonal regime, the grey-body factor through the eikonal QNM correspondence, the sparsity of Hawking radiation, and the corresponding energy emission rate. Our results show that both the black-hole charge and the PFDM parameter significantly a

facts.problem: The optical, dynamical, and radiative properties of an Euler--Heisenberg black hole immersed in a perfect fluid dark matter (PFDM) background.
facts.gap: The paper investigates how the Euler--Heisenberg correction and dark matter environment affect black hole properties like photon sphere, shadow, quasinormal modes, and Hawking radiation, specifically addressing the subleading nature of the correction and the dominant imprint of dark matter.

topics.title: Evaluating Dark Matter Heating Models via Spectral Features of Trans-Neptunian Objects
topics.rationale: Recent work on Planet 9 suggests that dark matter heating could explain observed orbital anomalies and spectral features in TNOs, yet the specific relationship between impact parameter, kinetic energy deposition rates, and surface temperature remains unquantified. This topic addresses the gap by proposing a quantitative analysis of spectral features to distinguish between axion, black hole, and dark matter star hypotheses. The approach involves simulating dark matter impact parameters and kinetic energy deposition rates to generate synthetic spectral data, which will be compared against observational data from infrared domain wavelength estimations. This study will utilize a domain-specific evaluation corpus of TNO spectral features to derive constraints on the dark matter density and interaction cross-sections. By analyzing the time evolution of the planet and comparing it against numerical evaluations, we aim to resolve the nature of the hypothetical ninth planet.
topics.approach: We will employ a dark matter heating model to simulate the time evolution of the Planet 9 system, calculating the mass and kinetic energy deposition rates based on the impact parameter and surface temperature. Synthetic spectral features will be generated using the estimated dark matter density and interaction cross-sections, then compared against the observed infrared domain wavelengths and spectral features of TNOs. Statistical analysis will be performed to determine the ratio of dark matter to ordinary matter and the saturation cross-section, while the mass and kinetic energy deposition rates will be estimated from the time evolution parameters.
datasets: Synthetic Dark Matter Heating Simulation Data

topics.title: Topology-Dependent Dark Matter Constraints from Reionization Bubble Morphology
topics.rationale: Existing models of dark matter and reionization fail to account for the specific effects of self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) on halo gas binding energies and ionizing-photon escape duty cycles, which alter the topology of cosmic reionization. This research addresses the gap by leveraging the sensitivity of reionization topology to SIDM microphysics to derive constraints on dark matter properties. The approach involves performing halo-by-halo semi-numerical simulations to model the blowout model and velocity-dependent SIDM analysis, which will modify the ISM column density distribution and source population morphology. We will analyze the Euler characteristic and shot-noise metrics to detect bias shifts caused by SIDM, providing a novel probe for dark matter self-interaction cross-sections.
topics.approach: We will conduct halo-by-halo semi-numerical simulations to model the blowout model and velocity-dependent SIDM analysis, which will modify the ISM column density distribution and source population morphology. The approach will involve calculating the Euler characteristic and shot-noise metrics from the modified halo structures to detect bias shifts caused by SIDM. By comparing these metrics against the expected CMB baryonic uncertainty band, we will derive constraints on the self-interacting dark matter cross-section and detection thresholds.

topics.title: Primordial Black Hole Clustering Constraints from the Gravitational Wave Background
topics.rationale: Existing constraints from primordial black hole evaporation, such as those derived from Cosmic Microwave Background distortion, do not account for the scenario where these black holes are strongly clustered, potentially forming the majority of the dark matter. This topic addresses the gap by investigating the detection of a flat stochastic background of gravitational waves produced by clustered primordial black holes. The approach involves analyzing the gravitational wave production mechanisms and evaluating the Einstein Telescope's sensitivity to this background, which is otherwise strongly constrained by evaporation bounds.
topics.approach: We will analyze the gravitational wave production mechanisms associated with primordial black hole evaporation and clustering scenarios. The approach will involve evaluating the Einstein Telescope's sensitivity to the flat stochastic background of gravitational waves produced by these clustered black holes, distinguishing them from the evaporation constraints.

topics.title: Ultralight Dark Matter Detection via Ground Motion Activity and Background Electromagnetic Field Quality
topics.rationale: Existing experiments operating or under construction cannot operate in the specific frequency band inaccessible to current experiments, creating a critical gap for searching ultralight bosonic dark matter. This research addresses the gap by proposing a long-baseline atom interferometer experiment in the Gotthard Base Tunnel, a promising site for environmental measurements. The approach involves conducting an environmental measurement campaign to evaluate ground motion activity and background electromagnetic field quality, which are critical metrics for the experiment's success.
topics.approach: We will conduct an environmental measurement campaign to evaluate ground motion activity and background electromagnetic field quality within the Gotthard Base Tunnel. The approach will involve setting up an 800-m atom interferometer experiment to search for gravitational waves in a frequency band inaccessible to current experiments, using the tunnel's environmental conditions as a key validation metric.

topics.title: Nuclear Isomer Transitions as a Probe for Supersymmetric Dark Matter
topics.rationale: While supersymmetric dark matter has been extensively studied, the specific implications for nuclear isomer transitions remain underexplored, representing a significant gap in our understanding of dark matter-nucleus interactions. This research addresses the gap by investigating how supersymmetric dark matter could affect nuclear isomer transitions, providing a novel experimental avenue for dark matter detection. The approach involves analyzing the nuclear structure of the isomer and its implications for dark matter-nucleus interactions.
topics.approach: We will analyze the nuclear structure of the isomer and its implications for dark matter-nucleus interactions. The approach will involve investigating how supersymmetric dark matter could affect nuclear isomer transitions, providing a novel experimental avenue for dark matter detection.

topics.title: Dark Matter Constraints from the Cosmic X-ray Background and Solar Wind Interactions
topics.rationale: The Cosmic X-ray Background (CXB) and solar wind interactions have been extensively studied for dark matter constraints, but the specific contribution of dark matter to the CXB and the detailed mechanism of solar wind interaction with dark matter remain underexplored, creating a gap in our understanding of dark matter's cosmological impact. This research addresses the gap by investigating the specific contribution of dark matter to the CXB and the detailed mechanism of solar wind interaction with dark matter.
topics.approach: We will investigate the specific contribution of dark matter to the Cosmic X-ray Background and the detailed mechanism of solar wind interaction with dark matter. The approach will involve analyzing the CXB and solar wind interactions to derive constraints on dark matter properties.

articles.title: Standalone optical frequency-offset locking electronics for atomic physics
articles.abstract: We present a standalone frequency-offset locking system for controlling narrow-linewidth lasers using off-the-shelf electronic components. We lock two frequency-doubled 1560 nm lasers to a stable primary laser operating at 780 nm via their optical beat note. This radio-frequency beat note is fed through a broadband variable divider, a frequency-to-voltage converter, and a proportional-integrator controller to lock each follower laser to a tunable offset frequency relative to the primary. This ar

articles.title: Rapid axial loading of a grating MOT with a cold-atom beam
articles.abstract: Laser-cooled atoms are increasingly being used to realise practical quantum devices, motivating the development of compact and robust atom sources. Grating magneto-optical traps (gMOTs) simplify the cold-atom source architecture but are typically vapour-loaded and provide limited atomic flux. Here we explore the loading of gMOTs from cold-atom beams. We numerically simulate loading to show that unbalanced diffracted beams deflect incoming atoms away from the trap centre, thereby strongly constra

articles.title: Sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of cesium with static magnetic fields
articles.abstract: Laser cooling of alkali atoms typically requires time-varying magnetic fields, introducing unwanted coupling between atom preparation and coherent operations. Here we demonstrate sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of alkali atoms in a fully static magnetic-field configuration. Using a blue-detuned Type-II magneto-optical trap (MOT) operating on the closed $F=3 \rightarrow F'=2$ transition of the D2 line in cesium, we achieve temperatures of 17(1) $μ$K without changing the magnetic-f

articles.title: Hyperfine spectroscopy and laser cooling of the fermionic isotopes $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti
articles.abstract: We report on magneto-optical trapping of the two fermionic isotopes of atomic titanium, $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti. Unlike the even mass-number isotopes, which were recently laser cooled, $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti have nonzero nuclear spins and, consequently, their atomic levels are split by hyperfine structure. Combining and comparing theoretical calculations and atomic beam-spectroscopy measurements, we determine the hyperfine structures and isotope shifts of the $\mathrm{3d^24s^2}$ $\mathrm{a^3F_4

articles.title: Ultracold atoms in a dipole trap in microgravity
articles.abstract: Most cold atoms experiments in microgravity platforms or in Space are achieved using atom chips, leading to limitations in terms of optical access and inhomogeneous magnetic fields. Optical dipole traps do not have these drawbacks but have difficulties producing atomic samples with a large number of atoms at ultra low temperature in the absence of gravity. Here, we report on an efficient evaporative cooling in two-crossed laser beams during parabolic flights. Time-averaged potentials combine the

articles.title: Purely optical macroscopic trap for alkaline-earth and similar atoms
articles.abstract: We consider a laser cooling and trapping of alkaline-earth and similar atoms in a bichromatic field resonant to a closed optical transition $^1S_0 \to \, ^1P_1$ or $^1S_0 \to \, ^3P_1$. It is shown that new kinetic effects emerge compared to monochromatic fields, enabling the formation of a deep macroscopic trap capable of capturing and cooling neutral atoms to sub-Doppler temperatures. Such a purely optical macroscopic trap can serve as an alternative to the well-known magneto-optical trap and

articles.title: Microscopic Rydberg electron orbit manipulation with optical tweezers
articles.abstract: Laser cooling and trapping of atomic matter waves in optical potentials has enabled rapid progress in quantum science, particularly when combined with Rydberg excitation of the atoms to induce long-range interactions. Here, we propose the local manipulation and spatio-temporal sculpting of the electronic matter wave of a Rydberg atom by a laser field focused so that its beam width is smaller than the Rydberg electron orbit. We compute the electronic eigenstates in the presence of a sharply focus

articles.title: Precision spectroscopy of a trapped $^{173}$Yb$^+$ ion using a bath of ultracold atoms
articles.abstract: We demonstrate precision laser spectroscopy of a trapped $^{173}$Yb$^+$ ion that is not directly laser cooled by coupling it to ultracold atoms. The atomic bath continuously cools the internal degrees of freedom of the ion to its hyperfine ground state via spin-exchange collisions. Successful laser excitation is detected via state-selective charge transfer and subsequent ion loss. We probe the $6^2S_{1/2}\rightarrow 6^2P_{3/2}$ transition at 329 nm and measure the magnetic and electric hyperfine

articles.title: Effect of dipole interactions on the properties of an expanding ultracold plasma: A study using quantum mechanical scattering theory
articles.abstract: While generating ultracold plasma (UCP) by photoinization of laser-cooled atoms, only a small fraction of atoms are ionized, and the remaining neutrals interact with the electrons present therein. These interactions, in addition to the Coulomb interactions between ions and electrons, cause phenomena such as ionization of Rydberg atoms and three body recombination, all of which affect the overall behaviour of the ultracold plasma. We had earlier developed a quantum treatment to analyze these inte

articles.title: High-optical-depth, sub-Doppler-width absorption lines at telecom wavelengths in hot, optically driven rubidium vapor
articles.abstract: Doppler broadening presents a major limitation for high-resolution spectroscopy and nonlinear optics in room-temperature atomic vapors. Here, we demonstrate the suppression of Doppler broadening accompanied by pronounced absorption on the upper transition of a three-level ladder system, achieved by dressing the intermediate state with a strong control field. As a concrete realization, we study a hot vapor of $^{87}$Rb where the lower transition is driven by a strong control field resonant with t

We lock two frequency-doubled 1560 nm lasers to a stable primary laser operating at 780 nm via their optical beat note. This radio-frequency beat note is fed through a broadband variable divider, a frequency-to-voltage converter, and a proportional-integrator controller to lock each follower laser to a tunable offset frequency relative to the primary. facts.problem: Controlling narrow-linewidth lasers with high precision and stability, particularly without requiring a dedicated, precise clock reference.
facts.gap: Existing systems often require a dedicated, precise clock reference for frequency locking, whereas this paper aims to achieve high resolution and stability without such a reference.

Grating magneto-optical traps (gMOTs) simplify the cold-atom source architecture but are typically vapour-loaded and provide limited atomic flux. Here we explore the loading of gMOTs from cold-atom beams. facts.problem: The development of compact and robust atom sources for practical quantum devices, specifically addressing the limitations of vapour-loaded grating magneto-optical traps (gMOTs) which provide limited atomic flux.
facts.gap: Existing gMOTs are typically vapour-loaded and offer limited atomic flux, constraining the development of portable and high-flux cold-atom systems.

Here we demonstrate sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of alkali atoms in a fully static magnetic-field configuration. Using a blue-detuned Type-II magneto-optical trap (MOT) operating on the closed $F=3 \rightarrow F'=2$ transition of the D2 line in cesium, we achieve temperatures of 17(1) $μ$K without changing the magnetic-f

facts.problem: Producing magneto-optical traps (MOTs) for atomic isotopes with nonzero nuclear spins, specifically $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti, which are difficult due to hyperfine structure and lack of prior laser cooling.
facts.gap: Existing even mass-number isotopes of titanium were laser cooled, but the odd mass-number isotopes ($^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti) have nonzero nuclear spins causing hyperfine splitting, preventing their laser cooling.

atomic flux loading from titanium sublimation pump

Optical dipole traps do not have these drawbacks but have difficulties producing atomic samples with a large number of atoms at ultra low temperature in the absence of gravity. facts.problem: Achieving efficient evaporative cooling of atomic samples at ultra-low temperatures in microgravity environments (parabolic flights) to overcome limitations of atom chips.
facts.gap: Existing methods using atom chips suffer from limitations in optical access and inhomogeneous magnetic fields, while optical dipole traps lack the ability to produce large numbers of atoms at ultra-low temperatures without gravity.

facts.problem: Laser cooling and trapping of neutral atoms using bichromatic fields to achieve sub-Doppler temperatures and minimize magnetic fields.
facts.gap: Existing macroscopic traps rely on magneto-optical traps which require magnetic fields; this paper introduces a purely optical alternative to achieve deeper traps and lower temperatures.

facts.problem: Controlling the electronic matter wave of Rydberg atoms to induce long-range interactions and manipulate their dipole moments.
facts.gap: Existing methods for Rydberg atom manipulation often lack the ability to sculpt the electronic wavefunction with high bandwidth and spatial-temporal precision on sub-orbital length scales.
methods: Laser field focused to smaller beam width than the Rydberg electron orbit
Spatio-temporal sculpting of the electronic matter wave
Computing electronic eigenstates in the presence of a sharply focused Gaussian laser beam
Modulation of dipole moments with high bandwidth controlled by local tweezer intensity
Ponderomotive forces acting on sub-orbital length scales

facts.problem: Performing precision laser spectroscopy on trapped ions that are not directly laser cooled, specifically overcoming the challenge of continuous cooling via spin-exchange collisions while maintaining state-selective excitation.
facts.gap: Existing methods for precision spectroscopy on non-directly laser-cooled ions often lack the ability to continuously cool the internal degrees of freedom to the hyperfine ground state while still achieving state-selective detection.
methods: magnetic and electric hyperfine interaction constants measurement

articles_with_facts.title: Effect of dipole interactions on the properties of an expanding ultracold plasma: A study using quantum mechanical scattering theory
