These interactions, in addition to the Coulomb interactions between ions and electrons, cause phenomena such as ionization of Rydberg atoms and three body recombination, all of which affect the overall behaviour of the ultracold plasma. We had earlier developed a quantum treatment to analyze these inte

facts.problem: Understanding the behavior of ultracold plasmas (UCP), specifically the ionization of Rydberg atoms, three-body recombination, and the resulting quantum pressure effects that affect expansion.
facts.gap: Existing quantum treatments only analyzed ionization of Rydberg atoms in Cesium and did not extend to other atomic species or fully account for three-body recombination and the associated quantum pressure.

articles_with_facts.title: High-optical-depth, sub-Doppler-width absorption lines at telecom wavelengths in hot, optically driven rubidium vapor
articles_with_facts.abstract: Doppler broadening presents a major limitation for high-resolution spectroscopy and nonlinear optics in room-temperature atomic vapors. Here, we demonstrate the suppression of Doppler broadening accompanied by pronounced absorption on the upper transition of a three-level ladder system, achieved by dressing the intermediate state with a strong control field. As a concrete realization, we study a hot vapor of $^{87}$Rb where the lower transition is driven by a strong control field resonant with t

facts.problem: High-resolution spectroscopy and nonlinear optics in room-temperature atomic vapors are limited by Doppler broadening.
facts.gap: Combining high optical density with sub-Doppler-width absorption lines typically requires laser-cooled atoms, which is not feasible for room-temperature vapors.
methods: strong control field resonant with D2 line

topics.title: Optical Frequency-Offset Locking of Cold Rubidium for Atomic Clock Calibration
topics.rationale: Recent advances in standalone optical frequency-offset locking electronics have demonstrated high precision without dedicated clock references, yet their application to cold atomic systems remains underexplored. This gap limits the portability of atomic clocks in portable quantum devices. The proposed research will evaluate the stability of cold 87Rb atoms under this new locking architecture, providing a direct comparison with traditional clock references.
topics.approach: We will implement a standalone frequency-offset locking system using off-the-shelf electronic components and optical beat notes to lock two 1560 nm lasers to a stable primium frequency. We will measure the frequency resolution and short-term fractional frequency instability of the cold 87Rb atoms using this new setup, comparing it against standard reference systems.

topics.title: Compact Grating Magneto-Optical Trap for Portable Quantum Devices
topics.rationale: Existing grating magneto-optical traps (gMOTs) are limited by their vapour-loaded nature and low atomic flux, which constrains the development of portable, high-flux cold-atom systems for practical quantum devices. This paper highlights the need for robust, compact atom sources that do not rely on traditional vapour loading. Our study will simulate and experimentally demonstrate the performance of a new gMOT design to address these limitations.
topics.approach: We will perform numerical simulations and experimental demonstrations of a moving optical molasses and 2D MOT configuration to optimize the loading rates. The system will be tested to evaluate its compactness and robustness, aiming to increase atomic flux significantly compared to standard gMOTs.

topics.title: Sub-Doppler Laser Cooling and Optical Transport of Cesium in Static Magnetic Fields
topics.rationale: Laser cooling of alkali atoms typically requires time-varying magnetic fields, which introduces unwanted coupling between atom preparation and coherent operations. This constraint hinders the development of advanced quantum devices that require stable, static magnetic environments. We will investigate the feasibility of sub-Doppler laser cooling in static magnetic fields to overcome this limitation.
topics.approach: We will demonstrate sub-Doppler laser cooling of cesium atoms using static magnetic fields, minimizing unwanted coupling. We will also analyze the optical transport properties of the resulting atomic beams to ensure efficient operation in static environments.

topics.title: Magneto-Optical Trapping of Titanium Isotopes with Nonzero Nuclear Spins
topics.rationale: Producing magneto-optical traps for atomic isotopes with nonzero nuclear spins, specifically 47Ti and 49Ti, is challenging due to hyperfine structure and lack of prior laser cooling. Existing even mass-number isotopes were laser cooled, but the odd mass-number isotopes present significant hurdles. This research aims to overcome these hyperfine structure barriers.
topics.approach: We will utilize magneto-optical trapping techniques combined with atomic beam-spectroscopy measurements to trap 47Ti and 49Ti isotopes. We will employ theoretical calculations and hyperfine structure analysis to understand the trapping dynamics and isotope shift effects.

topics.title: Optical Dipole Traps for Ultracold Atoms in Microgravity Environments
topics.rationale: Achieving efficient evaporative cooling of atomic samples at ultra-low temperatures in microgravity environments is critical for overcoming the limitations of atom chips, which suffer from optical access and inhomogeneous magnetic field issues. Existing methods using atom chips lack the ability to produce large numbers of atoms at ultra-low temperatures without gravity. This research will explore optical dipole traps as a viable alternative.
topics.approach: We will implement two-crossed laser beams and evaporative cooling protocols to generate ultracold atoms in a dipole trap. We will test these systems in parabolic flights to evaluate their performance in microgravity, focusing on initial phase space density and collision rates.

results.query: I want to explore experimental and theoretical aspects of quantum sensing and metrology, including atom interferometry, optical clocks, and their applications to tests of fundamental physics and gravitational wave detection

articles.title: Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Matter-Wave Interference
articles.abstract: Operating matter-wave interferometers as quantum detectors for fundamental physics or inertial sensors in real-world applications with unprecedented accuracies relies on noise rejection, often implemented by correlating two sensors. Such sensors can be spatially separated (gradiometry or gravitational-wave detection) or consist of different internal states (magnetometry or quantum clock interferometry), in which case a signal-amplitude modulation may serve as a signature of a differential phase.

articles.title: A fast and accurate method for simulating Bragg atom interferometers
articles.abstract: Atom interferometers are used in a variety of applications, from measuring gravity and gravity gradients in the field to performing tests of fundamental physics in the lab. One method of increasing interferometer sensitivity is to produce a larger momentum difference between interferometer arms through the use of large momentum transfer methods, such as Bragg diffraction. However, Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to char

articles.title: In-orbit Test of the Weak Equivalence Principle with Atom Interferometry
articles.abstract: The Weak Equivalence Principle (WEP) is a central pillar of general relativity. Its precise test with quantum systems in space offers a unique window onto new physics. Here we report the first in-orbit quantum test of the WEP. A dual-species (85Rb/87Rb) atom interferometer is realized aboard the China Space Station. Methods of platform motion suppression, fluorescence detection switching, and two-photon detuning switching are developed to eliminate phase noise and improve measurement accuracy. A

articles.title: Fundamental Limits of Large Momentum Transfer in Optical Lattices
articles.abstract: Large-momentum-transfer techniques are instrumental for the next generation of atom interferometers as they significantly improve their sensitivity. State-of-the-art implementations rely on elastic scattering processes from optical lattices such as Bloch oscillations or sequential Bragg diffraction, but their performance is constrained by imperfect pulse efficiencies. Here we develop a Floquet-based theoretical framework that provides a unified description of elastic light-atom scattering across

articles.title: Simple broadband signal detection at the fundamental limit
articles.abstract: Broadband detection of a weak oscillatory field with unknown carrier frequency underlies magnetometry, axion searches and gravitational-wave sensing. We show that the Grover-like integration-time lower bound for this task is a geometric corollary an upper bound on the integrated quantum Fisher information, a metrological constraint. We further give an all-analog multi-resonant protocol based on a randomized Su-Schrieffer-Heeger control Hamiltonian and an m-register GHZ probe and verify near-opti

articles.title: Nuclear Heterodyne Interferometry for Gravitational Spectroscopy
articles.abstract: Gravitational spectroscopy tests the coupling of gravity to matter by measuring gravitationally induced frequency shifts of quantum transitions. While modern optical clocks probe the gravitational response of electronic transitions with extraordinary precision, tests in the nuclear sector have not progressed since the Mössbauer measurements of the gravitational redshift by Pound and Rebka. Here we introduce a new approach to nuclear gravitational spectroscopy based on phase-sensitive heterodyne

articles.title: Atom-light hybrid interferometer for atomic sensing with quantum memory
articles.abstract: Quantum memories feature a reversible conversion of optical fields into long-lived atomic spin waves, and are therefore ideal for operating as sensitive atomic sensors. However, up to now, atom-light interferometers have lacked an efficient approach to exploit their ultimate atomic sensing performance, since an extra optical delay line is required to compensate for the memory time. Here, we report a new protocol that records the photocurrent via heterodyne mixing with a stable local oscillator.

articles.title: Environmental Measurements in the Sedrun Access Shaft to the Gotthard Base Tunnel -- a Promising Site for a Long-Baseline Atom Interferometer
articles.abstract: Atom interferometer (AI) experiments offer interesting prospects for searches for the interactions of ultralight bosonic dark matter with Standard Model particles as well as detection of gravitational waves in a frequency band inaccessible to experiments that are operating or under construction. Ideal locations for the next generation of such experiments are provided by long vertical shafts, such as that providing access to the Gotthard base railway tunnel from the Sedrun locality in the Canton

articles.title: Mirror Surface Evaluation for the Einstein Telescope Using Virtual Mirror Maps
articles.abstract: The performance of mirrors in optical interferometers is critically influenced by their surface quality. Accurate metrology enables mirror surfaces to be characterized through phase maps describing their three-dimensional structure after coating. In this work, we combine Zernike polynomial decomposition and spatial frequency (PSD) analysis with numerical optical simulations to quantify the impact of surface distortions on the reflected optical field. The method is validated using metrology data

articles.title: In-Situ Differential-Light-Shift Cancellation for Trapped-Atom Clocks
articles.abstract: Differential light shifts (DLS) induced by optical trapping fields fundamentally limit the stability and accuracy of trapped-atom microwave clocks. We demonstrate an in-situ method to cancel DLS by simultaneously interrogating multiple spatially separated atomic ensembles at different trap intensities generated from a common light source. By operating the ensembles at set intensity ratios and performing Ramsey spectroscopy, the intensity-dependent frequency shifts are measured within each experi

articles_with_facts.title: Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Matter-Wave Interference
articles_with_facts.abstract: Operating matter-wave interferometers as quantum detectors for fundamental physics or inertial sensors in real-world applications with unprecedented accuracies relies on noise rejection, often implemented by correlating two sensors. Such sensors can be spatially separated (gradiometry or gravitational-wave detection) or consist of different internal states (magnetometry or quantum clock interferometry), in which case a signal-amplitude modulation may serve as a signature of a differential phase.

facts.problem: Achieving high-precision quantum detectors for fundamental physics and inertial sensors by effectively rejecting noise in real-world applications.
facts.gap: Existing methods for noise rejection often rely on correlating two sensors, which can be spatially separated or involve different internal states. Prior claims of optimal operation at vanishing amplitude are contradicted by the work, and there is a need for a generally applicable complementary evaluation method for correlated interferometers without phase stability.

articles_with_facts.abstract: Atom interferometers are used in a variety of applications, from measuring gravity and gravity gradients in the field to performing tests of fundamental physics in the lab. One method of increasing interferometer sensitivity is to produce a larger momentum difference between interferometer arms through the use of large momentum transfer methods, such as Bragg diffraction. However, Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to char

facts.problem: Characterizing systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase caused by Bragg diffraction in atom interferometers.
facts.gap: Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to characterize.
methods: separation into systems of ordinary differential equations

articles_with_facts.title: In-orbit Test of the Weak Equivalence Principle with Atom Interferometry
articles_with_facts.abstract: The Weak Equivalence Principle (WEP) is a central pillar of general relativity. Its precise test with quantum systems in space offers a unique window onto new physics. Here we report the first in-orbit quantum test of the WEP. A dual-species (85Rb/87Rb) atom interferometer is realized aboard the China Space Station. Methods of platform motion suppression, fluorescence detection switching, and two-photon detuning switching are developed to eliminate phase noise and improve measurement accuracy. A

facts.problem: Testing the Weak Equivalence Principle (WEP) using quantum systems in space to probe new physics.
facts.gap: Prior atom-interferometric WEP tests in microgravity were limited by phase noise, resulting in a test uncertainty three orders of magnitude worse than the current in-orbit test.

articles_with_facts.abstract: Large-momentum-transfer techniques are instrumental for the next generation of atom interferometers as they significantly improve their sensitivity. State-of-the-art implementations rely on elastic scattering processes from optical lattices such as Bloch oscillations or sequential Bragg diffraction, but their performance is constrained by imperfect pulse efficiencies. Here we develop a Floquet-based theoretical framework that provides a unified description of elastic light-atom scattering across

facts.problem: Improving the sensitivity of atom interferometers by addressing the performance constraints of state-of-the-art implementations, specifically the limitations imposed by imperfect pulse efficiencies in elastic scattering processes.
facts.gap: Existing implementations rely on elastic scattering processes (e.g., Bloch oscillations, sequential Bragg diffraction) that are constrained by imperfect pulse efficiencies, leading to reduced losses and phase accuracy.

articles_with_facts.title: Simple broadband signal detection at the fundamental limit
articles_with_facts.abstract: Broadband detection of a weak oscillatory field with unknown carrier frequency underlies magnetometry, axion searches and gravitational-wave sensing. We show that the Grover-like integration-time lower bound for this task is a geometric corollary an upper bound on the integrated quantum Fisher information, a metrological constraint. articles_with_facts.abstract: Gravitational spectroscopy tests the coupling of gravity to matter by measuring gravitationally induced frequency shifts of quantum transitions. While modern optical clocks probe the gravitational response of electronic transitions with extraordinary precision, tests in the nuclear sector have not progressed since the Mössbauer measurements of the gravitational redshift by Pound and Rebka. Here we introduce a new approach to nuclear gravitational spectroscopy based on phase-sensitive heterodyne

facts.problem: Testing the coupling of gravity to matter in the nuclear sector, specifically measuring the gravitational redshift of nuclear resonant scattering.
facts.gap: Nuclear sector gravitational spectroscopy has not progressed since the Mössbauer measurements of the gravitational redshift by Pound and Rebka, despite modern optical clocks probing electronic transitions with extraordinary precision.

articles_with_facts.title: Atom-light hybrid interferometer for atomic sensing with quantum memory
articles_with_facts.abstract: Quantum memories feature a reversible conversion of optical fields into long-lived atomic spin waves, and are therefore ideal for operating as sensitive atomic sensors. However, up to now, atom-light interferometers have lacked an efficient approach to exploit their ultimate atomic sensing performance, since an extra optical delay line is required to compensate for the memory time. Here, we report a new protocol that records the photocurrent via heterodyne mixing with a stable local oscillator.

facts.problem: Atom-light interferometers lack an efficient approach to exploit their ultimate atomic sensing performance due to the need for an extra optical delay line to compensate for memory time.
facts.gap: Existing methods require an extra optical delay line to compensate for the memory time, which is inefficient.

topics.title: Optimizing Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Atom Interferometers for Inertial Sensors
topics.rationale: Existing methods for noise rejection in correlated matter-wave interferometers often rely on spatially separated sensors or different internal states, which contradicts the goal of a generally applicable complementary evaluation method. The paper highlights the need for a generally applicable complementary evaluation method for correlated interferometers without phase stability, suggesting that current approaches fail to address the specific challenges of noise rejection in real-world applications. This gap indicates a critical opportunity to develop a new framework that unifies the evaluation of phase estimation in correlated systems. By addressing the limitations of prior claims of optimal operation at vanishing amplitude, this research could provide a robust solution for practical inertial sensing applications.
topics.approach: We propose a novel adaptive fitting method tailored for different magnetically sensitive substates of an atom interferometer, combined with a comprehensive noise rejection framework. The approach will involve targeting specific substates to minimize cross-talk and implementing a general complementary evaluation metric that does not rely on phase stability. We will simulate the system dynamics using 1D-TDSE partial differential equations to validate the noise rejection capabilities under realistic conditions.

topics.title: Characterizing Systematic Effects in Bragg Atom Interferometers via Adaptive Numerical Methods
topics.rationale: Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to characterize, yet existing methods struggle to capture the full complexity of these effects. The paper's use of adaptive step size Runge-Kutta methods and split-step Crank-Nicolson methods suggests a need for more flexible and accurate numerical techniques to handle the partial differential equations governing the system. This gap represents a significant limitation in current characterization techniques, as they often lack the precision required for high-accuracy applications. Developing robust numerical methods is essential to overcome these systematic errors and improve the overall fidelity of the interferometer.
topics.approach: We will implement a hybrid numerical approach combining 1D-TDSE partial differential equations with adaptive step size Runge-Kutta methods to simulate Bragg diffraction effects. The simulation will utilize split-step Crank-Nicolson methods and lookup table methods to accurately model the system dynamics. We will then analyze the systematic effects to develop a correction algorithm that mitigates these errors in real-time.

topics.title: In-Orbit Test of the Weak Equivalence Principle with Atom Interferometry: Enhancing Metrological Constraints
topics.rationale: Prior atom-interferometric WEP tests in microgravity were limited by phase noise, resulting in a test uncertainty three orders of magnitude worse than the current in-orbit test, creating a significant bottleneck for new physics searches. The current state-of-the-art implementations rely on specific techniques like dual-species atom interferometers and fluorescence detection switching, which may not be universally applicable or robust enough for in-orbit operations. This limitation highlights a pressing need for advanced metrological constraints and error estimation methods that can push the test uncertainty closer to the current in-orbit benchmark. Addressing this gap is crucial for advancing our understanding of fundamental physics in space.
topics.approach: We will design and implement a dual-species atom interferometer with advanced platform motion suppression techniques to minimize phase noise. The error estimation will be refined using fluorescence detection switching and two-photon detuning switching strategies to ensure high precision. We will conduct a comprehensive in-orbit test campaign and evaluate the results against the current in-orbit test uncertainty to demonstrate the feasibility of pushing the metrological constraints further.

topics.title: Fundamental Limits of Large Momentum Transfer in Optical Lattices for Enhanced Atom Interferometer Sensitivity
topics.rationale: Existing implementations of atom interferometers rely on elastic scattering processes that are constrained by imperfect pulse efficiencies, leading to reduced losses and phase accuracy. The paper highlights that state-of-the-art implementations are limited by these constraints, which prevents achieving the orders of magnitude of reduced losses and improved phase accuracy required for next-generation sensing. This gap represents a fundamental barrier to improving the sensitivity of atom interferometers, as current methods do not fully exploit the potential of large momentum transfer techniques. Resolving this issue is essential for unlocking the full potential of optical lattices in high-precision applications.
topics.approach: We will develop a Floquet-based theoretical framework to analyze the impact of imperfect pulse efficiencies on elastic scattering processes. The approach will involve exact numerical solutions of the Schrödinger equation to quantify the loss mechanisms and phase accuracy. We will then benchmark these theoretical predictions against experimental results to validate the proposed methods and identify specific parameters for optimization.

topics.title: Broadband Detection of Weak Oscillatory Fields at the Fundamental Limit for Gravitational-Wave Sensing
topics.rationale: Broadband detection of a weak oscillatory field with unknown carrier frequency underlies magnetometry, axion searches, and gravitational-wave sensing, yet existing methods struggle to achieve the fundamental limits imposed by geometric constraints. The paper provides a geometric corollary and upper bound on the integrated quantum Fisher information, offering a metrological constraint that is not yet fully realized in practical implementations. This gap indicates a critical need for an all-analog multi-resonant protocol that can operate within these fundamental bounds without sacrificing sensitivity. Developing such a protocol is vital for advancing the capabilities of next-generation gravitational-wave detectors.
topics.approach: We will design an all-analog multi-resonant protocol based on a Randomized Su-Schrieffer-Heeger control Hamiltonian to achieve broadband detection. The method will utilize a Grover-like integration-time lower bound as a geometric corollary and an upper bound on integrated quantum Fisher information to optimize the detection efficiency. We will simulate the system dynamics using a m-register GHZ probe and evaluate the results against the theoretical constraints to demonstrate feasibility.

articles.abstract: Ultralight bosonic dark matter with masses in the meV range, corresponding to terahertz (THz) Compton frequencies, remains largely unexplored due to the difficulty of achieving both efficient signal conversion and single-photon-sensitive detection at THz frequencies. We show that this topology is sensitive to the microphysics of dark matter. Self-interacting dark matter (SIDM), with cross-sections $σ/m\sim 1$--$10\;\mathrm{cm^2/g}$ motivated by small-scale structure anomalies, reduces halo gas binding energies and increases the duty cycle of ionizing-photon escape. articles.title: Hunting Dark Matter with the Einstein Telescope
articles.abstract: Too light primordial black holes evaporate and are therefore strongly constrained by various bounds, e.g. Cosmic Microwave Background distortion. However, if they are formed strongly clustered, the corresponding haloes may collapse in heavier black holes which may form the entirety of the dark matter of the universe. The indirect signal of such scenario is the production of a flat stochastic background of gravitational waves which is detectable by the Einstein Telescope.

A long-standing blind spot for experimental searches is the sub-100 MeV mass range, where the particle lifetime is too long to be constrained by prompt-decay collider searches yet too short to be reached by beam-dump experiments. These oscillations are characterized by extremely weak couplings or high suppression scales, with the Planck scale - the characteristic scale of quantum gravity - serving as a natural benchmark. articles.abstract: Hypothetical light scalar particles trigger the superradiant instability around spinning black holes (BHs), causing clouds of scalars to grow around the BH. In the presence of sufficiently strong particle self-interactions (characterized by the decay constant $f$), scalars are ejected from BH orbits, resulting in coherent, non-relativistic emissions that continuously carry away the BH's angular momentum. articles.abstract: In this work, we investigate the optical, dynamical, and radiative properties of an Euler--Heisenberg black hole immersed in a perfect fluid dark matter (PFDM) background. We analyze the photon sphere and shadow, the scalar quasinormal-mode spectrum in the eikonal regime, the grey-body factor through the eikonal QNM correspondence, the sparsity of Hawking radiation, and the corresponding energy emission rate. Previous studies have focused on the impact of scalar radiation channels, revealing a rich structure of resonances, sharp features and floating orbits. facts.problem: The motion of massive test particles and gravitational-wave emission around a magnetically charged black hole in a Hernquist dark matter halo.
facts.gap: Existing work likely lacks a detailed analysis of the combined effects of dark matter halos and magnetic monopole charges on the stability of periodic trajectories and the resulting gravitational-wave polarizations in extreme mass-ratio regimes.

Investigation of innermost stable circular orbit

articles_with_facts.title: Black hole scalar sirens in the Milky Way
articles_with_facts.abstract: Hypothetical light scalar particles trigger the superradiant instability around spinning black holes (BHs), causing clouds of scalars to grow around the BH. In the presence of sufficiently strong particle self-interactions (characterized by the decay constant $f$), scalars are ejected from BH orbits, resulting in coherent, non-relativistic emissions that continuously carry away the BH's angular momentum. Parameters exist for which cloud growth is much faster, and scalar depletion is much slower,

facts.problem: The main problem is the detection of astrophysical sources of scalar particles, specifically isolated black holes that emit scalars continuously, which are otherwise invisible and difficult to observe directly.
facts.gap: Existing methods for detecting scalars rely on early-universe production or cosmological initial conditions, which are difficult to observe directly. There is a lack of a probe for isolated black hole populations that can be detected via their scalar emissions.
methods: Population modeling of isolated stellar-mass BHs
Framework extension to supermassive, intermediate-mass, and light BHs

articles_with_facts.title: Shadow, Quasinormal Modes, Sparsity, and Energy Emission Rate of Euler-Heisenberg Black Hole Surrounded by Perfect Fluid Dark Matter
articles_with_facts.abstract: In this work, we investigate the optical, dynamical, and radiative properties of an Euler--Heisenberg black hole immersed in a perfect fluid dark matter (PFDM) background. We analyze the photon sphere and shadow, the scalar quasinormal-mode spectrum in the eikonal regime, the grey-body factor through the eikonal QNM correspondence, the sparsity of Hawking radiation, and the corresponding energy emission rate. Our results show that both the black-hole charge and the PFDM parameter significantly a

facts.problem: The optical, dynamical, and radiative properties of an Euler--Heisenberg black hole immersed in a perfect fluid dark matter (PFDM) background.
facts.gap: The paper investigates how the Euler--Heisenberg correction and dark matter environment affect black hole observables like photon sphere, shadow, quasinormal modes, and Hawking radiation, specifically addressing the subleading nature of the correction and the dominant imprint of dark matter.

articles_with_facts.title: Relativistic signatures of scalar dark matter in extreme-mass-ratio inspirals
articles_with_facts.abstract: We study gravitational wave emission by circular extreme-mass-ratio systems in a spherically symmetric scalar environment. Previous studies have focused on the impact of scalar radiation channels, revealing a rich structure of resonances, sharp features and floating orbits. Through the backreaction of the cloud on the metric, corrections to the gravitational sector come in at the same order. We develop the computational methods, and provide a characterization of this new, fully relativistic clou

facts.problem: Gravitational wave emission by circular extreme-mass-ratio systems in a spherically symmetric scalar environment, specifically investigating the impact of scalar radiation channels and relativistic cloud corrections.
facts.gap: Previous studies focused on scalar radiation channels, revealing resonances and floating orbits, but this paper addresses the backreaction of the cloud on the metric, characterizing a new fully relativistic cloud signature and identifying regimes where polar sector corrections dominate.
methods: backreaction of the cloud on the metric

articles_with_facts.abstract: This is a review of key aspects of a model presented at the Lake Como School: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and the Cosmological Tensions, June, 2025. The associated publication can be found at: A Schlatter and R E Kastner 2023 J. Phys. Commun. 7 065009

articles.abstract: We present a standalone frequency-offset locking system for controlling narrow-linewidth lasers using off-the-shelf electronic components. We lock two frequency-doubled 1560 nm lasers to a stable primary laser operating at 780 nm via their optical beat note. This radio-frequency beat note is fed through a broadband variable divider, a frequency-to-voltage converter, and a proportional-integrator controller to lock each follower laser to a tunable offset frequency relative to the primary. articles.title: Rapid axial loading of a grating MOT with a cold-atom beam
articles.abstract: Laser-cooled atoms are increasingly being used to realise practical quantum devices, motivating the development of compact and robust atom sources. Grating magneto-optical traps (gMOTs) simplify the cold-atom source architecture but are typically vapour-loaded and provide limited atomic flux. Here we explore the loading of gMOTs from cold-atom beams. articles.title: Sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of cesium with static magnetic fields
articles.abstract: Laser cooling of alkali atoms typically requires time-varying magnetic fields, introducing unwanted coupling between atom preparation and coherent operations. Here we demonstrate sub-Doppler laser cooling and optical transport of alkali atoms in a fully static magnetic-field configuration. articles.abstract: We report on magneto-optical trapping of the two fermionic isotopes of atomic titanium, $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti. Unlike the even mass-number isotopes, which were recently laser cooled, $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti have nonzero nuclear spins and, consequently, their atomic levels are split by hyperfine structure. articles.abstract: Most cold atoms experiments in microgravity platforms or in Space are achieved using atom chips, leading to limitations in terms of optical access and inhomogeneous magnetic fields. Optical dipole traps do not have these drawbacks but have difficulties producing atomic samples with a large number of atoms at ultra low temperature in the absence of gravity. articles.title: Purely optical macroscopic trap for alkaline-earth and similar atoms
articles.abstract: We consider a laser cooling and trapping of alkaline-earth and similar atoms in a bichromatic field resonant to a closed optical transition $^1S_0 \to \, ^1P_1$ or $^1S_0 \to \, ^3P_1$. It is shown that new kinetic effects emerge compared to monochromatic fields, enabling the formation of a deep macroscopic trap capable of capturing and cooling neutral atoms to sub-Doppler temperatures. articles.abstract: We investigate high frequency motional states of trapped atomic ions. Trapped ions in rf traps are confined by an approximate harmonic potential and exhibit quantum motional states that mediate essential techniques in quantum computing, simulation, networking, and precision measurement. articles.abstract: We demonstrate precision laser spectroscopy of a trapped $^{173}$Yb$^+$ ion that is not directly laser cooled by coupling it to ultracold atoms. The atomic bath continuously cools the internal degrees of freedom of the ion to its hyperfine ground state via spin-exchange collisions. Successful laser excitation is detected via state-selective charge transfer and subsequent ion loss. Here we present a series of experiments with continuous molecular beam sources of AlF, utilising this reaction. We demonstrate a compact AlF molecular beam oven whose total far-field brightness is $5\times 10^{15}$ molecules per steradian per second at 923~K, just below the melting

facts.gap: Existing sources are either not continuous or have lower brightness and higher rotational temperatures, limiting their utility for specific experimental setups.
methods: thermochemical reaction between sublimated aluminum trifluoride and aluminum metal

topics.title: Optical dipole trap performance and cooling efficiency for fermionic Titanium isotopes using a new bichromatic field configuration
topics.rationale: The paper on hyperfine spectroscopy of titanium isotopes highlights the challenge of achieving magneto-optical trapping for isotopes with nonzero nuclear spins due to hyperfine structure splitting. Existing laser cooling techniques for even mass-number isotopes are not applicable, necessitating a novel approach to overcome hyperfine structure barriers. This topic directly addresses the gap in realizing portable, high-flux cold-atom systems for quantum devices by optimizing the bichromatic field configuration to achieve sub-Doppler temperatures without magnetic fields.
topics.approach: We propose a theoretical and experimental framework to design a bichromatic optical field configuration specifically resonant with the closed optical transition in $^{47}$Ti and $^{49}$Ti. The approach involves simulating the potential landscape to identify optimal laser frequencies and intensities that suppress hyperfine splitting, followed by an experimental test using a programmable agile resonator for frequency stability. We will measure the sub-Doppler temperatures and compare cooling rates against the theoretical predictions derived from the hyperfine structure analysis.

topics.title: Sub-Doppler laser cooling of Rubidium vapor in hot environments using a programmable agile PZT-on-SiN resonator for arbitrary frequency tuning
topics.rationale: Hot, optically driven rubidium vapor presents a significant challenge for high-resolution spectroscopy and nonlinear optics due to Doppler broadening, which is limited by room-temperature conditions. Existing systems rely on bulky, power-hungry modulators that lack the compactness and portability required for quantum experiments and atomic sensing. This research fills the gap by utilizing a semiconductor laser and a PZT-on-SiN resonator to achieve sub-Doppler temperatures and arbitrary frequency tuning sequences in a portable platform.
topics.approach: We will develop a compact experimental setup integrating a semiconductor laser with a lead zirconate titanate (PZT)-actuated resonator cavity to enable fast, programmable, and arbitrary frequency tuning. The rubidium vapor will be cooled to sub-Doppler temperatures using the resonator's precise control over laser frequency. We will measure the cooling rates, optical depth, and full width at half maximum (FWHM) to demonstrate the scalability and efficiency of this platform for quantum error correction protocols and atomic clocks.

topics.title: High-optical-depth, sub-Doppler-width absorption lines in hot, optically driven rubidium vapor: A study of dressing the intermediate state with a strong control field
topics.rationale: Achieving high-resolution spectroscopy and nonlinear optics in room-temperature atomic vapors is currently limited by Doppler broadening, which prevents the use of conventional laser-cooled atoms in such environments. This study addresses the gap by demonstrating the feasibility of high optical density with sub-Doppler-width absorption lines in hot rubidium vapor through the dressing of an intermediate state with a strong control field.
topics.approach: We will implement a three-level ladder system where the intermediate state is dressed by a strong control field resonant with the D2 line, while a weak counter-propagating probe field is used to achieve high optical depth. By optimizing the control field strength and counter-propagating probe intensity, we aim to reduce the Doppler width below the natural linewidth. The results will be validated against existing benchmarks to confirm the scalability of this method for applications such as atomic clocks and quantum computing.

topics.title: Two-dimensional shelving spectroscopy of ultraviolet ground state transitions in dysprosium: A new approach to dipolar atom experiments
topics.rationale: The open inner-shell electronic structure of lanthanides like dysprosium offers a rich spectrum of ultraviolet (UV) transitions below 400 nm, which are rarely utilized in dipolar atom experiments despite their availability for laser cooling, trapping, and coherent control. This research addresses the gap by introducing two-dimensional shelving spectroscopy to fully exploit these transitions, enabling more efficient and portable quantum devices.
topics.approach: We will apply two-dimensional shelving spectroscopy to the UV ground state transitions in dysprosium, measuring isotope shifts, hyperfine coefficients, and total angular momentum J values. By analyzing the King plots, we will map out the shelving efficiency and identify optimal wavelengths for laser cooling and trapping. This approach will pave the way for the development of more compact and portable dipolar atom systems for quantum information processing.

topics.title: Optical repumping and atom number balancing in a two-color MOT for $^{88}$Sr: A study of repumping efficiency and recycling schemes
topics.rationale: Efficient recycling of atoms in a magneto-optical trap (MOT) is critical for continuous atomic beam generation and quantum experiments. Traditional repumping schemes for $^{88}$Sr have low efficiency, which limits the throughput and stability of the trap. This study addresses the gap by investigating novel repumping transitions and two-color MOT configurations to significantly improve recycling efficiency.
topics.approach: We will study a novel repumping transition for $^{88}$Sr atoms trapped in a 'blue' magneto-optical trap, optimizing the two-color configuration (green and blue MOTs) to enhance recycling efficiency. By comparing experimental control parameters with theoretical predictions, we will determine the optimal repumping transition and laser frequencies to achieve high efficiency while maintaining low temperatures. The results will be validated against existing benchmarks to assess the scalability of this approach for continuous atomic beam generation.

topics.title: Continuous thermochemical sources of AlF molecules for laser cooling and molecular magneto-optical traps
topics.rationale: Producing continuous molecular beam sources for AlF with high brightness and low rotational temperature is essential for applications like laser cooling and molecular magneto-optical traps. Existing sources are often not continuous or have lower brightness and higher rotational temperatures, limiting their utility. This research addresses the gap by developing a continuous thermochemical reaction source for AlF.
topics.approach: We will design and implement a thermochemical reaction between sublimated aluminum trifluoride and aluminum metal, utilizing a molecular beam oven and cryogenic Ne buffer gas cell to achieve continuous molecular beam sources. By optimizing the reaction conditions and using pulsed laser ionisation spectroscopy, we will measure the total far-field brightness and signal-to-noise ratio. The results will demonstrate the scalability of this approach for portable quantum experiments requiring high-brightness molecular beams.

results.query: I want to explore experimental and theoretical aspects of quantum sensing and metrology, including atom interferometry, optical clocks, and their applications to tests of fundamental physics and gravitational wave detection

articles.title: Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Matter-Wave Interference
articles.abstract: Operating matter-wave interferometers as quantum detectors for fundamental physics or inertial sensors in real-world applications with unprecedented accuracies relies on noise rejection, often implemented by correlating two sensors. Such sensors can be spatially separated (gradiometry or gravitational-wave detection) or consist of different internal states (magnetometry or quantum clock interferometry), in which case a signal-amplitude modulation may serve as a signature of a differential phase.

articles.title: A fast and accurate method for simulating Bragg atom interferometers
articles.abstract: Atom interferometers are used in a variety of applications, from measuring gravity and gravity gradients in the field to performing tests of fundamental physics in the lab. One method of increasing interferometer sensitivity is to produce a larger momentum difference between interferometer arms through the use of large momentum transfer methods, such as Bragg diffraction. However, Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to char

articles.title: In-orbit Test of the Weak Equivalence Principle with Atom Interferometry
articles.abstract: The Weak Equivalence Principle (WEP) is a central pillar of general relativity. Its precise test with quantum systems in space offers a unique window onto new physics. Here we report the first in-orbit quantum test of the WEP. A dual-species (85Rb/87Rb) atom interferometer is realized aboard the China Space Station. Methods of platform motion suppression, fluorescence detection switching, and two-photon detuning switching are developed to eliminate phase noise and improve measurement accuracy. A

articles.title: Fundamental Limits of Large Momentum Transfer in Optical Lattices
articles.abstract: Large-momentum-transfer techniques are instrumental for the next generation of atom interferometers as they significantly improve their sensitivity. State-of-the-art implementations rely on elastic scattering processes from optical lattices such as Bloch oscillations or sequential Bragg diffraction, but their performance is constrained by imperfect pulse efficiencies. articles.title: Simple broadband signal detection at the fundamental limit
articles.abstract: Broadband detection of a weak oscillatory field with unknown carrier frequency underlies magnetometry, axion searches and gravitational-wave sensing. We show that the Grover-like integration-time lower bound for this task is a geometric corollary an upper bound on the integrated quantum Fisher information, a metrological constraint. articles.title: Nuclear Heterodyne Interferometry for Gravitational Spectroscopy
articles.abstract: Gravitational spectroscopy tests the coupling of gravity to matter by measuring gravitationally induced frequency shifts of quantum transitions. While modern optical clocks probe the gravitational response of electronic transitions with extraordinary precision, tests in the nuclear sector have not progressed since the Mössbauer measurements of the gravitational redshift by Pound and Rebka. Here we introduce a new approach to nuclear gravitational spectroscopy based on phase-sensitive heterodyne

articles.title: Atom-light hybrid interferometer for atomic sensing with quantum memory
articles.abstract: Quantum memories feature a reversible conversion of optical fields into long-lived atomic spin waves, and are therefore ideal for operating as sensitive atomic sensors. However, up to now, atom-light interferometers have lacked an efficient approach to exploit their ultimate atomic sensing performance, since an extra optical delay line is required to compensate for the memory time. Here, we report a new protocol that records the photocurrent via heterodyne mixing with a stable local oscillator.

articles.title: Environmental Measurements in the Sedrun Access Shaft to the Gotthard Base Tunnel -- a Promising Site for a Long-Baseline Atom Interferometer
articles.abstract: Atom interferometer (AI) experiments offer interesting prospects for searches for the interactions of ultralight bosonic dark matter with Standard Model particles as well as detection of gravitational waves in a frequency band inaccessible to experiments that are operating or under construction. Ideal locations for the next generation of such experiments are provided by long vertical shafts, such as that providing access to the Gotthard base railway tunnel from the Sedrun locality in the Canton

articles.title: Mirror Surface Evaluation for the Einstein Telescope Using Virtual Mirror Maps
articles.abstract: The performance of mirrors in optical interferometers is critically influenced by their surface quality. Accurate metrology enables mirror surfaces to be characterized through phase maps describing their three-dimensional structure after coating. In this work, we combine Zernike polynomial decomposition and spatial frequency (PSD) analysis with numerical optical simulations to quantify the impact of surface distortions on the reflected optical field. The method is validated using metrology data

articles.abstract: We consider a laser cooling and trapping of alkaline-earth and similar atoms in a bichromatic field resonant to a closed optical transition $^1S_0 \to \, ^1P_1$ or $^1S_0 \to \, ^3P_1$. articles.title: Clock Noise Cancellation in Heterodyne Links between Optical Cavities for Space-Borne Gravitational-Wave Telescopes
articles.abstract: Space-borne gravitational-wave telescopes are key to extend the observation band below $10\,\mathrm{Hz}$. articles_with_facts.title: Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Matter-Wave Interference
articles_with_facts.abstract: Operating matter-wave interferometers as quantum detectors for fundamental physics or inertial sensors in real-world applications with unprecedented accuracies relies on noise rejection, often implemented by correlating two sensors. Such sensors can be spatially separated (gradiometry or gravitational-wave detection) or consist of different internal states (magnetometry or quantum clock interferometry), in which case a signal-amplitude modulation may serve as a signature of a differential phase.

facts.problem: Achieving high-precision quantum detectors for fundamental physics and inertial sensors by improving noise rejection and signal accuracy.
facts.gap: Prior methods often assume perfect correlation or operate at vanishing amplitude, leading to reduced trueness or suboptimal precision; existing approaches lack a complementary evaluation method for correlated interferometers without phase stability.

facts.problem: The conventional understanding of interference attributes it to path-accumulated phase differences, treating measurement as a passive readout. This paper addresses the fact that single-particle interference is governed by a joint quantity (relative phase between the prepared state and the measurement basis) that is operationally inaccessible in conventional interferometers.
facts.gap: Existing interferometers cannot access the joint quantity of the relative phase between the prepared quantum state and the detector-defined measurement basis, which is the true driver of single-particle interference.
methods: coherently seeded entangled nonlinear biphoton sources
continuous control of fringe visibility through idler-state overlap

facts.problem: Systematic errors in short-baseline atomic interferometer-gravimeters caused by lineshape-asymmetry-caused shift (LACS) in gravitational acceleration measurements.
facts.gap: The existence and magnitude of LACS-induced shifts in short-baseline atomic gravimeters were not previously experimentally observed or investigated, leading to potential systematic errors in high-precision measurements of g.

These transverse fields unavoidably accompany any static magnetic or electric field gradients, and have been shown by Comparat [Phys. Rev. A 101, 023606 (2020)] to limit the precision application of SGIs. As a concrete example, we consider SGIs with ultracold Rb Rydberg atoms accelerated by

facts.problem: The precision application of Stern-Gerlach Interferometers (SGIs) is limited by transverse fields accompanying static magnetic or electric field gradients, which cause significant sensitivity differences between similar implementations.
facts.gap: Existing implementations of SGIs are constrained by unavoidable transverse fields that limit the precision of field gradient applications and reduce fringe visibility.

topics.title: Optimizing Phase Estimation from Amplitude Collapse in Correlated Matter-Wave Interferometers for Inertial Sensors
topics.rationale: Recent advances in PEAC have shown promise for high-precision detectors, yet existing methods assume perfect correlation or operate at vanishing amplitudes, leading to reduced trueness. There is a critical gap in complementary evaluation methods for correlated interferometers without phase stability, hindering practical deployment. This topic addresses the need for robust signal accuracy and noise rejection in real-world inertial sensing applications.
topics.approach: This research will propose a novel adaptive fitting strategy to target specific magnetically sensitive substates of the atom interferometer. We will implement a complementary evaluation method using a synthetic benchmark dataset generated via the simulation of correlated matter-wave interference scenarios to quantify noise rejection capabilities.

topics.title: Characterizing Systematic Effects in Bragg Diffraction Accumulated Phase for Fast Atom Interferometer Simulation
topics.rationale: Bragg diffraction introduces systematic effects in the accumulated interferometer phase that are challenging to characterize, limiting the accuracy of current simulation models. Existing approaches using lookup tables and standard Runge-Kutta methods lack the flexibility to handle complex coupled differential equations arising from Bragg diffraction. This work aims to bridge the gap between theoretical partial differential equations and practical simulation accuracy.
topics.approach: We will develop a hybrid simulation framework combining 1D-TDSE partial differential equations with adaptive step size Runge-Kutta methods to accurately model Bragg diffraction effects. The approach will utilize a lookup table method to efficiently solve the system of ordinary differential equations derived from the separation of the systems.

topics.title: Testing the Weak Equivalence Principle in Space Using Dual-Species Atom Interferometry with Motion Suppression
topics.rationale: While atom-interferometric WEP tests in microgravity have been limited by higher test uncertainties, there is a distinct gap in utilizing dual-species interferometers for precise space-based testing. Existing platforms lack effective motion suppression mechanisms that can isolate the quantum signal from environmental noise, reducing test uncertainty significantly.
topics.approach: This study will design and implement a dual-species atom interferometer equipped with advanced motion suppression techniques, including fluorescence detection switching and two-photon detuning switching. We will employ a rigorous error estimation protocol to evaluate the test uncertainty and result precision under simulated microgravity conditions.

topics.title: Improving Sensitivity and Phase Accuracy in Atom Interferometers via Large Momentum Transfer Pulse Efficiencies
topics.rationale: State-of-the-art atom interferometer implementations are constrained by imperfect pulse efficiencies in elastic scattering processes, which limits the achievable sensitivity and phase accuracy. There is a significant gap in optimizing these pulse efficiencies to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio in practical applications.
topics.approach: We will develop a comprehensive optimization framework to enhance pulse efficiencies in elastic scattering processes. This will be validated using a synthetic benchmark dataset generated from the simulation of various atom-interferometer pulse sequences to quantify improvements in sensitivity and phase accuracy.

topics.title: Quantifying Systematic Errors in Atom Interferometer-Gravimeter Integration for High-Precision Accelerometry
topics.rationale: Atom-interferometer-gravimeters face systematic errors from lineshape-asymmetry-caused shifts (LACS) and other instrumental artifacts, which are critical for high-precision applications. Existing error models often lack sufficient detail to accurately predict these shifts, limiting the reliability of gravimeter measurements.
topics.approach: This work will develop a detailed error model for atom-interferometer-gravimeter systems, accounting for LACS and other instrumental artifacts. We will employ a synthetic benchmark dataset generated from the simulation of various experimental scenarios to evaluate the accuracy of the error model and identify potential systematic errors.
